To the right, the background transitions into a driving highway scene that fades into the band’s right edge. Overlaid on the left, aligned with the product image, the product name appears in large, uppercase typography, with a concise tagline just below. The rating-summary is tucked just under the title, aligned left, while the fitment and availability badges hover toward the top-right corner of the band, partially over the driving scene. The band has 16–24px internal padding and a subtle bottom border or bevel that clearly separates it from the two-column region below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page; acts as the principal hero section anchoring the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb-trail sits at the very top of the content container, directly below the main site navigation band and above the product-header. It is left-aligned within the 960–1024px content width and spans roughly 60–70% of that width, forming a thin horizontal strip of small, light text on a dark gradient background. There is about 8–12px of padding above and below the breadcrumb text, and it sits 8–16px above the back-link bar, creating a compact but distinct navigation cluster without large blank spaces.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with content; always appears above the hero band as part of the top navigation cluster.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-link-bar is positioned immediately below the breadcrumb-trail and directly above the product-header band, spanning the entire width of the central content container. It appears as a shallow, glossy strip (≈32–40px high) with a horizontal gradient, slightly beveled top and bottom edges, and a subtle inner glow. A left-pointing arrow icon and "Back to results" label are aligned to the left with 16px horizontal padding. The right side remains mostly empty or may contain a faint separator line, preserving the mockup’s strong left anchor while keeping the bar visually light compared to the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page; acts as a contextual navigation band immediately preceding the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The vehicle-fitment-indicator appears as a prominent badge within the upper-right quadrant of the product-header band. It is sized like a pill or shield (≈160–200px wide), with a bright green or yellow gradient, subtle glow, and bold label (e.g., "Fits your 2016 Civic" or "Check fitment"). It slightly overlaps the right edge of the product image’s visual zone and the left edge of the driving background, emphasizing its connection to both product and driving context. Internally, it has 8–12px padding and a small icon on the left. It sits just above or horizontally aligned with the product name baseline, anchoring the upper-right hero space without obstructing the product photo.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned within the hero band, scrolls with it; floats visually over the hero background but is not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-specs-section occupies a glossy card in the left main column, below the product-description-section (so that users get narrative then details). Within the left 70% column, it spans nearly the full width with 16px internal padding and a subtly beveled border. The card is separated from the description above by about 24px of vertical space. Inside, specs are presented in a dense table-like grid with alternating row backgrounds and thin dividing lines, mimicking a technical panel. The section heading aligns left at the top, while the table uses the full card width, giving a compact but readable, information-dense block that fills most of the left column’s visible area when scrolled into view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, stacked below the description; scrolls with the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-description-section is the first major content block in the left main column, positioned directly below the hero band and aligned to the same left edge as the product-header content. It appears as a medium-height card with a dark-to-mid gradient background, soft inner shadow, and 16–20px padding. A bold heading sits at the top, followed by 1–3 short paragraphs and possibly a small feature list, keeping text concise. This card spans nearly the entire width of the left column and is separated from the hero by about 24–32px of vertical space, forming a clear step down from the hero’s high-impact visuals to more explanatory content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the left column; forms the top card in the left-content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rating-summary is embedded within the product-header band, directly under the product title and left-aligned with it. It occupies a thin horizontal strip (≈1/4 of the hero band’s height), containing star icons, an average rating value, and a review count. The stars are glossy and high-contrast, with a slight glow against the darker hero gradient. The rating sits just above the hero band’s lower edge, helping lead the eye down toward the main content area. Optionally, a compact repetition of the rating (e.g., stars only) may appear in the top-right corner of the purchase card to reinforce social proof near the CTA, but the primary, text-rich instance lives in the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned within the hero band under the title; scrolls with the hero content and does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The protection-plan-selector lives inside the right sidebar’s purchase card, roughly in the upper-middle part of that card. The purchase card occupies about 30% of the total content width and sits directly to the right of the left column, starting just beneath the hero band. Within this card, the protection plan area spans the full inner width, with a small heading and one or more selectable options styled as glossy toggles or pill buttons with bright hover states. It is separated from the price/availability region above by a thin gradient divider and from the quantity/add-to-cart controls below by 16–24px of vertical space, maintaining dense but readable stacking in the sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the purchase sidebar card; vertically stacked between price/availability (above) and quantity/add-to-cart (below).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity-selector is placed inside the same right sidebar purchase card, directly below the protection-plan-selector. It occupies a narrow horizontal strip spanning about half to two-thirds of the card’s width, aligned to the left. The control itself is compact, with a small numeric field and plus/minus buttons styled as beveled squares with a glossy finish. There is about 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the control, and it sits immediately to the left of or slightly above the main add-to-cart button, visually tying quantity selection to the primary CTA without stealing attention from it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the purchase card; horizontally aligned with and just above the add-to-cart form controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-form forms the core of the right sidebar purchase card, positioned beneath the quantity-selector and spanning almost the full inner width of the card. The main add-to-cart button is large, high-contrast, and glossy, occupying about 70–80% of the card’s width and sitting near the bottom third of the sidebar. It uses a saturated green or yellow gradient with strong beveling and a soft outer glow, making it the primary focal point in the sidebar. The form may also include a compact price display and any required hidden inputs, but visually the big CTA dominates. There is 16–20px of padding around the button, and the card’s bottom edge sits about 24–32px above the related-products bands, ensuring the purchase panel feels like a contained, energy-filled block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the purchase sidebar; scrolls normally and remains aligned with the right column, without fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-button appears as a secondary action within the right sidebar purchase card, positioned directly below or to the right of the add-to-cart button depending on available width. In narrower layouts, it sits below with about 8–12px vertical spacing; in wider desktop layouts, it can sit as a smaller, outlined button aligned to the right of the main CTA. It uses a less saturated gradient or a dark outline with subtle glow, indicating lower priority while still fitting the glossy aesthetic. Its size is about half to two-thirds the width of the add-to-cart button, reinforcing its secondary nature.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, attached to the lower region of the purchase card; scrolls with the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-products-section is a full-width band that appears below the two-column main content area (both left content cards and right purchase card). It spans the entire central content width and uses a slightly lighter gradient than the hero, with 24–32px top padding to create a distinct step down from the main product information. Inside, a left-aligned heading sits at the top, below which a horizontal grid of product tiles (3–4 across) is laid out. Each tile is a small glossy card with a product image, title, and mini CTA button, closely spaced with 16px gutters to maintain high density. This band occupies roughly one screen height or slightly less, depending on the number of items, and ends with a faint bottom border before the bundles section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width content band stacked below the primary two-column region; scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-bundles-section sits directly beneath the related-products-section as another full-width band with a slightly different gradient tint (e.g., more yellow/amber) to distinguish its content type. It mirrors the structure of the related-products band: a bold heading at the top left, then 2–3 wider tiles per row, each representing a bundle with combined product imagery and pricing. Tiles are slightly larger and more promotional, with stronger glow effects and more prominent "View bundle" or "Add bundle" buttons. Vertical spacing between the related-products and related-bundles bands is tight (≈24px), creating a stacked promotional zone that extends the page downward in clear, stepped segments.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width band positioned directly below the related-products-section; scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-notice appears in two closely related positions to keep stock status visible near key decisions. Its primary placement is inside the product-header band, near the top-right area just below or beside the vehicle-fitment-indicator, styled as a compact pill or label with a green (in stock) or amber (limited) gradient and clear text like "In Stock" or an ETA. A secondary, smaller text line or icon-state version is repeated at the top of the right sidebar purchase card, directly above the price and protection-plan-selector, left-aligned. Both instances use bright color and subtle glow but are smaller than CTAs, ensuring they inform rather than dominate. The hero instance sits a few pixels below the fitment badge, aligning their right edges to form a neat badge cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Both instances are static within their respective parent containers (hero band and purchase card); they scroll with those sections and do not have sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The operation-feedback-banner is a horizontally spanning notification strip that appears just below the main site navigation and above the breadcrumb-trail when active. It uses a bright, saturated gradient (e.g., yellow or red depending on status) with a pronounced glow and subtle drop shadow, visually floating over the darker page background. It occupies a shallow height (≈40–56px) and spans the same centered content width as the rest of the page. Text is centered or slightly left-biased, and a close/dismiss icon sits at the far right edge. When present, it pushes the breadcrumb and the rest of the content downward, maintaining the overall banded structure without overlapping other modules.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow but appears conditionally; when visible, it occupies its own band beneath the header and scrolls away with the page like other sections.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Displays products matching a search query with filters, sorting, vehicle fitment options, comparison selection, and navigation to product detail and comparison pages.

core_features: Execute a product search using the query string and current filters, sorting, fitment scope, and pagination via the searchProducts interface.
Display a summary of the current search query, total number of matching products, and the active sort order.
Show and manage a filter sidebar populated dynamically via getSearchFilterOptions scoped to the current query, including price, rating, feature, RMS power, and channel count filters.
Allow users to refine results by limiting to products that fit the currently selected vehicle context retrieved via getSelectedVehicle and applied through the fitmentScope parameter.
Provide sorting controls for relevance, price, and review-based metrics, mapped to the sortBy URL parameter and passed into searchProducts.
Render a paginated product grid with product information and navigation links to individual product detail pages (product.html) including the ref=search parameter.
Support selection of products for comparison via checkboxes in the product grid and manage comparison sessions through createCompareSession, then navigate to compare.html with compareSessionId.
Maintain full deep-linking by reflecting all filters, sorting, fitment scope, and pagination in URL parameters so the same state can be restored via direct URL access or browser navigation.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Landing on Search Results
1. User submits the global search form on any page.
2. Browser navigates to search_results.html with the q URL parameter set to the user-entered query.
3. On page load, the page reads all URL parameters (q, priceMin, priceMax, ratingMin, feature flags, rmsPowerMin, channelCount, fitmentScope, sortBy, page).
4. The page calls getSelectedVehicle (no parameters) to retrieve any existing vehicle context.
5. The page calls getSearchFilterOptions using the q parameter to obtain available filter ranges and options.
6. The page constructs a filters object from URL parameters and calls searchProducts with query=q, filters, fitmentScope, sortBy, page, and a configured pageSize.
7. The returned items and pagination data are rendered in the product grid, filter sidebar, and pagination controls.

Workflow: Refining Results with Filters
1. User adjusts a filter in the filter sidebar (for example, changes the price range or toggles a feature flag).
2. The filter-sidebar component updates the corresponding URL parameters (e.g., priceMin, priceMax, featureBluetooth), removes parameters for filters that are cleared, and resets page to the first page.
3. The browser history is updated with the new URL query string so the state is shareable and navigable.
4. Using the updated URL parameters, the page rebuilds the filters object and calls searchProducts again with the same q and fitmentScope but new filters and page=1.
5. The product grid and pagination controls are re-rendered based on the new searchProducts result.

Workflow: Sorting Results
1. User changes the sort option (for example, selects a different sort mode from a dropdown in the sort-and-page-size-bar component).
2. The component updates the sortBy URL parameter and resets page to the first page.
3. The page reconstructs the filters from URL parameters and calls searchProducts with the new sortBy value while preserving q, filters, and fitmentScope.
4. The product grid re-renders showing results in the new order, and the header summary reflects the active sort mode.

Workflow: Applying Vehicle Fitment Scope
1. If getSelectedVehicle indicates there is a selected vehicle, the vehicle-fitment-context component displays controls to limit results to that vehicle.
2. User toggles the fitment limitation control.
3. If the user enables limitation, the component sets fitmentScope in the URL (for example, to a value representing the selected-vehicle scope) and resets page to the first page; if disabled, it removes or clears fitmentScope from the URL.
4. The page re-runs searchProducts with the updated fitmentScope while keeping q, filters, and sortBy the same.
5. The product grid updates, and the fitment context display indicates whether results are currently restricted by vehicle.

Workflow: Navigating to a Product Detail Page
1. User clicks on a product’s image or name in the product grid.
2. The product-grid component reads the product’s id and triggers navigation to product.html with URL parameters id set to the product id and ref set to search.
3. The product detail page can use the ref parameter to adjust its behavior or display breadcrumbs back to search results.

Workflow: Selecting Products for Comparison
1. In the product grid, each product row includes a comparison checkbox.
2. User clicks one or more comparison checkboxes; each click toggles inclusion of that productId in a local comparison selection state maintained by the comparison-selection-toolbar component.
3. As selections are made, the comparison-selection-toolbar updates its count of selected products and enables or disables the Compare button based on internal rules (for example, ensuring at least two products are selected).
4. When the user clicks the Compare button, the component calls createCompareSession with the current list of selected productIds.
5. Upon a successful response, the component reads compareSession.id from the returned compareSession object and navigates to compare.html with compareSessionId set to that id.
6. Comparison selection state on the search results page remains local to the current page instance and is not persisted via URL parameters or storage.

Workflow: Pagination Through Results
1. User interacts with pagination controls (for example, clicks a next-page link or a specific page number).
2. The pagination-controls component updates the page URL parameter to the chosen page number while leaving q, filters, sortBy, and fitmentScope unchanged.
3. The page reconstructs the filters object from URL parameters and calls searchProducts with the new page value.
4. The product grid re-renders with the items for the requested page, and pagination controls update to reflect the current page.
5. Using browser back/forward controls correctly navigates between paginated states because each page change updates the URL.
interactions: On initial load or when the URL changes, the page reads all relevant URL parameters, calls getSelectedVehicle, getSearchFilterOptions(query=q), and searchProducts(query, filters, fitmentScope, sortBy, page, pageSize), then binds the results to the filter sidebar, product grid, pagination, and header.

Submitting a search within any search input on this page triggers a GET navigation to search_results.html with q set to the new query and all other search-related URL parameters cleared, allowing a fresh search state.

Changing any filter control in the filter-sidebar component updates the corresponding URL parameter(s), resets page to the first page, and triggers a new searchProducts call using the updated filters.

Toggling feature filters (such as Apple CarPlay, Bluetooth, backup camera input, free shipping, or includes wiring kit) sets or removes corresponding boolean URL parameters and re-runs searchProducts.
Adjusting numeric filters like priceMin, priceMax, ratingMin, rmsPowerMin, or channelCount modifies those numeric URL parameters and initiates a new search via searchProducts.
Changing the sort option updates the sortBy URL parameter and re-calls searchProducts with page reset to the first page.
If a vehicle is selected, toggling the vehicle fitment limitation updates the fitmentScope URL parameter and re-runs searchProducts to include or exclude vehicle-based filtering.
Clicking a product’s image or title triggers navigation to product.html with id set to the product’s id and ref set to search.

Clicking a product’s comparison checkbox adds or removes that productId from a local list of selected products in the comparison-selection-toolbar; this does not affect URL parameters or any interface calls until Compare is clicked.

Clicking the Compare button in the comparison-selection-toolbar calls createCompareSession with the selected productIds and then navigates to compare.html with compareSessionId set to the returned session id.
Using pagination controls changes the page URL parameter and triggers a new searchProducts call to load results for the selected page.

When there are no results (searchProducts.pagination.totalItems indicates zero), a no-results state is shown with an option to clear all filters; clicking that option removes all filter-related URL parameters (leaving q and optional fitmentScope) and re-executes searchProducts.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s state is fully driven by URL parameters and data returned by the assigned interfaces, without using localStorage or other persistent client storage. The main URL parameters are: q (search query), priceMin, priceMax, ratingMin, featureAppleCarPlay, featureBluetooth, featureBackupCameraInput, featureFreeShipping, featureIncludesWiringKit, rmsPowerMin, channelCount, fitmentScope, sortBy, and page. On load and whenever the URL changes, the page performs these steps: (1) Read q from the URL. If q is absent or empty, the page may show an appropriate message but does not invent a query. (2) Call getSelectedVehicle with no parameters to obtain hasSelectedVehicle and the selected Vehicle (if any). This information is used only for display and to enable/disable the fitmentScope toggle; the selected vehicle is not encoded directly in the URL. (3) Call getSearchFilterOptions with query=q to retrieve available ranges and options for numeric and feature filters. The filter-sidebar uses this to decide which controls to render and how to constrain user input. (4) Construct a filters object for searchProducts from the current URL parameters: priceMin and priceMax are mapped from their numeric URL values if present; ratingMin from its numeric URL value; featureAppleCarPlay, featureBluetooth, featureBackupCameraInput, featureFreeShipping, and featureIncludesWiringKit are interpreted as booleans based on the presence and value of their URL parameters; rmsPowerMin and channelCount come from their numeric URL values when present. (5) Determine fitmentScope from the URL; if missing, the behavior is equivalent to not restricting by vehicle. (6) Determine sortBy from the URL; if missing, the implementation may choose a default, such as relevance. (7) Determine page from the URL; if missing or invalid, default to the first page. (8) Call searchProducts with the derived query, filters, fitmentScope, sortBy, page, and a configured pageSize that is consistent across calls. The page never hardcodes specific product ids, category ids, or content values; it only uses dynamic data returned by the interfaces and values from the URL. Whenever a user interaction changes filters, sort, fitmentScope, or pagination, the page updates the URL parameters to reflect the new state, then repeats steps (4) through (8). Temporary UI-only state, such as which products are currently selected for comparison, is stored in runtime component state only and is not reflected in the URL or persisted across reloads.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading URL parameters, fetching the selected vehicle context, fetching available filter options for the current query, and executing the main product search. It coordinates re-fetching of data when the URL changes.
event_handlers: onLoad: Read all URL parameters and invoke getSelectedVehicle, getSearchFilterOptions(query=q), and searchProducts(query, filters, fitmentScope, sortBy, page, pageSize).

onUrlChange: Whenever the URL query string changes (for example, via filter, sort, fitment toggle, or pagination interaction), reconstruct the filters object from URL parameters and re-call searchProducts with the new parameters.
onSearchResultsUpdate: Propagate the latest searchProducts response (items and pagination) to child components such as search-query-header, filter-sidebar, product-grid, and pagination-controls.

components.functionality: Displays the current search query, the total number of matching products, and the active sort mode. Optionally provides an embedded search input that submits a new query.
event_handlers: onSearchSubmit: Submit a form using the GET method to search_results.html with q set to the new query value and without preserving existing filter, sort, or page URL parameters, so the new query starts with default filters.
onSortSummaryClick: Optionally opens sort controls if they are collapsed, but does not itself modify data.

components.functionality: Shows the currently selected vehicle context (if any) and provides a control to limit search results to products that fit the selected vehicle by manipulating the fitmentScope URL parameter.
event_handlers: onToggleFitmentScope: When the user enables vehicle limitation, set or update the fitmentScope URL parameter to represent limiting to the selected vehicle and reset the page URL parameter to the first page; when disabling, remove or clear fitmentScope and reset page, then let search-results-init re-run searchProducts with the new fitmentScope value.

components.functionality: Provides interactive controls for refining search results by price, rating, features, RMS power, and channel count. It uses getSearchFilterOptions to determine which filters are available for the current query and uses URL parameters to read and update the selected filter values.
event_handlers: onPriceChange: When the user adjusts the price range, update priceMin and/or priceMax URL parameters, remove them if cleared, reset page to the first page, and let search-results-init re-call searchProducts.
onRatingChange: When the user selects a minimum rating, set ratingMin in the URL, remove it if cleared, reset page, and trigger a new search via URL change.

onFeatureToggle: For each feature filter (Apple CarPlay, Bluetooth, backup camera input, free shipping, includes wiring kit), set or remove the corresponding boolean URL parameter and reset page, causing a new searchProducts call.
onRmsPowerChange: Update rmsPowerMin in the URL when the user chooses a minimum RMS power, remove it if cleared, reset page, and trigger a new search.
onChannelCountChange: Update channelCount URL parameter based on the chosen value, remove it if cleared, reset page, and trigger a new search.

onClearFilters: Remove all filter-related URL parameters (priceMin, priceMax, ratingMin, all feature flags, rmsPowerMin, channelCount) while preserving q and optional fitmentScope, set page to the first page, and allow search-results-init to execute an unfiltered search for the current query.

components.functionality: Displays sort options (such as relevance, price ascending or descending, and review-based options) and a summary of which results range is currently visible based on pagination. Changing the sort updates the URL and triggers a new search.
event_handlers: onSortChange: When the user selects a different sort mode, update the sortBy URL parameter, reset page to the first page, and let search-results-init call searchProducts with the new sortBy value.
onPageSizeDisplay: Uses searchProducts.pagination values to compute and display the index range of visible items; this is a display-only computation and does not modify state.

components.functionality: Renders a list or grid of products from the searchProducts response, showing key product attributes and controls such as links to product detail pages and comparison checkboxes.
event_handlers: onProductClick: When a user clicks a product image or title, navigate to product.html with id set to the clicked product’s id and ref set to search, without altering any other state on the search results page.

onCompareCheckboxToggle: When a user toggles a comparison checkbox for a product, emit an event containing the productId and the new checked state so that the comparison-selection-toolbar can update its internal list of selected products.

components.functionality: Maintains a transient list of selected products for comparison and allows the user to initiate a comparison session via createCompareSession, then navigates to the comparison page.
event_handlers: onCompareSelectionChange: Respond to events from product-grid compare checkboxes by adding or removing productIds from local.selectedProductIds.

onCompareClick: When the user clicks the Compare button and the selection rules are satisfied, call createCompareSession with productIds set to local.selectedProductIds; on success, read compareSession.id from the response and navigate to compare.html with compareSessionId set to that id.
onClearSelection: Clear local.selectedProductIds and notify product-grid to uncheck all comparison checkboxes; this does not affect URL parameters or search results.

components.functionality: Displays pagination controls based on searchProducts.pagination and allows navigating between result pages by updating the page URL parameter.
event_handlers: onPageChange: When the user clicks a pagination control (such as next, previous, or a specific page number), update the page URL parameter to the chosen page while preserving q, filters, sortBy, and fitmentScope; this triggers search-results-init to re-run searchProducts for the new page.
onFirstPageRequest: Optionally set page to the first page when filters or sort are changed elsewhere; this is coordinated through URL updates rather than direct calls to searchProducts.

components.functionality: Displays a message and recovery actions when searchProducts returns zero items for the current query and filters.
event_handlers: onClearFiltersClick: If there are no results, remove all filter-related URL parameters (priceMin, priceMax, ratingMin, feature flags, rmsPowerMin, channelCount), keep q and optional fitmentScope, reset page to the first page, and rely on search-results-init to perform a new search with relaxed criteria.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: Within the existing fixed-width, center-aligned site shell (logo band, nav band, and possibly a global hero above), the search results page begins with a strong, full-width content header band that plays the role of a mini-hero for the query. Directly beneath that sits a slimmer contextual band for vehicle fitment. Below these two stacked bands, the page breaks into a classic two-column structure: a broad main results column on the left (about 70% of the content width) and a narrower filter sidebar on the right (about 30%). The left column carries the sort bar at the top, then the product grid, then pagination and comparison-selection elements at the bottom. The right column is a tall, continuous filter card. The data-loader overlays this entire content area when active, and the no-results-message replaces the product grid area when there are no matching products.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Within the existing fixed-width, center-aligned site shell (logo band, nav band, and possibly a global hero above), the search results page begins with a strong, full-width content header band that plays the role of a mini-hero for the query. Directly beneath that sits a slimmer contextual band for vehicle fitment. Below these two stacked bands, the page breaks into a classic two-column structure: a broad main results column on the left (about 70% of the content width) and a narrower filter sidebar on the right (about 30%). The left column carries the sort bar at the top, then the product grid, then pagination and comparison-selection elements at the bottom. The right column is a tall, continuous filter card. The data-loader overlays this entire content area when active, and the no-results-message replaces the product grid area when there are no matching products.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content container spans roughly 960–1024px and is centered within a full-bleed gradient background. The query header band and vehicle-fitment band together occupy about the top 20–25% of the above-the-fold search results area. The remaining 75–80% is dedicated to the two-column results body: the left column uses about 70% of the container width for the sort bar, grid, and pagination, while the right column uses about 30% for filters. Vertically, each major band (query header, fitment context, results body) is visually separated by 24–32px worth of gradient transitions or borders rather than large blank gaps, keeping the page visually full and continuous.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately dense to high, echoing the marketing-heavy mockup. Every major section is inside a visually treated band or card with gradients, bevels, and clear edges. Text blocks are kept short and strong (headlines, counts, labels, filter titles), always anchored by imagery or strong chrome. Cards and bars have 12–16px internal padding and 24–32px vertical spacing between them. The product grid is tightly packed with only 16–24px gutters, and the filter sidebar stacks many compact filter groups. Overall, almost every pixel has color, texture, or UI elements, resulting in a visually busy, energetic layout rather than a sparse one.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first hits the search-query-header at the top-left of the content area, reading the bold query title and result count, then sweeps right to any supplementary controls in that band. It drops down into the vehicle-fitment-context strip, where the left-aligned vehicle icon or badge and fitment summary provide context. From there, the eye descends into the main results area: it catches the sort-and-page-size bar, then flows diagonally down into the top row of product cards in the product-grid. The strong product photography reinforces a left-to-right scanning pattern across each row. The dense, high-contrast filter-sidebar on the right is a persistent secondary focal column that the eye dips into periodically. At the bottom, pagination-controls draw attention for navigation, while the comparison-selection-toolbar, when active, forms a high-contrast band at the lower edge of the viewport, pulling the eye to selected items and the path to comparison. When no results exist, the no-results-message becomes the primary focal card in the main column, framed by the still-present filters on the right.

layout.overall_description: Inside the site’s glossy, gradient-framed shell, the search results page presents as a stacked series of bold, horizontal bands that quickly resolve into a two-column product listing. A bright, beveled query header stretches across the full content width at the top, announcing the search term and result count like a promotional headline. Immediately below, a slimmer, chrome-edged fitment strip reinforces vehicle context. Under this header block, the layout splits: on the left, a wide results column begins with a shiny sort-and-page-size bar, then drops into tightly packed rows of product cards with vivid photography and metallic gradients; at the bottom of this column, pagination controls sit in a compact bar, and a glowing comparison toolbar can rise up along the bottom edge when items are selected. On the right, a tall, slightly inset filter sidebar runs from near the top of the results down toward the fold, segmented into stacked filter groups with their own mini-headers. The entire assembly sits on layered gradients that subtly shift between sections, so the page feels like a continuous, energetic dashboard of car audio products rather than a minimalist search list. When loading, a semi-transparent overlay with a centered spinner washes over both columns, and when no products are found, a bold central message card in the left column replaces the grid while the filters remain accessible on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader manifests as a semi-transparent dark overlay that covers the entire search results content container, spanning from just below the global navigation/hero area down to just above the footer. It stretches across both the left main results column and the right filter sidebar, matching the full width of the centered content (roughly 960–1024px) and extending to the full visible height of the content region. At its center—both horizontally and vertically within the viewport—a glossy, beveled loading panel (about 240–320px wide) contains a spinner or progress indicator with a short loading tagline. The underlying query header, fitment context, sort bar, grid, filters, and pagination remain visible but dimmed beneath the overlay, reinforcing the sense of a single cohesive page temporarily paused while data refreshes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-screen overlay relative to the content area, positioned fixed to the viewport while active so that it remains centered even if the user scrolls; it sits above all other components in stacking order until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-query-header is the first visible component within the content container, positioned directly beneath the site’s navigation/hero band. It appears as a bold, glossy horizontal strip spanning the full width of the central content area (roughly 960–1024px) with no side gutters inside the container, but with colored gradient margins on the outer page background. Vertically, it is relatively tall—about 80–96px high—to read like a mini-hero. Inside, content is left-aligned: on the top-left, a large, uppercase headline presents the search term (e.g., “SEARCH RESULTS FOR ‘SUBWOOFER’”) in a bright green or yellow, with a subtle outer glow; below or to the right of it in smaller type, the result count and perhaps a brief subtitle explain what’s being shown. On the right side of the band, aligned horizontally with the headline, there is room for a compact auxiliary control cluster (e.g., ‘Refine search’ link or quick toggle), but this remains visually lighter than the main title. The band’s bottom edge aligns visually with the start of the vehicle-fitment-context strip, separated by a thin highlight line or a tight 8–12px gradient fade rather than a large gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, anchoring the top of the search results area; it scrolls off-screen as the user browses down the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The vehicle-fitment-context sits directly below the search-query-header as a slightly slimmer, but still glossy, horizontal band that spans the exact same content width. It is approximately 48–64px tall, acting as a contextual subheader. The band uses a slightly darker or more muted gradient than the query header to indicate secondary priority, with a crisp top border or highlight that visually ties it to the band above. On the left side of this strip, starting about 16px from the content edge, a small car icon or silhouette and a bold label (e.g., “YOUR VEHICLE”) precede the selected vehicle information in a brighter accent color. To the right of the vehicle specs, there is a concise ‘Change vehicle’ link or button, aligned on the same baseline. The right side of the band can host a compact indicator (e.g., “Showing only products that fit” with a checkmark) aligned to the right edge, giving a sense of reassurance. Vertically, this band is separated from the two-column body that follows by approximately 16–24px of space, expressed mostly through a gradient transition and a soft drop shadow under the band rather than a pure blank gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, sitting immediately above the split into the two-column results area; it scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar occupies the right-hand column of the two-column layout, starting just below the vehicle-fitment-context band. It aligns with the right edge of the central content container and spans roughly 30% of the container width (about 280–320px on a 960–1024px grid). Vertically, the sidebar runs down most of the viewport, beginning around the same vertical line as the sort-and-page-size-bar in the left column, with a small top margin of 16–24px to visually separate it from the fitment band above. The sidebar itself is a tall, glossy card with slightly inset edges: a metallic or dark gradient background, subtle inner glow, and a thin, bright border to separate it from the page background. Inside, filter groups are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing between them. Each group starts with a beveled header bar (about 32–40px tall) containing the filter title in uppercase on the left and a small collapse/expand icon on the right. Below each header, checkboxes, sliders, and pill buttons are arranged in compact rows with 8–12px vertical spacing. The sidebar creates a solid, uninterrupted visual column that balances the left-side product grid while reinforcing the page’s dense, dashboard-like feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the two-column flow, the sidebar is positioned to the right of the main results and scrolls with the page; optionally, its inner content area can become independently scrollable if filters exceed viewport height, while the outer sidebar container stays aligned with the main column top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-and-page-size-bar sits at the very top of the left main results column, horizontally aligned so that its left edge lines up with the content container’s left edge and its right edge aligns with the boundary between the main column and the filter sidebar. Placed approximately 16–24px below the vehicle-fitment-context, it appears as a low-profile but glossy control strip about 48–56px tall. The bar uses a metallic gradient with a subtle bevel and a slightly lighter tone than the main background, visually connecting it to other chrome UI elements. Inside, on the left side, there is a compact label such as “Sort by:” followed by a dropdown; on the right side, aligned to the column’s right edge, a “Show: 24 | 48 | 96 per page” control or a page-size dropdown is placed. There is a gentle 16px horizontal padding on both sides and 8–12px vertical padding inside the bar. The bottom edge of this strip carries a thin divider or shadow to clearly delineate it from the denser product-grid below, creating a crisp transition while keeping the vertical separation tight (8–16px).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column’s normal flow, acting as the top control row for the results; it scrolls off-screen as the user moves down the product grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid is the dominant body of the left column, positioned directly below the sort-and-page-size-bar with a modest vertical gap of about 16px. It fills the remaining vertical space of the left column and spans its full width, which is roughly 70% of the content container. The grid presents products in compact, card-like tiles arranged in 2–3 columns depending on available width (typically 3 columns on a 960–1024px grid, each tile about 30–32% of the column width, with 16px gutters). Each product tile has a glossy card background with a dark-to-light gradient, a subtle drop shadow, and 12–16px internal padding. The top portion of each card is dominated by product photography, taking roughly the upper half of the tile height, with the product image slightly larger than strictly necessary to echo the mockup’s emphasis on imagery. Below the image, a bold product name, a short 1–2 line description, price, fitment badge, and a ‘Compare’ checkbox or toggle are stacked in a tight vertical rhythm with 4–8px spacing. The row spacing between products is about 16–24px, keeping the grid feeling full but legible. Horizontally, the grid’s right edge aligns visually with the left edge of the filter-sidebar, leaving a crisp vertical seam that reinforces the two-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow of the left column, extending downward as results increase; it dictates the page height and scroll behavior, with new rows simply flowing below the fold.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-selection-toolbar is a high-impact, horizontal band that appears when the user selects one or more products to compare. It spans the full width of the central content container, crossing beneath both the left product column and the right filter sidebar so it feels like a unified control tray. Visually, it sits near the bottom edge of the viewport as a floating, glossy strip about 64–80px tall, with a vibrant green or yellow gradient, strong bevel, and inner glow, making it stand out from the darker background. Inside the tray, on the left side, a bold label such as “COMPARE (3 SELECTED)” appears, while the center and right portions display small thumbnail images or names of selected products, laid out in a horizontal row with 8–12px gaps. On the far right, a prominent ‘Compare Now’ button and a ‘Clear’ link are grouped together. The tray casts a subtle shadow onto the content above, clearly differentiating it as an overlay element. When no items are selected, the toolbar is either hidden entirely or reduced to a very slim tab (about 24px tall) tucked along the bottom of the content area, showing a low-contrast ‘Select products to compare’ prompt.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky overlay anchored to the bottom of the viewport within the search results page; when active, it remains fixed as the user scrolls, always visible above the footer and above the scrolling content columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the bottom of the left main results column, directly below the last row of the product-grid with a 24–32px vertical gap that visually concludes the grid. They are constrained to the width of the left column, not extending under the filter sidebar. The controls are housed inside a compact, bevelled bar about 40–48px tall, with a subtle gradient that differentiates it from the background but keeps it lighter in visual weight than the comparison toolbar. Inside the bar, page numbers, ‘Previous’ and ‘Next’ buttons are centered horizontally or right-aligned, depending on the overall design direction, with consistent 8–12px spacing between controls. Small glowing hover states on the buttons continue the glossy visual language. The bar’s top border may feature a thin highlight line to cap off the grid section. Below the bar, there is a modest 24–32px gap before any following content or the page footer, giving the end of the results a clear termination point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column’s flow, positioned after the grid; it scrolls off-screen as the user moves up and down the page, appearing again when the user reaches the bottom of the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results-message replaces the product grid area in the left column when a search yields no matching products. It is positioned in the same column where the product-grid would normally appear, directly below the sort-and-page-size-bar and aligned with the left column’s edges. The message appears as a prominent, centered card about 60–70% of the left column width (leaving subtle side padding inside the column) with generous vertical padding of 32–48px, making it visually substantial without feeling like a full hero. The card has a bright, slightly lighter gradient than the surrounding background, a clear border, and a soft shadow, drawing the eye immediately. At the top center of the card, a large icon (e.g., a magnifying glass with a slash) sits above a bold headline like “No Results Found,” followed by a brief explanatory text in shorter lines. Beneath this, suggested actions (e.g., ‘Try different keywords’ or ‘Adjust your filters’) are presented as a compact bullet list or small pill buttons, aligned center. The filter-sidebar on the right remains fully visible and functional, creating a visual contrast: a large, focused message on the left and a dense, interactive controls column on the right, prompting the user to adjust filters or search terms.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow of the left column, conditionally rendered in place of the product-grid and pagination-controls; it scrolls normally with the rest of the page.

name: Product Comparison
title: Product Comparison
description: Displays selected car audio products side by side, showing key specifications and highlighting differences to support spec-based decision making. Users can remove or replace products in the comparison and open individual product detail pages.

core_features: Load comparison data for a given compareSessionId using getCompareSessionDetails and render all products and their specs side by side.
Display key spec rows (e.g., power, size, connectivity) derived from the specs object in getCompareSessionDetails for each product.
Highlight differences between products by visually emphasizing spec cells whose values differ or are highest/lowest within the row, controlled by a user toggle.
Allow navigation to individual product detail pages by clicking the product name or image, using product.html?id={product.id}&ref=compare.
Allow users to remove a product from the comparison, updating the CompareSession via updateCompareSessionProducts and refreshing the comparison view.
Allow users to replace a product in the comparison by submitting a new productId, updating the CompareSession via updateCompareSessionProducts, and re-rendering the updated comparison.
Support clearing all products from the comparison by updating the CompareSession’s productIds to an empty or reduced set via updateCompareSessionProducts.
Handle error and empty states when compareSessionId is missing, invalid, or when the CompareSession has no products, with functional messaging and options to recover (e.g., clear or navigate away).
user_workflows:
Workflow: Viewing a comparison
1. User lands on compare.html with a compareSessionId URL parameter.
2. On page initialization, the page reads compareSessionId from the URL and calls getCompareSessionDetails(compareSessionId).
3. While loading, a functional loading state is shown.
4. On success, the page renders a comparison table with one column per product and one row per relevant spec, along with overall info (price, rating if present in the product object).
5. User can optionally toggle a 'Show differences only' control; the table recalculates which rows differ and hides identical rows when this mode is active.
6. User reviews specs and differences to support their decision.

Workflow: Opening a product detail from the comparison
1. In the comparison table, the user clicks on a product’s name or image.
2. The click handler reads that product’s id.
3. The browser navigates to product.html with URL parameters id={product.id} and ref=compare.
4. The user can use the browser back button to return to the comparison page, which will reload using the same compareSessionId URL parameter.

Workflow: Removing a product from comparison
1. User identifies a product column to remove and clicks its 'Remove' control.
2. The page builds a new productIds array by taking the current CompareSession.productIds and excluding the removed product’s id.
3. The page calls updateCompareSessionProducts(compareSessionId, productIds).
4. While awaiting the response, the product column shows a pending state or the whole table shows a brief updating state.
5. On success, the page updates its in-memory comparison data either from the updateCompareSessionProducts response or by re-calling getCompareSessionDetails, and re-renders the comparison table without that product.
6. If the resulting comparison has no products, the empty comparison state is shown.

Workflow: Replacing a product in comparison
1. User clicks a 'Replace' control on a specific product column.
2. A small inline replace form appears for that column, allowing the user to input a new productId (or some identifier that the backend accepts as productId).
3. On form submit, the component constructs a new productIds array by copying the current CompareSession.productIds and replacing the selected index with the submitted new productId.
4. The page calls updateCompareSessionProducts(compareSessionId, productIds).
5. While waiting, the replaced column shows an updating state.
6. On success, the updated CompareSession and items from the response are bound to the comparison table, which now shows the newly added product in that column.
7. If the backend rejects the new productId (e.g., invalid or inactive product), the component displays an error message and restores the previous productIds without changing the table.

Workflow: Clearing the comparison
1. User clicks a 'Clear all' control in the comparison toolbar when multiple products are present.
2. The page constructs an updated productIds array representing the cleared state (such as an empty array).
3. The page calls updateCompareSessionProducts(compareSessionId, productIds).
4. On success, the page detects that there are no products in compareSession.productIds and switches to an empty comparison state, informing the user that no products are being compared.

Workflow: Sharing or bookmarking a comparison
1. User copies the browser URL for the comparison page (which includes compareSessionId as a query parameter).
2. When another user opens this URL or the original user revisits the bookmark, the page again reads compareSessionId from the URL and calls getCompareSessionDetails(compareSessionId).
3. The page reconstructs the comparison table exactly as it was last saved by the updateCompareSessionProducts operations, subject to any product-level availability rules managed by the backend.

Workflow: Handling invalid or missing compareSessionId
1. User opens compare.html without a compareSessionId, or with a value that causes getCompareSessionDetails to fail.
2. The page attempts to read compareSessionId from the URL; if it is missing, it immediately enters an error state explaining that no comparison session could be loaded.
3. If compareSessionId is present but getCompareSessionDetails returns an error, the page shows an error state with a functional message and suggests navigating back to category or search pages.
4. No comparison table is rendered until a valid compareSessionId is available in the URL.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial data load
- Trigger: Page load or URL change that affects compareSessionId.
- Behavior: Read compareSessionId from URL query string; if present, call getCompareSessionDetails(compareSessionId). Manage loading, success, and error states. If not present, show error state.

Interaction: Toggle 'Show differences only'
- Trigger: User clicks the differences toggle control.
- Behavior: Local UI state flips between 'all specs' and 'differences only'. The component compares values across all products for each spec row from getCompareSessionDetails.items[].specs and hides rows where all values are identical when the toggle is active. No API calls are made.

Interaction: Navigate to product detail
- Trigger: User clicks a product name or image in the comparison table.
- Behavior: Read the clicked product’s id from the bound Product field. Redirect browser to product.html with URL parameters id={product.id} and ref=compare. No API calls on this page are needed for the navigation.

Interaction: Remove product from comparison
- Trigger: User clicks 'Remove' button on a product column.
- Behavior: Build a new productIds array by filtering out that product’s id from the currently loaded compareSession.productIds. Call updateCompareSessionProducts(compareSessionId, productIds). On success, update local compareSession and items from the API response or via a subsequent call to getCompareSessionDetails. Re-render the table.

Interaction: Replace product via inline form
- Trigger: User clicks 'Replace' on a product column, then submits a new productId in the inline form.
- Behavior: On form submit, validate that some value is present, then create a new productIds array by copying compareSession.productIds and replacing the productId at that column’s index with the submitted new productId. Call updateCompareSessionProducts(compareSessionId, productIds). On success, bind returned compareSession and items to the UI. On failure, display an inline error and keep the previous product in place.

Interaction: Clear all products
- Trigger: User clicks 'Clear all' in the toolbar.
- Behavior: Set productIds to an empty array (or another cleared configuration as defined by backend rules) and call updateCompareSessionProducts(compareSessionId, productIds). On success, show the empty comparison state.

Interaction: Refresh comparison
- Trigger: User manually refreshes the page or revisits the URL from history/bookmark.
- Behavior: compareSessionId is re-read from the URL; getCompareSessionDetails(compareSessionId) is called again and the comparison table is rebuilt.

Interaction: Error recovery
- Trigger: getCompareSessionDetails or updateCompareSessionProducts returns an error.
- Behavior: Show an error message component with the error information supplied by the backend if available. The user can retry actions such as 'Reload comparison' (calling getCompareSessionDetails again) or navigate away. No local state is persisted beyond the current page instance.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from the URL parameter compareSessionId. On every page load, the script parses compareSessionId from the query string of compare.html. If compareSessionId is present, getCompareSessionDetails(compareSessionId) is called to obtain the CompareSession and its items. The returned compareSession.productIds and items array become the in-memory state used to render the comparison table. When the user removes, replaces, or clears products, the page constructs an updated productIds array and calls updateCompareSessionProducts(compareSessionId, productIds). The API response provides a new CompareSession and items set, which replace the previous in-memory state. The URL compareSessionId does not change during these operations, allowing the comparison state to remain addressable via the same URL. No state is stored in localStorage or other client-side persistence; the server-side CompareSession identified by compareSessionId is the single source of truth and is always reloaded via getCompareSessionDetails upon page load. View-only UI toggles like 'Show differences only' are kept in transient client-side state and do not affect the URL or backend data.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial loading of comparison data, manages global loading and error states, and passes CompareSession and items to child components.
Displays high-level comparison controls such as product count, a 'Clear all' button, and a 'Show differences only' toggle.

Renders a matrix of products (columns) versus specs and key attributes (rows), using items[].product and items[].specs from getCompareSessionDetails. Applies difference-highlighting logic based on the current differences-only state.

For each product column, shows the product image, name, price, rating (if available), and per-product actions like 'Remove' and 'Replace'. Handles navigation to product detail pages when clicking name or image.

Iterates over the specs properties returned in getCompareSessionDetails (rmsPower, speakerSizeInches, speakerType, dinSize, channelCount, hasAppleCarPlay, hasBluetooth, hasBackupCameraInput, and any other spec fields provided). For each spec key, creates a row and displays values per product, applying highlighting and optionally hiding rows when the 'differences only' mode is active.

Appears within a product column when the user chooses to replace that product. Accepts a new productId input and, on submission, constructs and submits an updated productIds array via updateCompareSessionProducts.
Provides the 'Remove' control for each product column, and when activated, updates the CompareSession productIds by excluding the selected productId through updateCompareSessionProducts.
Allows the user to switch between showing all spec rows and showing only rows where at least one product differs. Updates local UI state that other components (like spec-rows) use to filter rows.

Displays when compareSession.productIds is empty after loading. Informs the user that no products are currently being compared and may provide navigation links to category or search pages (via URL navigation) without calling additional interfaces.
Shows while waiting for getCompareSessionDetails or updateCompareSessionProducts to complete, blocking or dimming interaction with the comparison table as needed.

Displays when there is no compareSessionId in the URL or when getCompareSessionDetails/updateCompareSessionProducts returns an error. Provides an option to retry loading if compareSessionId is present.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page sits inside the existing fixed‑width, center‑aligned shell (logo + social header band, nav band, hero). The Product Comparison experience occupies the left main column of the two‑column body (roughly 70% width), directly under a comparison‑specific hero strip. Within this main column, components flow top‑to‑bottom in distinct bands: (1) a comparison page controller band that introduces the comparison and anchors the module, (2) a glossy comparison toolbar band for session controls, and (3) the comparison table band as the dominant visual area. Empty, loading, and error states all occupy the same table band region, replacing the table when needed. Inside the table band, content is arranged as vertical stacks of spec rows, with horizontal product columns running across, echoing the mockup’s modular, card‑like structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page sits inside the existing fixed‑width, center‑aligned shell (logo + social header band, nav band, hero). The Product Comparison experience occupies the left main column of the two‑column body (roughly 70% width), directly under a comparison‑specific hero strip. Within this main column, components flow top‑to‑bottom in distinct bands: (1) a comparison page controller band that introduces the comparison and anchors the module, (2) a glossy comparison toolbar band for session controls, and (3) the comparison table band as the dominant visual area. Empty, loading, and error states all occupy the same table band region, replacing the table when needed. Inside the table band, content is arranged as vertical stacks of spec rows, with horizontal product columns running across, echoing the mockup’s modular, card‑like structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and navigation still consume the top ~15–20% of the initial viewport, and a comparison‑specific hero/intro strip uses another ~20–25% of the above‑the‑fold vertical space. Below that, the comparison module in the left column fills most of the remaining vertical space. Horizontally, the fixed content container is ~960–1024px wide, center‑aligned. Within this, the main comparison column occupies about 70% of the width (roughly 670–720px), while the existing right sidebar takes the remaining 30%. The comparison toolbar spans the full width of the main column but remains visually slimmer (about 56–72px tall), while the comparison table band beneath it is deep enough to show 8–12 spec rows above the fold. Within the table, a narrow left spec‑label column uses about 20–25% of the table width, and the remaining 75–80% is divided evenly among product columns.
layout_strategies.content_density: The comparison area follows a moderate‑to‑high density style: gradients, borders, and subtle textures define every band. There is consistent inner padding (12–16px) on all sides of the page controller band, the toolbar, and each table cell, so content feels packed but still legible. Spec rows are relatively tight (row heights around 40–56px), with limited whitespace between rows; contrast and alternating backgrounds are used instead of large gaps. Headings and product names are bold and prominent, while body text is short and punchy. Visual elements like product imagery in column headers and glossy buttons in the toolbar consume visible space, maintaining the lively, marketing‑forward feel of the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a Z‑pattern adapted to this page: from the global logo at top‑left to the nav on the right, then down into the comparison hero text in the left column, landing first on the bold comparison title inside the page controller. From there, the eye moves horizontally across the comparison toolbar controls (including the differences toggle on the right), then drops into the comparison table. Within the table, attention starts at the left spec‑label column, then sweeps right across the product column headers with product images and key badges. As users scroll, sticky or semi‑persistent elements (toolbar and column headers) keep the session controls and product identities in view, reinforcing the vertical "step" rhythm established in the mockup through bands, gradients, and card edges.

layout.overall_description: Inside the site’s familiar high‑contrast shell, the Product Comparison page appears as a strong, glossy module anchored in the left main column beneath the hero. The central content area is a fixed‑width, center‑aligned container, with background gradients extending to the browser edges in saturated greens and yellows. The left ~70% of this container is occupied by a tall comparison block; the right ~30% continues to host the "Latest Products"‑style sidebar from the original mockup. At the top of the comparison block, a beveled, gradient-backed page controller band introduces the comparison with a bold title and short description, visually echoing the mockup’s promo strip. Directly beneath it, a darker comparison toolbar forms a slim horizontal control band, with session actions aligned left and the differences toggle shining on the right as a pill-shaped, glowing switch. Below, the comparison table expands to fill the rest of the main column, framed by a glossy border and subtle outer glow. Within this table band, a narrow left column lists spec names in stacked rows, while 2–4 product columns extend rightward, each crowned by a product header card featuring imagery and basic info. Spec rows alternate between darker and lighter gradients, keeping a dense but structured feel. When no products are selected or data is loading, this table region transforms into a hero-like empty or loading card with centered messaging and iconography, maintaining the same width and banded position. As users scroll down through specs, the product headers and toolbar remain visually anchored at the top of the table area, keeping the high-energy, vertical step‑down flow that mirrors the original car audio mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The compare-page-controller is the topmost band of the comparison module in the left main column, sitting directly beneath the page’s hero/intro area and aligned to the left edge of the main content column. It spans the full width of that column (about 70% of the total page width, roughly 670–720px inside the 960–1024px central container) and has a card-like presentation: a glossy gradient background (e.g., deep green fading to black from left to right), a subtle inner bevel, and a 1–2px bright top border that echoes the promo strips in the mockup. Vertically, it occupies approximately 80–120px, depending on text content. Inside, content is left-aligned: the main heading (e.g., "Compare Products") sits at the top-left with 16–24px top padding, followed by a brief 1–2 line description. On the right side of this band, horizontally aligned with the heading, there is room for a small status indicator (e.g., "3 items selected") or a compact link to clear the comparison. The bottom edge of this controller band sits about 16px above the comparison-toolbar, creating a clear but tight visual step between bands. On narrower viewports, this band remains fixed-width and centered, preserving the early-2010s fixed layout, with text wrapping within the fixed column instead of reflowing to full-bleed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal document flow; defines the top boundary of the comparison module. It does not scroll independently, but as the user scrolls down the page, it moves off-screen along with the main content, leaving the global header and nav in place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-toolbar is a slim, full-width control band directly beneath the compare-page-controller within the main left column, with only a narrow gap of about 8–16px separating them. It spans the same width as the controller (about 70% of page width inside the central container) and has a slightly darker, more metallic gradient background (e.g., dark charcoal to black) with a subtle bottom glow that visually connects it to the brighter comparison table below. The toolbar height is around 56–72px, creating a compact strip of controls. Inside, content is arranged in a single horizontal line: on the far left, there is a cluster of session controls (e.g., "Add Product", "Clear All", or a breadcrumb-like label) arranged from left to right with 16px spacing between buttons and 16px padding from the left edge. These controls are glossy, pill-shaped buttons consistent with the site’s marketing-heavy aesthetic. On the far right of the toolbar, aligned vertically with the left controls, sits the differences-toggle component, occupying about 25–30% of the toolbar width. The middle area remains relatively open, allowing the eye to travel across. This band visually "caps" the comparison table below and appears as a persistent control header for the whole comparison area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a semi-sticky control bar: in a long comparison with many spec rows, the toolbar can optionally remain stuck to the top edge of the viewport once the user has scrolled it into that position, while the comparison table content scrolls underneath. In absence of sticky behavior, it still forms a clear, fixed band at the top of the table area within the normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-table occupies the largest portion of the comparison module, sitting directly under the comparison-toolbar with a small 8–12px gap. It spans the full width of the main content column (about 70% of the page), framed by a high-contrast border and subtle outer glow that makes it feel like a glossy hardware panel. Its top edge aligns flush with the left and right inner edges of the main column, with 16px padding on all sides inside the border. Vertically, the table extends down the page, typically showing 8–12 spec rows above the fold, with additional rows reachable via scrolling. Inside the table, a fixed left spec-label column occupies roughly 20–25% of the table’s width, while the remaining width is divided evenly among 2–4 product columns. Each product column begins with its product-column-header at the very top, forming a horizontal row of product cards beneath the toolbar. Below this header row, spec-rows are stacked vertically, forming a grid where each row spans across the spec-label column and all product columns. Table rows are separated by 1px dividers and alternating gradient backgrounds to maintain a tight but readable, high-density grid. The entire table area sits about 24–32px above any content that follows (such as a footer), providing a clear visual end to the comparison section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column but vertically scrollable as part of the page. The left spec-label column and product-column-header elements can be configured to remain visually fixed relative to their axes (header sticky at the top of the table, labels sticky on the left) to support easier comparison, while the individual spec cells scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each product-column-header sits at the top of its respective product column inside the comparison-table, forming a horizontal strip of 2–4 visually rich product cards directly beneath the comparison-toolbar. The headers are aligned side by side from left to right, starting just to the right of the spec-label column. Each header occupies an equal fraction of the right-hand portion of the table (e.g., if there are 3 products, each header uses about 25–26% of the table width, leaving 22–25% for the spec-label column). Vertically, each header is about 140–180px tall, creating a prominent area for product imagery and summary. The header background uses a bold gradient (e.g., neon green to dark green) and a subtle inner glow to distinguish it from the more neutral spec rows. Within each header, the product image is positioned toward the upper half, either centered or slightly left-biased within the column, taking roughly 50–60% of the header height. Beneath or to the right of the image, the product name appears in uppercase, bold text, followed by 1–2 key spec bullets or badges. In the top-right corner of each header, the remove-product-action is overlaid as a small, glossy icon/button, while the bottom area of the header provides a slot where the product-replace-form can slide down inline when activated. The bottom edge of each header aligns perfectly with the top edge of the first spec row cells for that product column, creating a clean grid line across the entire table.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the anchored top section of each product column. Within the scrolling comparison-table, the row of product-column-headers can remain sticky to the top of the table area, so when a user scrolls through many spec rows, the headers stay visible as a reference. The header content itself does not scroll horizontally; it remains locked to its column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The spec-rows form the vertical spine of the comparison-table, stacked immediately below the row of product-column-headers. Each spec row spans the full width of the table, from the left spec-label column to the rightmost product column. The spec-label cell on the left occupies about 20–25% of the table width, with 12–16px padding and left-aligned text, while each corresponding product cell occupies an equal share of the remaining 75–80% of width. Rows are relatively tight, with a typical height of 40–56px, using alternating background treatments—e.g., one row a dark graphite gradient, the next a slightly lighter metallic tone—to create striping that helps users scan across. Thin 1px horizontal dividers separate rows, and subtle vertical separators delineate the product columns. Text within each cell is concise and left-aligned (or centered for numeric values), with occasional iconography or small badges to highlight key differences. The overall column alignment is strict, so each spec aligns perfectly across all products. The spec rows extend downward as far as needed; on long comparisons, the user scrolls through them while the top of the table (headers and possibly toolbar) remains visually fixed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the comparison-table’s scrollable area; behaves like a standard table body. Spec rows themselves do not become sticky; instead, they move under any sticky headers or toolbar as the user scrolls. In responsive scenarios, if horizontal space becomes constrained, spec row cells may reflow to show fewer products side-by-side, but within the fixed-width desktop layout they remain fully visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-replace-form appears inline within a product-column-header, emerging as a slide-down panel that occupies the full width of that specific product column while sharing its glossy aesthetic. When activated (e.g., via a "Change" link or button in the header), the form expands downward from the bottom edge of the header’s main content area, pushing the spec-rows for that column (and the table grid for that row) slightly down to maintain alignment. Vertically, the panel is about 120–160px tall, depending on content, with 12–16px padding. It features a darker interior gradient (e.g., dark green to black) to distinguish it as a secondary surface beneath the main header. Inside, a compact text field or dropdown is left-aligned, with a small product thumbnail list or suggestions aligned in a single column. The right side of the panel houses glossy action buttons (e.g., "Apply" and "Cancel"), aligned horizontally. The panel’s left and right edges line up perfectly with the product column’s boundaries, preserving the tight grid. When inactive, this form is completely collapsed, freeing the vertical space so that the headers and spec rows touch directly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands and collapses within the vertical flow of the specific product column while keeping the overall table grid coherent. It pushes content below it down when opened instead of overlaying it, ensuring spec rows remain aligned. Scrolling behavior is inherited from the table; if the user scrolls while the form is open, it scrolls away along with its column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-product-action is a compact control embedded within each product-column-header, positioned at the top-right corner of the header card. It appears as a small, circular or square glossy button with a high-contrast icon (e.g., an "X" or trash symbol), sized around 24–32px, with 8px inset from the header’s right and top edges to avoid touching the border. It subtly overlaps the product image or header background, maintaining the energetic, layered look of the mockup. Its placement allows users to quickly remove a product without searching; visually, it sits above the main text and image, almost like a badge. If the user removes the product, the entire column (header plus associated spec cells) collapses and the remaining product columns expand proportionally to fill the available right-hand table width, while the left spec-label column remains fixed.
