component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in position relative to its product header; it does not scroll independently. When triggered, it triggers layout reflow of the comparison-table by removing a column and resizing the remaining columns to maintain equal distribution.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The differences-toggle sits inside the comparison-toolbar at the far right, aligned vertically to the center of the toolbar strip. It occupies approximately 20–30% of the toolbar width, appearing as a pill-shaped, glossy toggle control with a saturated accent gradient (e.g., neon yellow to green) to distinguish it from the darker toolbar background. The toggle is right-aligned with about 16–24px right padding, mirroring the left padding of the session controls on the opposite side. It includes a brief, uppercase label (e.g., "SHOW DIFFERENCES ONLY") and a switch indicator or two-state button grouped tightly in a horizontal unit. When activated, the pill glows more intensely, and non-differing spec rows in the comparison-table fade or collapse, visually tightening the table below. Its position at the top-right of the control band makes it a natural endpoint of the user’s initial left-to-right scan across the toolbar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the comparison-toolbar. If the toolbar is configured as sticky, the differences-toggle remains visible at the top of the viewport while the user scrolls through the comparison-table, allowing rapid toggling without scrolling back up.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-empty-state occupies the same spatial region as the comparison-table within the main left column, appearing when there are no products in the current comparison. It is centered within that area both horizontally and vertically above the fold, framed by a glossy, card-like container that matches the width of the table (full width of the main column, about 70% of page width). The card’s height is roughly 240–320px, creating a hero-like panel that feels important but not as tall as the main hero banner. The background uses a rich gradient (e.g., dark grey fading to deep green) with a large, semi-transparent icon or subtle product silhouette aligned to the right, suggesting the car audio theme. On the left side of the card, a bold heading such as "No Products to Compare" appears in large, uppercase text, followed by a 1–2 line explanation and a prominent, glossy call-to-action button (e.g., "Add Products to Start Comparing") aligned below it. The card sits directly beneath the comparison-toolbar, with a small 8–12px gap, preserving the familiar banded layout even in the empty state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the comparison-table entirely in the layout; when the empty state is active, the table and its internal components are not rendered. It remains static within the scrolling page; as the user scrolls, it moves like any other card within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-loading-state appears in place of the comparison-table whenever data is being fetched or updated, occupying the same width and approximate height as the visible portion of the table area directly under the comparison-toolbar. Instead of spec rows and headers, the user sees a centered loading indicator on a dark, glossy panel. The panel background uses a subdued gradient (e.g., dark charcoal with a faint radial glow in the center) to suggest depth while keeping focus on the loading indicator. The spinner or progress animation is centered horizontally in the main column and positioned slightly above the vertical center of the panel, with a short, uppercase status label (e.g., "LOADING COMPARISON...") beneath it. The panel edges align with the table’s typical border, maintaining consistency with the rest of the page’s card treatments. If the loading content is very tall, the panel extends vertically but keeps the loader anchored near the viewport center where possible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the comparison-table region within the normal flow. It scrolls with the page but is typically short enough that the loader appears above the fold. Once loading completes, it is removed and replaced with the full comparison-table (or error/empty state, as appropriate).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-error-state manifests as a bold, high-contrast alert band positioned directly above the comparison-table region, immediately below the comparison-toolbar. It spans the full width of the main left column (about 70% of the page) and is approximately 64–80px tall. The background uses a saturated error gradient (e.g., dark red to black) with a bright top border line, matching the beveled look of the other bands. Inside, a warning icon is aligned to the left with 16px padding, followed by a clear error message and a brief hint or retry link. Text is left-aligned and in uppercase for the main error title, with a smaller sentence or action link on the second line. Below this band, either the comparison-table remains visible in its last known state (dimmed slightly) or, if no data is available, the table area is replaced by a smaller, centered card restating the error. The overall intent is to clearly signal the problem without breaking the banded vertical structure of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits inline in the normal vertical flow and pushes the comparison-table or alternative states downward when present. It remains static as the user scrolls down through any remaining visible content; if the user scrolls back up, the error band reappears just under the toolbar, maintaining its contextual relationship to the comparison controls.

name: Cart
title: Shopping Cart
description: Page for viewing, updating, and managing items currently in the cart before proceeding to checkout.

core_features: Load current cart contents using getCartSummary and display all cart items with associated product and optional protection plan details.
Allow users to change the quantity for each cart item, calling updateCartItemQuantity and refreshing the cart summary from the response.
Allow users to remove individual items from the cart using removeCartItem and update the cart view with the returned cart and items.

Display an order summary including cart subtotal from the Cart model and, if available from the response or server logic, an estimated shipping indicator and overall total or guidance that shipping will be calculated at checkout.

Provide navigation controls to proceed to checkout (checkout.html), continue shopping in the Car Audio category (category.html?categoryId=car_audio), and open product detail pages from cart items (product.html?id=productId&ref=cart).
Handle empty cart state by indicating there are no items and emphasizing the Continue Shopping action.
Handle loading and error states for all cart operations (initial load, quantity update, item removal) without using static or hardcoded data values.
user_workflows:
View and review cart contents:
1) User navigates to cart.html (via header Cart icon/link or direct URL).
2) On page load, the cart-page-controller component calls getCartSummary with no parameters.
3) The response provides a Cart object and an items array of {cartItem, product, protectionPlan} objects.
4) The cart-item-list component renders one cart-item-row per item, showing product details, quantity, unit price, and lineSubtotal from cartItem.
5) The cart-totals-panel component binds to cart.subtotal and any additional total-related fields available from the getCartSummary response or backend-calculated values.
6) User can visually confirm items, quantities, and order summary before deciding next actions.

Change item quantity in the cart:
1) User locates an item in the cart-item-list and interacts with the quantity-selector control within a specific cart-item-row.
2) User changes the quantity value (e.g., via input field, stepper, or dropdown), triggering the quantity-selector onChange event.
3) The quantity-selector component validates the new quantity (e.g., positive integer) and calls updateCartItemQuantity with parameters {cartItemId, quantity} derived from the bound cartItem.
4) While waiting for the response, the loading-error-handler component can show a per-item loading state.
5) On success, the cart-page-controller receives the updated Cart and items array from updateCartItemQuantity and updates the shared cart state.
6) The UI re-renders: cart-item-row shows updated quantity and lineSubtotal; cart-totals-panel shows updated subtotal and any totals.
7) On error, the loading-error-handler displays an error message and reverts the visible quantity to the last confirmed value from the server response.

Remove an item from the cart:
1) User clicks the remove-item-control for a given cart-item-row.
2) The remove-item-control triggers onClick, optionally shows a confirmation (handled in the component), and then calls removeCartItem with parameter {cartItemId}.
3) While waiting, the loading-error-handler can show a per-item loading state or disable interactions for that row.
4) On success, the cart-page-controller replaces the current cart and items with the response from removeCartItem.
5) The cart-item-list re-renders without the removed item; cart-totals-panel reflects updated subtotal and any totals; header cart indicators (if bound to the same state) will also update.
6) If the last item was removed and the items array becomes empty, the empty-cart-message component is shown instead of the cart-item-list.
7) On error, the error state is shown and the removed item remains visible with its previous data.

Proceed to checkout:
1) After reviewing the cart, the user clicks the proceed-to-checkout-button within the navigation-actions component.
2) The button’s onClick handler may optionally ensure the latest cart state is already confirmed (no additional interface call is required if state is current).
3) The page navigates to checkout.html with no additional URL parameters, relying on the current open Cart in the backend session or context.
4) Checkout flow continues on checkout.html using its own interfaces.

Continue shopping in Car Audio:
1) User decides to add more items instead of checking out and clicks the continue-shopping-link within the navigation-actions component.
2) The onClick handler triggers navigation to category.html with a URL parameter categoryId=car_audio.
3) The user browses products in the Car Audio category and can later return to cart.html via the header Cart icon/link.

Open product details from the cart:
1) Within a cart-item-row, the user clicks the product image or product name.
2) The onClick handler reads the bound product.id and navigates to product.html with parameters id=product.id and ref=cart.
3) On product.html, the ref=cart parameter can be used by that page (outside this specification) to adjust breadcrumbs or back-navigation, while the user reviews product details.
4) The user can then use site navigation to return to the cart or keep shopping.
interactions: Page load (cart-page-controller onLoad): invoke getCartSummary, manage loading state, bind the returned Cart and items to all child components, and handle any errors in loading-error-handler.

Quantity change (quantity-selector onChange or onBlur): validate the new quantity, call updateCartItemQuantity with {cartItemId, quantity}, update shared cart state with the response, re-render cart-item-list and cart-totals-panel, and display errors if the update fails.

Remove item (remove-item-control onClick): optionally confirm removal, call removeCartItem with {cartItemId}, update shared cart state with the response, and show empty-cart-message if the items array becomes empty after removal.

Proceed to checkout (proceed-to-checkout-button onClick): verify there is at least one cart item in the current state; if items array is non-empty, navigate to checkout.html; if empty, optionally prevent navigation and show a message in loading-error-handler or near navigation-actions.
Continue shopping (continue-shopping-link onClick): navigate to category.html?categoryId=car_audio regardless of cart contents, allowing users to add more items from the Car Audio department.
View product details (cart-item-row onProductClick): use the bound product.id to navigate to product.html?id=product.id&ref=cart when the user clicks the product image or product name.

Handle empty cart state (empty-cart-message): when getCartSummary or subsequent updates return an items array with length 0, hide cart-item-list and cart-totals-panel, show an empty state message, and emphasize the continue-shopping-link.

Error handling and recovery (loading-error-handler): on any failed interface call (getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem), display a descriptive error message, allow retry of the failed action (re-invoking the same interface), and ensure the UI reflects the last known good state from the server.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The cart.html page does not require any specific incoming URL parameters for its core cart functionality; it always operates on the current open Cart associated with the user context on the server. All cart content and totals are derived exclusively from the getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, and removeCartItem interfaces.

Optional URL parameters may be read for UI-only behavior, but not for data loading or mutation. Examples include:
- ref: cart.html?ref=header or cart.html?ref=miniCart – can be used purely for presentation, such as adjusting breadcrumbs or tracking entry point, without changing which Cart is loaded.

Navigation from this page uses URL parameters as follows:
- When the user clicks a product name or image in a cart-item-row, the page navigates to product.html?id={product.id}&ref=cart. The id parameter identifies the product to show; ref=cart indicates that the user came from the cart.
- When the user clicks the Continue Shopping link, the page navigates to category.html?categoryId=car_audio. The categoryId parameter selects the Car Audio category; the value is interpreted by the category.html page using the Category.code field.
- When the user clicks Proceed to Checkout, the page navigates to checkout.html without additional URL parameters; the backend associates the current open Cart with the checkout session.

Page state (such as selected quantities, cart item list, and totals) is always synchronized with the latest server response from the assigned interfaces and is not persisted via localStorage or other browser storage. Any transient UI states (loading, errors, and per-item highlighting after updates) are held only in in-memory component state for the duration of the page session and are reset on full page reloads.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and state management for the cart page; invokes getCartSummary on load and updates shared cart state after any cart mutations.
event_handlers: onLoad (calls getCartSummary with no parameters; sets loading state; on success stores returned cart and items; on error informs loading-error-handler)

onCartUpdated (central handler invoked after updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem; replaces current cart and items with values from the response, ensuring all child components re-bind to the latest state)
onReloadRequested (re-invokes getCartSummary when user chooses to retry after a load error)

components.functionality: Displays the list of items currently in the cart; renders a cart-item-row instance for each element in the items array from the cart-page-controller.
event_handlers: onItemsChange (triggered when the shared items array from cart-page-controller changes; re-renders the list)
onEmptyCheck (evaluates whether items array length is 0; if so, hides itself and allows empty-cart-message to show)

components.functionality: Represents a single cart line, showing product information, optional protection plan, quantity controls, unit price, and lineSubtotal; exposes controls for updating quantity and removing the item.
event_handlers: onProductClick (navigates to product.html?id=product.id&ref=cart when user clicks product image or product name bound from Product)
onQuantityChange (receives new quantity from quantity-selector, validates it, and delegates to cart-page-controller to call updateCartItemQuantity with {cartItemId: cartItem.id, quantity})
onRemoveClick (invoked by remove-item-control; delegates to cart-page-controller to call removeCartItem with {cartItemId: cartItem.id})

components.functionality: Allows the user to change the quantity for a specific cart item and triggers quantity updates via the updateCartItemQuantity interface.
event_handlers: onChange (when the user changes the quantity value; checks that the value is a valid positive integer; if valid, calls updateCartItemQuantity with parameters {cartItemId: cartItem.id, quantity}; if invalid, displays validation feedback via loading-error-handler or inline messaging)
onBlur (optional: confirms final value and, if changed since last saved state, triggers the same logic as onChange)
onUpdateSuccess (receives updated cart and items from cart-page-controller; ensures displayed quantity is synchronized with the latest cartItem.quantity from the response)
onUpdateError (reverts visual quantity to last known good value from the server and triggers error display in loading-error-handler)

components.functionality: Provides a control to remove a cart item and triggers the removeCartItem interface.
event_handlers: onClick (optionally asks for user confirmation, then calls removeCartItem with parameter {cartItemId: cartItem.id} via cart-page-controller; sets a loading state for this row until the response returns)
onRemoveSuccess (after cart-page-controller processes the removeCartItem response, ensures this cart-item-row is not rendered if the item no longer exists in the items array)
onRemoveError (clears the per-item loading state and triggers an error message via loading-error-handler without removing the item visually)

components.functionality: Displays the cart’s monetary summary, including subtotal and, if available from server-side calculations, additional totals such as shipping estimates or overall total; otherwise, indicates that shipping and final total will be calculated at checkout.
event_handlers: onCartChange (whenever the Cart object from cart-page-controller is updated, re-read and display subtotal and any available total-related fields)
onItemsChange (optional; used if any derived totals depend on items array structure rather than Cart fields; re-renders when items array changes)
onEmptyCart (if items array becomes empty, hides the totals panel to avoid showing a non-relevant summary)

components.functionality: Provides primary actions below the cart summary: proceeding to checkout and continuing shopping in the Car Audio category.
event_handlers: onProceedToCheckoutClick (checks if items array from cart-page-controller is non-empty; if there is at least one item, navigates to checkout.html; if empty, can trigger an informational message via loading-error-handler and block navigation)
onContinueShoppingClick (navigates to category.html?categoryId=car_audio, using the Car Audio category code as URL parameter; does not modify the cart)
onCartChange (enables or disables the proceed-to-checkout-button based on whether there are any items in the cart)

components.functionality: Shows an empty-cart state when there are no items, encouraging the user to continue shopping in the Car Audio category.
event_handlers: onItemsChange (if the items array from cart-page-controller has length 0, display this component; if it has items, hide this component)
onContinueShoppingClick (delegates to navigation-actions to perform navigation to category.html?categoryId=car_audio)

components.functionality: Manages and displays loading and error states for all cart operations: initial load, quantity updates, and item removal.
event_handlers: onLoadStart (triggered by cart-page-controller before calling getCartSummary; shows page-level loading indicator)
onLoadSuccess (triggered after successful getCartSummary; hides loading indicator and clears any previous errors)
onLoadError (triggered if getCartSummary fails; displays a page-level error message and offers a retry action that calls cart-page-controller.onReloadRequested)
onMutationStart (invoked before updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem; can show per-item loading state or disable relevant controls)
onMutationSuccess (invoked after successful mutation; clears relevant loading states and errors)

onMutationError (invoked if updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem fails; shows an appropriate error message, clears loading states, and signals affected components—such as quantity-selector or remove-item-control—to revert to last known good state from the server)

components.functionality: Represents the Cart icon/link in the global header which routes users to cart.html; keeps its item count synchronized with the current Cart state when available on this page.
event_handlers: onClick (navigates to cart.html when clicked from any page; when already on cart.html, may simply scroll to the main cart area without triggering new data loads)
onCartChange (updates any displayed item count or summary text based on Cart.itemsCount or derived items array length from cart-page-controller)

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: The cart page sits within the same fixed-width, center-aligned structure as the mockup. Above, the existing full-width header band and navigation bar remain unchanged. Directly beneath the navigation, a bold, horizontally spanning hero banner for Your Cart echoes the driving/energy imagery but is slightly shallower than the home hero. Below this hero, the cart content is organized into a two-column layout: a wider left column (around 70% of the content width) dedicated to the cart-item-list, and a narrower right column (around 30%) dedicated to the cart-totals-panel. Navigation-actions form a horizontal band near the bottom of the cart content, visually tying both columns together. Empty and loading/error states temporarily replace or overlay these main sections while respecting the same structural bands.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The cart page sits within the same fixed-width, center-aligned structure as the mockup. Above, the existing full-width header band and navigation bar remain unchanged. Directly beneath the navigation, a bold, horizontally spanning hero banner for Your Cart echoes the driving/energy imagery but is slightly shallower than the home hero. Below this hero, the cart content is organized into a two-column layout: a wider left column (around 70% of the content width) dedicated to the cart-item-list, and a narrower right column (around 30%) dedicated to the cart-totals-panel. Navigation-actions form a horizontal band near the bottom of the cart content, visually tying both columns together. Empty and loading/error states temporarily replace or overlay these main sections while respecting the same structural bands.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Within the viewport, the header and navigation occupy the top 15–20%, followed by a Your Cart hero banner consuming roughly 30–35% of the initial viewport height (slightly shorter than the home hero but still dominant). The remaining space is given to the two-column cart content layer. Horizontally, the center container is approximately 960–1024px wide, with the left column around 70% of that width for the cart-item-list and the right column around 30% for the cart-totals-panel. Vertical spacing between major bands (hero to cart content, cart content to footer) uses 24–32px gaps, suggested more by gradient shifts and subtle rules than by open whitespace. Individual cards and rows use 12–16px internal padding, with 16–24px applied to the hero banner and navigation-actions band for emphasis.
layout_strategies.content_density: The cart page maintains a moderate-to-high content density consistent with the mockup. The cart-item-list card, totals panel, and navigation actions all use glossy gradients and bordered modules, so very little of the center container is truly blank. Text content is concise (item names, prices, labels), with emphasis placed on product thumbnails, glossy buttons, and strong headings in the totals panel and action bar. Rows in the cart list are compact, with tight vertical spacing (8–12px gaps) between rows, but clear separation via alternating gradients or subtle borders. The overall feel is busy and energetic rather than minimalist, yet controlled through a regular grid and consistent padding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left logo in the header, sweeps right across the social icons and the mini-cart-header-link, then across the main navigation bar. From there it drops into the Your Cart hero band, where a bold typography treatment and motion-oriented background keep attention. The flow then moves down into the cart content: first to the prominent left-side cart-item-list where large product thumbnails act as anchors, then across to the right to review the cart-totals-panel. After scanning items and totals, the gaze is drawn downward to the navigation-actions band, where the primary Proceed to Checkout button sits on the right, balanced by a secondary Continue Shopping action on the left. Error or loading overlays draw temporary focus centrally without disrupting this underlying Z-like pattern.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: The cart page lives inside a full-width, high-contrast background gradient that frames a fixed-width, center-aligned content strip. At the top, the familiar glossy header band with logo, social icons, and the mini-cart link leads into a saturated navigation bar. Just below, a Your Cart hero banner stretches edge to edge within the main container, featuring a dynamic, road-like background and bold uppercase title on the left, with a short supporting line and maybe a small product or cart icon on the right. Beneath this, the page divides into two columns: the left, wider column contains a glossy, bordered card with the cart-item-list, where each row showcases a product thumbnail, name, and compact controls; the right, narrower column holds a stacked, high-contrast cart-totals-panel that resembles the marketing-heavy sidebar cards from the mockup. The background gradient subtly shifts behind these modules, giving the impression of stacked horizontal bands. At the bottom of the cart content, a full-width (within the container) navigation-actions band visually unifies both columns with a strong horizontal stripe and prominent checkout button. Error and loading states appear as overlays or bold banners within this same structure, preserving the clear vertical rhythm of header, hero, content, and footer.

layout.overall_description: The cart page adopts the mockup’s glossy, banded structure: a full-width colored background with a center-aligned, fixed-width content column. The existing header and navigation sit at the top as metallic, gradient bars, with the mini-cart link tucked into the upper-right area, visually near social icons. Just below, a Your Cart hero banner spans the content width, featuring motion-themed imagery (like a stylized highway or speed lines), a large uppercase title on the left, and a concise subtitle or reassurance message (secure checkout, easy returns) on the right. As you scroll downward, the layout steps into a two-column region framed by subtle gradients: the left 70% hosts a glossy cart-item-list card, where product rows are stacked tightly but clearly separated; the right 30% is occupied by a tall, eye-catching cart-totals-panel, styled similarly to a promotional sidebar card. The right panel’s bright gradient and large total figure balance the visual weight of the image-heavy list on the left. Below both columns, a horizontal navigation-actions band stretches across the container, echoing the nav bar’s treatment and housing the primary checkout button on the right and secondary actions on the left. Throughout, gradients, bevels, and concise labels maintain a high-energy, marketing-centric aesthetic while preserving a predictable, top-to-bottom flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-page-controller defines the main cart content region between the page’s navigation bar and the footer. It is centered horizontally within the full browser window, constrained to a fixed-width container of roughly 960–1024px. Vertically, it begins about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the Your Cart hero banner, creating a clear step down communicated by a slight gradient shift. Inside this controller, the content is split into two columns: a left main column occupying about 70% of the container width and a right sidebar column using the remaining 30%. Both columns align to a shared top baseline so that the top edge of the cart-item-list card on the left lines up with the top edge of the cart-totals-panel card on the right. The controller manages vertical rhythm, keeping 24–32px spacing between the hero, the top of the cart cards, and the navigation-actions band below, with inner margins of 16px around the columns. The outer background beyond the container shows continuous gradients rather than empty white space, extending the glossy, energetic feel to the page edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, center-aligned fixed-width container anchored in the page’s main content area; it scrolls normally with the page while maintaining the two-column grid inside.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-list sits in the left column defined by the cart-page-controller, occupying about 70% of the content width. It is contained within a glossy, beveled card that spans from the column’s left edge to its internal right gutter, with 16px padding on all sides. The card’s top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the cart-totals-panel on the right, creating a clean visual row. A bold cart summary header strip at the top of the card (e.g., Cart Items, with item count) uses a slightly darker gradient band 48–56px tall, with the title left-aligned and the item count right-aligned, mimicking the strong navigation/tab treatments from the mockup. Below this header, cart-item-row components are stacked vertically with 8–12px gaps or subtle separators between them, filling most of the vertical space above the navigation-actions band. The overall height of the list can extend beyond the initial viewport, encouraging scrolling, while the width remains fixed within the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls within the page as content grows, maintaining its position in the left column; the card itself is not sticky and expands vertically to fit the number of cart-item-row entries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart-item-row is a horizontally oriented strip within the cart-item-list card, spanning nearly the full width of the left column minus the card’s padding (roughly 100% of the card’s inner width). The row height is approximately 80–120px depending on image size. On the far left (about 20–25% of the row width), a product thumbnail image sits with a subtle inner shadow and glossy border, vertically centered within the row. To the immediate right of the image (around 40–45% of the width), product name, brief description, and key attributes are stacked in a compact vertical block, left-aligned, with 4–8px spacing between text lines. To the right of the details block, near the middle-right (about 15–20% of the width), the quantity-selector is placed, aligned vertically with the row center and horizontally aligned in a column with other rows so controls form a neat vertical line. To the immediate right of the quantity selector, the per-item price and line total are right-aligned in a narrow vertical stack (about 10–15% of row width). Finally, on the far right edge, the remove-item-control is placed as a small but visually clear icon or pill-shaped button, vertically centered and separated by 8px from the row’s right margin. Rows are separated with a subtle bottom border or alternating gradient bands to echo the mockup’s busy but structured aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands vertically just enough to contain its content and remains anchored within the cart-item-list’s vertical stack; maintains a consistent horizontal layout across different viewport heights due to the fixed-width grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity-selector appears inside each cart-item-row, positioned in the middle-right segment of the row. It is a compact, glossy control (such as a stepper with minus and plus buttons or a dropdown field) occupying roughly 15–20% of the row width. The control is vertically centered within the row, aligned so that all quantity selectors across rows form a clean vertical column. It sits immediately to the right of the product details block and just to the left of the price/totals stack, with about 8px of horizontal padding on each side. The selector’s height matches or slightly exceeds the line height of the main body text to make it tap/click-friendly, with 8–12px inner padding and a saturated green or metallic gradient to stand out against the darker row background. Its visual treatment echoes the glossy button style from the navigation and hero call-to-action elements, making it feel interactive and robust.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each cart-item-row, maintaining its relative position between product details and price; its width remains fixed so column alignment is preserved across all rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-item-control is located at the far right end of each cart-item-row, occupying a narrow vertical strip (around 5–10% of the row width). It appears as either a compact pill-shaped button or an icon button (e.g., an X or trash can) with a bright, high-contrast color (such as vivid red or yellow) to clearly indicate its destructive function. The control is vertically centered in the row and separated from the row’s right border by about 8px of padding. Its size is large enough to be easily clickable but visually subordinate to the price information; in other words, it is clearly visible but not the most prominent element in the row. The button follows the same glossy, beveled style as other action buttons on the site, with a subtle outer glow on hover to maintain the early-2010s marketing aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each row, anchored to the row’s right edge; it moves up and down as the list scrolls but does not float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-totals-panel sits in the right-hand sidebar column established by the cart-page-controller, occupying the full width of that column (about 30% of the container width). Its top edge aligns with the top of the cart-item-list card to form a unified horizontal baseline. The panel is shaped as a tall, glossy card with a vibrant gradient background (e.g., saturated green-to-yellow) that immediately draws the eye. Inside, 16–20px padding on all sides creates a compact but readable layout. At the top, a bold heading such as Order Summary or Cart Total is left-aligned, with a subtle metallic underline or band. Below, price breakdown lines (subtotal, taxes, shipping) are stacked with 8px vertical spacing, left-aligned labels, and right-aligned values. The grand total is emphasized in a slightly larger, brighter type and perhaps separated by a thin rule and 12–16px gap from the subtotals. At the bottom of the panel, a secondary Proceed to Checkout button may mirror the main navigation-actions CTA, or a note about secure payment appears. The panel extends vertically but tends to remain shorter than the full height of a long cart-item-list, so it feels like a dense summary block rather than a column of text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned within the right column and generally kept near the top of the viewport; optionally, it can become sticky within the container so that as the user scrolls through a long cart-item-list on the left, the totals panel remains visible until the bottom of the cart-page-controller is reached.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-actions component forms a horizontal band located directly below the two-column cart content within the cart-page-controller. It spans the full width of the internal container (matching the width of both the main and sidebar columns combined) and sits about 24px beneath the bottom edge of the cart-item-list card and cart-totals-panel, creating a visually distinct step down. This band is 56–72px tall and uses a strong horizontal gradient reminiscent of the main navigation bar: darker at the top, lighter at the bottom, with a subtle inner glow. Inside, actions are arranged on a left-right axis: a secondary Continue Shopping link-style button appears on the left side, aligned with the left column edge, while the primary Proceed to Checkout button is right-aligned, directly under or slightly offset from the cart-totals-panel to reinforce the association between totals and progression. Both buttons are glossy, with the primary button in a saturated green or yellow to stand out. The band visually anchors the bottom of the cart content and signals the main decision point for the user.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static band anchored at the bottom of the cart-page-controller; it scrolls with the page rather than sticking to the viewport, maintaining its position as the final step in the cart’s vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-cart-message appears when there are no items in the cart and replaces the usual cart-item-list and cart-totals-panel content within the cart-page-controller. In this state, a single, glossy message card is centered horizontally in the main content area, spanning roughly 60–70% of the container width (bridging where the left and right columns would normally be) and sitting about 24–32px below the Your Cart hero banner. The card has 24px padding and a light, uplifting gradient to distinguish it from the denser content states. Inside, a bold headline such as Your cart is empty is centered near the top, followed by a shorter explanatory sentence and a strong call-to-action button (e.g., Browse Products) centered beneath. The vertical spacing inside the card follows the 16–24px scale to keep the content focused yet energetic. The surrounding background still displays the site’s characteristic gradients, but the empty state card becomes the primary focal point due to its central position and consolidated layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered in place of the two-column layout; it remains static within the page flow and scrolls normally, with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-error-handler manages transient states over the cart-page-controller. During loading, it presents a semi-transparent dark overlay that covers the entire cart-page-controller area, including where the cart-item-list and cart-totals-panel appear. A centered, glossy loading indicator (spinner or pulsing cart icon) sits in the middle of this overlay, slightly above the vertical center to avoid overlapping the navigation-actions band if present. The loader uses a bright accent color and soft glow to stand out against the dimmed background. For errors, a high-contrast alert band appears at the top of the cart-page-controller, just below the Your Cart hero banner, spanning the full width of the content container. This band is 40–56px tall, with a strong red or amber gradient and a left-aligned error icon and message text, plus a right-aligned retry or dismiss action. The underlying cart layout remains visible but is visually secondary while the error band or loading overlay is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as an overlay or top-inset band over the cart-page-controller; the overlay is positioned absolutely over the cart region while active, and the error band is fixed within the controller’s top area, both scrolling away only when dismissed or when loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mini-cart-header-link resides in the global header band, not within the main cart-page-controller. It is positioned in the top-right quadrant of the header, horizontally aligned with the social icons and any account or search links. The link includes a small cart icon followed by a label (e.g., Cart (2)), rendered in a bright accent color that contrasts against the darker, metallic header gradient. It sits on the same baseline as the other header elements, about 8–12px from the header’s top edge and with 16px horizontal padding on each side to form a compact clickable area. On the cart page itself, the link remains visible but acts more as a status indicator than a primary control, reinforcing the current context. Its glossy treatment and subtle glow on hover echo the early-2010s aesthetic of the navigation bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in position within the global header; it remains at the top of the viewport as the user scrolls the page content, just like the other header elements.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Single-page checkout for capturing contact details, selecting a shipping method, reviewing the order, and confirming the purchase for the current cart.

core_features: Initialize or resume a checkout session for the current open cart using createOrGetCheckoutSession and load full checkout details (cart, items, available shipping methods) via getCheckoutSessionDetails.
Display and edit checkout contact information (email and full name), persisting changes to the current checkout session using updateCheckoutContactInfo.

List all available shipping methods for the checkout session (including any default option) using shippingMethods from getCheckoutSessionDetails, with optional fallback to getShippingMethods, and persist the user's selected method via updateCheckoutShippingMethod.

Show an up-to-date order summary including cart line items, quantities, unit prices, line subtotals, cart subtotal, selected shipping cost, and a computed order total using cart and items from getCheckoutSessionDetails.
Validate that required checkout fields (contact email, full name, shipping method) are present before allowing order placement.
Place/complete the order by calling completeCheckout, then show a success state with the returned orderReference and optionally redirect the user to the homepage (index.html).
Provide a persistent "Back to Cart" navigation link to cart.html so users can modify cart contents before completing checkout.
Handle and surface error states (e.g., missing cart, invalid checkout session, failed API calls) in a functional way, suggesting navigation back to cart.html or index.html as appropriate.
user_workflows:
1. Entering Checkout from Cart:
   1.1 User clicks "Proceed to Checkout" on cart.html, which navigates to checkout.html (optionally with a URL parameter like ?step=contact).
   1.2 On page load, the checkout-initializer component calls createOrGetCheckoutSession.
   1.3 After receiving a CheckoutSession, checkout-initializer immediately calls getCheckoutSessionDetails.
   1.4 The page renders contact-info-form (pre-filled if contactEmail/contactFullName exist), shipping-method-selector (pre-selected if shippingMethodId exists), and order-summary-panel based on the returned data.

2. Updating Contact Information:
   2.1 User enters or edits their email and full name in contact-info-form.
   2.2 When the user submits the contact form (e.g., clicking a "Save and Continue" action), contact-info-form validates the inputs (non-empty full name, syntactically valid email format) on the client.
   2.3 If valid, contact-info-form calls updateCheckoutContactInfo with contactEmail and contactFullName.
   2.4 On success, the returned CheckoutSession is stored in the checkout-initializer state and propagated to dependent components.
   2.5 The page updates the URL parameter step=shipping to reflect progression in the checkout flow.

3. Selecting a Shipping Method:
   3.1 shipping-method-selector reads shippingMethods from getCheckoutSessionDetails.
   3.2 If checkoutSession.shippingMethodId is already set, that shipping method is selected.
   3.3 If no shippingMethodId is set, shipping-method-selector selects the method where isDefault is true; if none, it selects the first available method.
   3.4 User can change the selection by clicking on different shipping options (e.g., radio inputs).
   3.5 On selection change, shipping-method-selector calls updateCheckoutShippingMethod with the chosen shippingMethodId.
   3.6 On success, the updated CheckoutSession is stored and order-summary-panel refreshes to reflect the current shipping cost in the order total.
   3.7 The page may update the URL parameter step=review to indicate that the user is now reviewing the order.

4. Reviewing Order Summary:
   4.1 order-summary-panel uses cart and items from getCheckoutSessionDetails to list each item, quantity, unitPrice, and lineSubtotal.
   4.2 It reads the selected shipping method (via checkoutSession.shippingMethodId and the shippingMethods array) to display shipping cost and any delivery estimate based on estimatedDaysMin/estimatedDaysMax.
   4.3 It computes a display-only order total as cart.subtotal plus the selected shippingMethod.cost.
   4.4 If cart.itemsCount is zero or the cart.status is not open, order-summary-panel shows an error state instructing the user to return to cart.html or index.html.

5. Placing/Completing the Order:
   5.1 The user clicks a “Place Order” button in the place-order-form component while on step=review.
   5.2 place-order-form validates that checkoutSession.contactEmail, checkoutSession.contactFullName, and checkoutSession.shippingMethodId are present; if any are missing, it highlights the relevant section and, if needed, updates the URL parameter step to focus that section.
   5.3 If validation passes, place-order-form temporarily disables the button and calls completeCheckout.
   5.4 On success=true, completeCheckout returns an updated CheckoutSession (with status typically set to completed), an orderReference, and a message.
   5.5 checkout-success-state is activated: it displays confirmation using orderReference and the message (if provided), and checkout-initializer updates the URL to include status=success and orderRef=<orderReference>.
   5.6 Optionally, after showing the success state, the page may redirect the user to index.html (optionally with orderRef=<orderReference> in the URL) when the user clicks a “Continue Shopping” button or after a short, configurable delay.
   5.7 After completion, attempting to interact with contact-info-form or shipping-method-selector either does nothing or shows a non-editable state, since the checkoutSession is no longer in progress.

6. Returning to Cart to Make Changes:
   6.1 At any time before placing the order, the user can click the "Back to Cart" link in back-to-cart-link.
   6.2 The page navigates to cart.html, optionally including a URL parameter (e.g., ?from=checkout) for analytics or UI messaging on the cart page.
   6.3 The existing checkoutSession remains server-side. If the user returns to checkout.html later, createOrGetCheckoutSession will resume the in-progress session for the current open cart.

7. Direct Return to an Existing Checkout Session:
   7.1 User revisits checkout.html directly (e.g., via browser history or bookmark).
   7.2 On load, checkout-initializer calls createOrGetCheckoutSession; if an in-progress CheckoutSession exists for the current cart context, the API returns it instead of creating a new one.
   7.3 checkout-initializer then invokes getCheckoutSessionDetails to repopulate the UI with persisted contact information, shipping method choice, and cart contents.
   7.4 The page reads any existing URL parameter step to focus the appropriate logical section (contact, shipping, or review).
interactions: On page load, the checkout-initializer component triggers a data load sequence: createOrGetCheckoutSession followed by getCheckoutSessionDetails, then renders child components with the returned data.
Typing into contact-info-form fields (email and full name) updates local component state but does not immediately call any API until the user submits the form.

Submitting contact-info-form invokes updateCheckoutContactInfo with the current email and full name; on success, it updates the global checkout session state and moves the logical step forward (e.g., updates URL to step=shipping).

Selecting a shipping option in shipping-method-selector (e.g., changing a radio button) invokes updateCheckoutShippingMethod with the associated shippingMethodId and updates the order summary on success.

Clicking the “Place Order” button in place-order-form triggers client-side validation and, if valid, calls completeCheckout; the button is disabled while the API call is pending to prevent duplicate submissions.

On successful completeCheckout, checkout-success-state displays confirmation using orderReference and message from the response, updates the URL (status=success&orderRef=...), and optionally enables a user-initiated redirect to index.html.
Clicking the "Back to Cart" link in back-to-cart-link navigates directly to cart.html without calling any checkout-related API.

If any API call fails (createOrGetCheckoutSession, getCheckoutSessionDetails, updateCheckoutContactInfo, updateCheckoutShippingMethod, or completeCheckout), checkout-error-banner shows a functional error message, and may provide retry actions (e.g., reload data) or navigation options to cart.html or index.html.

If the URL parameter step changes (e.g., via internal navigation or browser history), checkout-initializer updates which checkout sections are emphasized/expanded, without re-creating the checkout session unless a full page reload occurs.
page_functionality.state_logic: The checkout page state is driven by API responses and several URL parameters, without reliance on localStorage. The key URL parameter is `step`, which controls which part of the checkout flow is logically active. Accepted values include `contact`, `shipping`, and `review`. On initial load, if `step` is absent, the page defaults to `contact`. When the user successfully saves their contact information, the page updates the URL to `checkout.html?step=shipping` (preserving any additional parameters). After a shipping method is successfully saved, the URL is updated to `checkout.html?step=review`. The `status` URL parameter indicates overall checkout state. During an active (in-progress) session, `status` is typically absent or set to a non-success value. After successful completion via completeCheckout, the page sets `status=success` and adds `orderRef=<orderReference>` from the completeCheckout response, yielding a URL like `checkout.html?status=success&orderRef=<dynamic_value>`. The presence of `status=success` causes checkout-success-state to render the successful completion UI and disables further editing. The optional `step` parameter can still be present but is ignored for data modification once `status=success` is set. An optional `from` parameter may be appended when entering checkout (e.g., `?from=cart`) or when navigating back to cart.html (e.g., `cart.html?from=checkout`) to support contextual UI on those pages; this parameter does not affect API behavior. All interactions with checkout data use the assigned interfaces, which implicitly reference the current user's open cart and checkout session; the URL parameters control only the client-side flow/visibility and not which CheckoutSession entity is retrieved or modified.

components.functionality: Coordinates initialization of the checkout flow: creates or resumes the CheckoutSession, loads full checkout details (cart, items, and shipping methods), and manages high-level page state based on URL parameters such as step and status. It also propagates CheckoutSession updates from child components to all dependent UI sections.
event_handlers: onLoad -> call createOrGetCheckoutSession then getCheckoutSessionDetails, store the returned checkoutSession, cart, items, and shippingMethods in controller state, and initialize the active step from the URL parameter `step` or default to `contact`.

onUrlChange -> parse URL parameters (`step`, `status`, `orderRef`, `from`) and update the UI state (e.g., active section, success state) without reinitializing the CheckoutSession unless the page is fully reloaded.

onSessionUpdate -> when a child component receives an updated CheckoutSession from updateCheckoutContactInfo or updateCheckoutShippingMethod, merge and store that session and notify dependent components (e.g., shipping-method-selector, order-summary-panel, place-order-form).
onReloadRequest -> re-call getCheckoutSessionDetails (without recreate) to refresh cart/items/shippingMethods, for example when the user clicks a retry button after a transient error.

components.functionality: Collects and edits the user's checkout contact information (email and full name). Pre-fills fields from checkoutSession.contactEmail and checkoutSession.contactFullName when available, validates user input, and persists updates to the checkout session via updateCheckoutContactInfo.
event_handlers: onInit -> read checkoutSession.contactEmail and checkoutSession.contactFullName from checkout-initializer and populate the form fields.
onInputChange -> update local component state for email and full name as the user types, and clear any previous validation errors that are addressed.

onSubmit -> validate that email is syntactically valid and full name is non-empty; if valid, call updateCheckoutContactInfo with current contactEmail and contactFullName; on success, propagate the updated CheckoutSession to checkout-initializer and update the URL parameter `step=shipping` to advance the flow; on failure, show an inline error and keep the user on the contact step.
onValidationErrorClick -> when a user interacts with an error indicator, focus the corresponding field for quick correction (client-side only).

components.functionality: Displays all available shipping methods for the current checkout session and allows the user to select one. It determines the initial selection based on checkoutSession.shippingMethodId or the default shipping method (isDefault). It persists the user’s choice via updateCheckoutShippingMethod.
event_handlers: onInit -> read shippingMethods from checkout-initializer (from getCheckoutSessionDetails); determine the selected method from checkoutSession.shippingMethodId, or fallback to the method with isDefault=true, or the first method if none is default.

onSelectionChange -> when the user changes the selected shipping method, call updateCheckoutShippingMethod with the new shippingMethodId; on success, update the CheckoutSession in checkout-initializer and notify order-summary-panel so it can refresh shipping costs and totals.

onMethodsMissing -> if getCheckoutSessionDetails returns no shippingMethods, call getShippingMethods to retrieve them, then reapply initial selection logic and render them; if still none are available, display a functional error (via checkout-error-banner) and suggest returning to cart.html or index.html.
onAfterSave -> after a successful shipping method update, optionally update the URL parameter `step=review` to move the user into the review stage.

components.functionality: Shows the detailed order summary for the current checkout session: each cart item with product details and pricing, the cart subtotal, the selected shipping cost, and a computed total. It updates in response to cart or shipping method changes.
event_handlers: onInit -> read cart and items (each containing cartItem, product, and optional protectionPlan) from checkout-initializer and render line items and cart subtotal.

onShippingMethodChange -> when notified by checkout-initializer that checkoutSession.shippingMethodId has changed, look up the matching ShippingMethod from shippingMethods and recompute the displayed order total as cart.subtotal plus shippingMethod.cost.

onCartRefresh -> when checkout-initializer reloads checkout details (e.g., after retry), rebind to the latest cart and items and update the summary; if itemsCount is zero or cart.status is not open, display a non-interactive state with a prompt to go back to cart.html or index.html.

onViewItemClick -> when the user clicks an item name or image in the summary, navigate to the product detail page using a URL pattern such as product.html?id=<product.id> (without altering the checkout session).

components.functionality: Provides the final action to complete the checkout: validates that contact details and shipping method are set for the current CheckoutSession and, if valid, calls completeCheckout to place the order. It coordinates disabling the submit button during processing and handles success and error states.
event_handlers: onInit -> subscribe to checkout-initializer state changes to know whether checkoutSession and cart are fully loaded and whether status indicates an in-progress checkout.

onClickPlaceOrder -> before calling completeCheckout, check that checkoutSession.contactEmail, checkoutSession.contactFullName, and checkoutSession.shippingMethodId are present and that cart.itemsCount is greater than zero and cart.status is open; if any condition fails, highlight the relevant section and, if needed, update the URL parameter `step` to direct the user back to the missing information.

onSubmitValid -> when all required data is present, disable the Place Order button and call completeCheckout; on success, store the returned checkoutSession and orderReference in checkout-initializer, update the URL parameters to include `status=success` and `orderRef=<orderReference>`, and trigger checkout-success-state to display confirmation.

onSubmitError -> if completeCheckout returns success=false or the call fails, re-enable the Place Order button and send the error information to checkout-error-banner for display without changing the URL to status=success.

components.functionality: Displays the confirmation state after a successful checkout. Uses the orderReference and message returned by completeCheckout (and reflected in the URL as orderRef and status=success) to show the user their order reference and prevent further editing of checkout details.
event_handlers: onInitOrUrlChange -> detect when the URL parameter `status=success` is present; when it is, confirm that checkoutSession.status indicates a completed checkout and read the `orderRef` parameter to display the order reference to the user.

onContinueShoppingClick -> when the user clicks a "Continue Shopping" control in the success state, navigate to index.html, optionally appending `orderRef=<orderReference>` to the destination URL for downstream messaging.

onBlockEdits -> when in success state, intercept attempts to open or submit contact-info-form or shipping-method-selector and either prevent changes or show a non-editable message indicating that the order has already been completed.

components.functionality: Provides a persistent navigation option for the user to leave the checkout flow and return to the cart page to modify their cart before completing the order.
event_handlers: onClick -> navigate to cart.html, optionally including a URL parameter such as `?from=checkout` to indicate that the user arrived from the checkout page.

components.functionality: Shows non-visual, functional error messages for failures encountered during the checkout flow, such as errors from createOrGetCheckoutSession, getCheckoutSessionDetails, updateCheckoutContactInfo, updateCheckoutShippingMethod, or completeCheckout. Provides actions to retry the operation or navigate away if recovery is not possible.
event_handlers: onApiError -> when any checkout-related API call fails, receive an error descriptor (source interface name and message) from checkout-initializer or child components and display it in the banner.

onRetryClick -> when the user clicks a retry action, signal checkout-initializer to re-invoke the relevant interface (e.g., getCheckoutSessionDetails for data reload or reattempt the last update call as appropriate).
onNavigateToCartClick -> when a non-recoverable error is detected (e.g., no open cart in getCheckoutSessionDetails), navigate to cart.html.
onNavigateToHomeClick -> provide an option to navigate to index.html if the user prefers to leave the checkout flow after an error.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the classic fixed-width, center-aligned layout from the mockup: full-width header and navigation bands at the top, followed by a hero-style checkout header band. The actual checkout components live in the main content band below this hero, inside a 960–1024px wide centered container. Within this container, content is split into two columns: a wider left column (about 70% of the container) stacking the contact-info-form, shipping-method-selector, and place-order-form as separate glossy cards; and a narrower right column (about 30%) containing a tall order-summary-panel card. The checkout-error-banner, when present, appears as a full-width alert band at the top of the checkout content area, spanning both columns. The back-to-cart-link is placed at the top-left of the main content, just above the first form card. The checkout-success-state replaces the two-column layout with a centered, hero-like confirmation state once the order is completed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the classic fixed-width, center-aligned layout from the mockup: full-width header and navigation bands at the top, followed by a hero-style checkout header band. The actual checkout components live in the main content band below this hero, inside a 960–1024px wide centered container. Within this container, content is split into two columns: a wider left column (about 70% of the container) stacking the contact-info-form, shipping-method-selector, and place-order-form as separate glossy cards; and a narrower right column (about 30%) containing a tall order-summary-panel card. The checkout-error-banner, when present, appears as a full-width alert band at the top of the checkout content area, spanning both columns. The back-to-cart-link is placed at the top-left of the main content, just above the first form card. The checkout-success-state replaces the two-column layout with a centered, hero-like confirmation state once the order is completed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header + navigation occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the initial viewport, with the hero-style checkout band taking another ~25–30%. The checkout content band beneath uses the remaining area above the fold for the two-column layout. Within the centered content container, the left column occupies about 68–72% of the width and the right column 28–32%, with a 16–24px gutter between them. Each card (forms and summary) uses 12–16px internal padding, with 16–24px vertical spacing between stacked cards in the left column. The order-summary-panel is vertically more continuous, extending from near the top of the left column’s first card to around its bottom, creating a visually tall card. On success, the checkout-success-state consumes most of the vertical space in the main content band, appearing as a single dominant module with generous vertical padding (24–32px) but still within the same fixed-width container.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains moderate-to-high, with little unused space: each card packs headings, concise labels, form fields, and buttons, surrounded by glossy gradients and subtle textures rather than large whitespace. Fields within forms are arranged in one or two columns depending on width, but with at least 8–12px spacing between inputs. The order-summary-panel shows multiple line items, totals, and small thumbnails stacked tightly yet clearly separated by thin dividers and gradient shifts. The error banner is bold and dense, with icon, headline, and short explanatory text in a tight band. The success state uses slightly more breathing room but still follows the high-energy, glossy aesthetic with strong gradients, large iconography, and marketing-style copy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the global site logo and navigation, then moves into the hero-style checkout band (e.g., 'Secure Checkout' with driving imagery), establishing context. From there, the flow drops into the main checkout area: first to the checkout-error-banner if present (bright horizontal strip), then to the back-to-cart-link at the top-left of the content container. The main reading path continues down the left column through the contact-info-form, then the shipping-method-selector, and finally the place-order-form. In parallel, the right column’s order-summary-panel, with its bright total and product imagery, attracts peripheral attention, creating a subtle Z-pattern: from the left column’s headings to the right column’s total and 'Place Order' alignment near the bottom. On success, the visual flow is simplified into a single central confirmation block, with a large success icon and headline at the top, followed by order details and a strong primary action (e.g., 'Continue Shopping') below.

layout.overall_description: Below the glossy site header and navigation, a hero-style checkout band spans the full browser width, featuring a dark, high-contrast gradient background, a dynamic driving or dashboard image on the right, and bold 'Secure Checkout' messaging on the left. Beneath this band, the main checkout content sits inside a fixed-width (about 960–1024px) center-aligned container. At the very top of this container, a bright, horizontally stretched error banner (when active) cuts across the full width, immediately followed by a slim row hosting a back-to-cart link on the far left. The content below is divided into two columns: on the left, a stack of glossy, beveled cards for contact information, shipping method, and final confirmation, each spanning the full width of the left column and separated by 16–24px of vertical space; on the right, a tall, high-contrast order summary card begins at roughly the same top line as the first form card and runs down past the midline of the page. Gradients and thin borders give each card a distinct but tightly packed module appearance. The place-order button is large, glossy, and right-aligned within its card, visually tying to the order total in the sidebar to create a strong focal point near the bottom-right of the viewport. When the order is successfully placed, the two-column structure is visually replaced by a single, dominant confirmation module centered in the same container, framed by a vivid gradient and large success icon, giving the sense of a mini hero section within the checkout area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-initializer is a non-visual controller that does not occupy visible space on the page. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the checkout content hierarchy, just below the hero-style checkout band, orchestrating which of the checkout components (forms, summary, error banner, success state) are currently visible. In layout terms, it is considered to be positioned above the checkout-error-banner in the document flow but rendered with no height or width, exerting no visual footprint and not affecting spacing between visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logical controller only; not rendered as a visual element and does not affect layout spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-error-banner appears as a bold, full-width strip at the very top of the checkout content area, directly beneath the hero-style checkout band and above the two-column layout. It spans 100% of the centered content container (approximately 960–1024px) and about 56–80px in height, with 12–16px of internal padding. The banner’s background is a saturated red-to-orange horizontal gradient with a glossy sheen, framed by a subtle 1px inner glow and a darker outer border to match the 2010s aesthetic. On the left side of the banner, occupying roughly the first 20–25% of its width, a warning icon and a bold uppercase error title are stacked; to the right, a short explanatory message wraps within the remaining space. There is a 16px vertical gap below this banner before the row containing the back-to-cart-link and the top edges of the left and right columns. When the banner is hidden, the main content shifts up to occupy its space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable checkout content; pushes other components down when visible but does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-cart-link is positioned at the top-left corner of the checkout content container, immediately below the checkout-error-banner (or below the hero band if no error is shown). It sits in a slim horizontal row that spans the full content width but is visually anchored to the left, about 8–12px below the bottom of the error banner. The link itself is a compact, left-aligned text element with a small left-pointing arrow icon, styled as a glossy or slightly beveled pill or underlined text, occupying roughly 10–15% of the container width. To maintain the busy, marketing-driven aesthetic, the background behind this row continues the page’s dark gradient rather than open whitespace. The space to the right of the link remains mostly empty, letting the eye drop naturally from this link into the main left-column card below. There is a 8–12px vertical gap between this link row and the top edge of the contact-info-form card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the document above the main forms; scrolls away normally and does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-form is the first major card in the left column of the two-column layout. It starts just below the back-to-cart-link, aligned flush with the left edge of the centered container’s left column (about 0–16px inset from the container edge depending on gutter design). It occupies approximately 70% of the content container’s width, with the order-summary-panel occupying the remaining 30% on the right, separated by a 16–24px horizontal gutter. The card itself has 16–20px of internal padding and a glossy dark gradient background (e.g., charcoal to deep green) with a subtle bevel and soft outer glow. At the top of the card, a bold heading like 'Contact Information' spans about 60% of the card width, with an accompanying icon (envelope or user silhouette) to its left or right, following the image-first, marketing-oriented pattern. Below the heading, the form fields are arranged in a two-column grid where possible: for example, 'First Name' and 'Last Name' sit side-by-side, each taking about 48% of the card width with a 4–8px gap, while longer fields such as email or phone stretch across the full card width. Vertical spacing between form rows is tight but readable (about 8–12px). The bottom of this card sits 16–24px above the top of the next card (shipping-method-selector), maintaining a visually dense but modular stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, participating in the normal scroll; its top aligns roughly with the top of the order-summary-panel on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-method-selector card is stacked directly under the contact-info-form within the left column, aligned to the same left and right edges of that column so that the sides of the cards line up vertically. It begins about 16–24px below the bottom edge of the contact-info-form. This card spans the full width of the left column (again about 70% of the container) with 16px internal padding. The background uses a slightly lighter or differently tinted glossy gradient (for example, dark gray to deep teal) to differentiate it while still matching the polished aesthetic and beveled border. A bold section heading ('Shipping Method') runs along the top, occupying the left two-thirds of the card. Below, shipping options are displayed as horizontally stacked, radio-selectable 'capsules' or rows: each option is a mini sub-card with its own subtle gradient and 8–12px padding, arranged either in a single column or two-column grid depending on viewport width, but always kept within the left column boundaries. Pricing and estimated delivery time are emphasized with brighter text or a small icon. The bottom margin provides a 16–24px gap before the start of the place-order-form card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static stacked card in the left column, maintaining its relative position between the contact-info-form above and the place-order-form below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The place-order-form is the final card in the left column and acts as the strong visual anchor near the bottom-left of the above-the-fold area. It is aligned exactly with the left and right edges of the other left-column cards, starting 16–24px beneath the shipping-method-selector. Internally, it uses slightly more generous padding (20–24px) to emphasize its importance, while its background has the most vivid gradient within the column (for example, deep green fading into a bright, almost neon yellow-green at the right edge), plus a pronounced beveled border and soft outer glow to convey a high-energy, action-oriented feel. At the top of the card, a heading such as 'Review & Place Order' runs across the upper-left region, with a small security or lock icon reinforcing trust. Immediately below, any final form elements (terms acceptance checkbox, notes field, or payment confirmation) are arranged in one or two rows with 8–12px vertical spacing. The bottom-right quadrant of the card is reserved for the primary 'Place Order' button: a large, pill-shaped, glossy button roughly 30–40% of the card width, right-aligned, and visually lined up horizontally with the grand total section in the order-summary-panel to the right. This alignment creates a strong visual linkage between the cost displayed in the sidebar and the action in the main column. The card’s bottom margin uses 24px of space before any subsequent content or footer, giving it a slightly more anchored feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card anchored at the bottom of the left column; the primary CTA within it forms part of the page’s main visual focal point alongside the order-summary-panel’s totals.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-summary-panel occupies the entire right column of the two-column layout, beginning near the vertical level of the top of the contact-info-form and extending downward so that, above the fold, it feels like a single, tall, unified card. It is offset from the left column by a 16–24px horizontal gutter. The panel’s width is approximately 28–32% of the total content container. Inside, 16–20px padding is used to stack content in dense, clearly separated bands. A bright, glossy header strip (for example, a saturated yellow-to-green gradient) spans the top of the panel, with bold uppercase text ('Order Summary') and a small cart icon centered or left-aligned. Below the header, product line items are listed vertically: each row contains a small product thumbnail on the left (occupying about 25–30% of the row width) and the product name plus price on the right. Thin dividers or subtle gradient transitions separate each line. At the bottom of the list, a shaded subtotal section leads into a more prominent total section, where the grand total amount is displayed in large, high-contrast text near the bottom-right of the panel, roughly aligning horizontally with the 'Place Order' button in the left column. The bottom of the card has a slight inner glow and darker border, visually grounding it. Above-the-fold, this tall card gives a sense of continuity and anchors the right side of the layout with rich, marketing-driven detail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Configured as a sticky sidebar on larger screens: once the user scrolls past the top of the checkout content, the order-summary-panel sticks to the top of the viewport within its right column, remaining visible while the left-column forms scroll. On smaller screens, it can fall back to normal flow beneath the forms.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-success-state replaces the standard two-column checkout content once an order has been successfully placed. It appears in the same centered content container beneath the global hero/header area, but instead of two columns, it presents a single, wide, glossy module occupying roughly 70–80% of the container width and centered horizontally. This success module has generous vertical padding (24–32px top and bottom) and a bright, celebratory gradient background (for example, deep green at the edges fading to a luminous yellow-green in the middle), framed by a pronounced bevel and inner glow to echo the hero-banner feel. At the top center of this module, a large success icon (e.g., a circular checkmark badge) sits above or alongside a bold 'Order Confirmed' heading that spans about 60% of the width. Below, a short, marketing-style confirmation sentence and essential order details (order number, summary of items, estimated delivery) are stacked in concise, left-aligned lines. Additional actions—like 'Print Receipt' or 'Continue Shopping'—are presented as glossy buttons or pill-shaped links aligned horizontally near the bottom center. The background behind this module continues the page’s darker gradient, so the success card stands out as a bright, spotlighted centerpiece. All previous checkout forms, error banners, and the order-summary-panel are visually hidden when this state is active, leaving the success state as the single dominant element in the main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered full-width module that replaces the two-column layout; scrolls normally with the page but is usually short enough to appear largely above the fold on typical screens.

name: Vehicle Selector
title: Vehicle Selector
description: Select a vehicle by year, make, model, and body style to view compatible car audio products.

core_features: Cascading vehicle selection form that loads available years, makes, models, and body styles via getVehicleYears, getVehicleMakes, getVehicleModels, and getVehicleBodyStyles.
Automatic pre-fill of the selector and display of the current vehicle context using getSelectedVehicle when the page loads.
Validation of the vehicle selection and submission through setSelectedVehicle, then navigation to a results page (vehicle_results.html or the URL provided by the nextUrl parameter).
