Quick links to common product categories for the currently selected vehicle that navigate to vehicle_results.html with both vehicle context and category filter reflected in URL parameters.
Informational panel explaining how vehicle-specific shopping and fitment guarantees work, encouraging users to complete a full vehicle selection before browsing products.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: First-time visitor selecting a vehicle
1. User navigates to vehicle_selector.html (optionally with nextUrl, but with no existing SelectedVehicle).
2. On load, the page calls getSelectedVehicle. Since no vehicle is selected, the selector remains empty and the summary shows that no vehicle is currently set.
3. The page calls getVehicleYears and populates the Year dropdown; Make, Model, and Body Style controls start disabled.
4. User selects a Year. The page calls getVehicleMakes(year) and enables the Make dropdown.
5. User selects a Make. The page calls getVehicleModels(year, make) and enables the Model dropdown.
6. User selects a Model. The page calls getVehicleBodyStyles(year, make, model) and enables the Body Style dropdown.
7. User selects a Body Style. The page validates that all four attributes are chosen and enables the "Find products for this vehicle" button.
8. User clicks the submit button. The page calls setSelectedVehicle(year, make, model, bodyStyle).
9. If setSelectedVehicle returns success=true, the page redirects to either the URL from nextUrl (if present in the query string) or to vehicle_results.html, appending the selected vehicle context as URL parameters.
10. On the destination page, the SelectedVehicle context can be used to filter products to those that fit the chosen vehicle.

Workflow 2: Returning visitor with a previously selected vehicle
1. User opens vehicle_selector.html (with or without nextUrl).
2. On load, the page calls getSelectedVehicle and receives hasSelectedVehicle=true and a Vehicle object.
3. The page uses the returned Vehicle to populate and lock in the Year, Make, Model, and Body Style dropdowns to match the existing SelectedVehicle, and updates the vehicle summary.
4. The page still calls getVehicleYears/getVehicleMakes/getVehicleModels/getVehicleBodyStyles as needed to ensure dropdown options are in sync with available data.
5. User can either:
   - Immediately click "Find products for this vehicle" to confirm and proceed (calling setSelectedVehicle again for consistency and then redirecting), or
   - Change the selection by altering Year/Make/Model/Body Style. Changing an earlier field clears dependent fields and reloads options via the respective interfaces.
6. When the user submits, the page calls setSelectedVehicle with the updated values and then redirects using nextUrl or vehicle_results.html with vehicle parameters.

Workflow 3: Deep link with vehicle parameters in the URL
1. User opens a URL like vehicle_selector.html?year=...&make=...&model=...&bodyStyle=...&nextUrl=...
2. On load, the page reads year, make, model, and bodyStyle parameters from the URL (if present).
3. The page calls getVehicleYears, then:
   - If a year parameter is present and matches an available year, it pre-selects that year and calls getVehicleMakes(year).
   - If a make parameter is present and available for that year, it pre-selects that make and calls getVehicleModels(year, make).
   - If a model parameter is present and available for that year/make, it pre-selects that model and calls getVehicleBodyStyles(year, make, model).
   - If a bodyStyle parameter is present and matches one of the returned body styles, it pre-selects that body style.
4. If any of the URL-supplied values are invalid (not in the returned lists), the page ignores the invalid value, clears any dependent fields, and shows a non-blocking validation message.
5. Once all four attributes are valid and selected, the submit button is enabled. When the user clicks submit, the page calls setSelectedVehicle, then redirects to nextUrl (if present) or vehicle_results.html, preserving the vehicle context in URL parameters.

Workflow 4: Using quick category links after selecting a vehicle
1. User selects a valid vehicle (as in Workflows 1–3) and the page has successfully called setSelectedVehicle or has a valid getSelectedVehicle response.
2. The quick category links component becomes active and shows links to common categories (for example, stereos, speakers, installation accessories) that are logically mapped to Category.code values.
3. When the user clicks a quick category link, the page constructs a URL to vehicle_results.html that includes:
   - The selected vehicle context (e.g., via year, make, model, bodyStyle, or vehicleId if available from setSelectedVehicle.getSelectedVehicle).
   - A categoryId parameter corresponding to the chosen category.
4. The browser navigates to this URL, where the results page can use both the SelectedVehicle context and the categoryId URL parameter to filter products.
5. The user can return to vehicle_selector.html at any time to change the vehicle, at which point the quick links will automatically update to use the new vehicle context for future clicks.
interactions: On page load, a controller component calls getSelectedVehicle to determine whether a SelectedVehicle exists and updates the form and summary state accordingly.
On page load, after querying getSelectedVehicle, the page calls getVehicleYears to populate the Year dropdown. The list of years is fully driven by the API response.
When the user changes the Year selection, the page clears Make, Model, and Body Style selections, disables their dropdowns, and calls getVehicleMakes(year) to repopulate the Make dropdown.
When the user changes the Make selection, the page clears Model and Body Style selections, disables their dropdowns, and calls getVehicleModels(year, make) to repopulate the Model dropdown.

When the user changes the Model selection, the page clears the Body Style selection, disables the Body Style dropdown, and calls getVehicleBodyStyles(year, make, model) to repopulate available body styles.

When the user selects a Body Style, the page validates that all four fields (Year, Make, Model, Body Style) are defined and enables the primary "Find products for this vehicle" button if validation passes.

If the user clicks the submit button with incomplete or invalid selections (e.g., missing body style), the page prevents submission, displays validation messages near the relevant controls, and does not call setSelectedVehicle.

When the user clicks the submit button with a valid selection, the page calls setSelectedVehicle(year, make, model, bodyStyle). If success=false is returned, the page shows an error message and does not redirect. If success=true, the page updates any on-page vehicle summary using the returned Vehicle object and then redirects according to state_logic.

If the URL contains a nextUrl parameter, the page uses it as the redirect destination after a successful setSelectedVehicle call, appending appropriate vehicle context parameters. If nextUrl is absent or empty, the page redirects to vehicle_results.html with vehicle context parameters.

Quick category link clicks read the current vehicle context from the in-memory state (kept in sync with getSelectedVehicle/setSelectedVehicle) and build a navigation URL to vehicle_results.html, including both vehicle context and a categoryId parameter tied to the clicked link.

The informational fitment panel does not change data but can reactively update small details like showing the currently recognized vehicle display name if getSelectedVehicle returns one, to reinforce that product recommendations will be vehicle-specific.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL parameters and the SelectedVehicle backend context (via getSelectedVehicle/setSelectedVehicle) to manage and persist state.

URL parameters used:
- nextUrl: Optional. If present, this string is treated as the intended destination after a successful vehicle selection. The page reads nextUrl on load and stores it in memory. When the user submits a valid selection and setSelectedVehicle returns success=true, the page redirects to nextUrl with vehicle context appended as query parameters. If nextUrl is missing or empty, the page redirects to vehicle_results.html instead.
- year, make, model, bodyStyle: Optional. If any of these parameters are present, the page attempts to pre-populate the vehicle selector:
  1) The page calls getVehicleYears. If the year parameter matches one of the returned years, the Year dropdown is pre-selected; otherwise, it is ignored.
  2) If a valid year is set (either from URL or user selection), the page calls getVehicleMakes(year). If the make parameter matches one of the returned makes, the Make dropdown is pre-selected; otherwise, the make parameter is ignored and dependent parameters (model, bodyStyle) are also ignored.
  3) If valid year and make are both set, the page calls getVehicleModels(year, make). If the model parameter matches one of the returned models, it is pre-selected; otherwise, it is ignored and bodyStyle is also ignored.
  4) If valid year, make, and model are set, the page calls getVehicleBodyStyles(year, make, model). If the bodyStyle parameter matches one of the returned bodyStyleCode values, it is pre-selected; otherwise, it is ignored.

Vehicle context in redirects:
- After a successful setSelectedVehicle call, the page builds a redirect URL. The base URL is either nextUrl (if provided) or vehicle_results.html.
- The page constructs a query string that includes the selected vehicle context. The specific parameters can include fields derived from the Vehicle object returned by setSelectedVehicle or getSelectedVehicle, such as:
  - vehicleId (if available from the Vehicle.id field),
  - year, make, model, bodyStyle (derived from the Vehicle fields).
- The page merges these vehicle-related parameters into the target URL's query string without overwriting unrelated existing parameters in nextUrl.

URL updates while interacting:
- When the user changes their vehicle selection on the page, the in-page state is updated immediately.
- Optionally, the page can update the browser URL (e.g., via history API) to reflect the current selection using year, make, model, and bodyStyle parameters so that reloading or sharing the link preserves the partially or fully selected vehicle. These updates do not call any additional interfaces; they only modify the URL.

Persistence and precedence:
- If getSelectedVehicle returns hasSelectedVehicle=true, this context is treated as the baseline selection, but explicit URL parameters (year/make/model/bodyStyle) are allowed to override that baseline on the current view. Once the user submits, setSelectedVehicle overwrites the backend SelectedVehicle with the new selection.
- The page does not use localStorage or other browser storage mechanisms. All persistent state across page loads relies on:
  - URL parameters (for sharable and restorable selection state), and
  - the SelectedVehicle entity on the backend (accessed via getSelectedVehicle and setSelectedVehicle).

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading URL parameters (year, make, model, bodyStyle, nextUrl), calling getSelectedVehicle to retrieve any existing SelectedVehicle, calling getVehicleYears to populate the Year dropdown, and orchestrating pre-selection and cascading API calls based on URL parameters and backend state.

Displays available vehicle years returned by getVehicleYears, allows the user to choose a year, and triggers loading of makes when changed. When a year is selected, it clears and disables dependent dropdowns (make, model, bodyStyle) until new data is fetched.

Displays available makes for the selected year using getVehicleMakes(year). It remains disabled until a valid year is selected. When the make changes, it clears and disables model and body style selections, then triggers getVehicleModels(year, make).

Displays available models based on the selected year and make via getVehicleModels(year, make). It is disabled until both year and make are set. When the model changes, it clears the body style selection and triggers getVehicleBodyStyles(year, make, model).

Displays available body styles for the selected year, make, and model using getVehicleBodyStyles(year, make, model). It is disabled until year, make, and model are all selected. When a body style is selected, it completes the vehicle selection and allows form validation to enable submission.

Shows real-time and on-submit validation messages for the vehicle selection form (e.g., missing fields, invalid combinations where API calls return no results). It listens to form state and API responses but does not call interfaces itself.

Summarizes the currently recognized vehicle selection for the user. On load, it uses the Vehicle object from getSelectedVehicle (if available) and updates whenever setSelectedVehicle completes successfully or the user completes a new selection in the form.

Wraps the year, make, model, and body style dropdowns into a single logical form. Handles form-level validation on submit, preventing submission if any required field is missing or invalid, and coordinating the call to setSelectedVehicle when the selection is complete.

Primary action control labeled to find products for the selected vehicle. It remains disabled until the form has a valid year, make, model, and body style. When clicked, it triggers the form submission logic, which calls setSelectedVehicle and, upon success, redirects using the state_logic rules.

Displays links to commonly used categories for the currently selected vehicle (for example, stereos, speakers, installation accessories). Each link builds a navigation URL to vehicle_results.html that includes the current vehicle context (such as vehicleId or year/make/model/bodyStyle) and a categoryId parameter mapped to a Category.code value. Links are disabled or hidden until a valid vehicle selection exists (either from getSelectedVehicle or a new setSelectedVehicle call).

Provides explanatory text about the benefits of selecting a vehicle (e.g., that product listings will be filtered to fit the chosen vehicle and that fitment guarantees depend on accurate vehicle information). Optionally displays the current vehicle displayName from the Vehicle object to contextualize the information.

Keeps the browser URL query parameters in sync with the in-memory vehicle selection. When the user changes year, make, model, or bodyStyle, this component updates the URL parameters (without reloading) so that the page can be reloaded or shared while preserving the selection state. It also exposes parsed URL parameters back to the initializer component.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s fixed-width, center-aligned structure: full-width glossy header and nav (outside this spec), a large marketing hero at the top, and beneath it a two-column content band. The vehicle selector experience is housed in the main left column as a prominent glossy card (the form and its elements), while supporting / shortcut content (category quick links and supplemental fitment info) lives in the narrower right sidebar card. All components are grouped into visually distinct modules with beveled, gradient-backed cards separated by subtle borders and tonal shifts rather than open white space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s fixed-width, center-aligned structure: full-width glossy header and nav (outside this spec), a large marketing hero at the top, and beneath it a two-column content band. The vehicle selector experience is housed in the main left column as a prominent glossy card (the form and its elements), while supporting / shortcut content (category quick links and supplemental fitment info) lives in the narrower right sidebar card. All components are grouped into visually distinct modules with beveled, gradient-backed cards separated by subtle borders and tonal shifts rather than open white space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Above the fold, the header + nav occupy around 15–20% of the viewport height, the hero banner takes roughly 40%, and the remaining 40–45% goes to the two-column band containing the vehicle selector. Within that band, the left content column spans about 70% of the central 960–1024px container, and the right sidebar spans the remaining 30%. Inside the left column, the vehicle-selection-form card takes the top two-thirds of the column area above the fold; the fitment info panel fills the remaining third underneath. In the right column, the category quick links card runs from the top of the column, matching the vertical height of the form card, leaving a modest space below for any extension or future content.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page uses a moderate-to-high density: the form card, summary band, validation strip, and quick links are all visually active with gradients, borders, and compact but legible spacing. Cards use roughly 16px padding on all sides, dropdowns and buttons are tightly stacked with 8–16px gaps, and text blocks are short and bold. There is little empty whitespace; section separations rely on card edges, gradient bands, and horizontal rules instead of large blank areas, matching the energetic, marketing-heavy aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the logo and nav across the top, then down into the hero’s product imagery and headline. From there, it is led directly downward into the left-column vehicle-selection-form card, starting at the "Select Your Vehicle" heading, then scanning left-to-right through the first row of dropdowns (Year, Make), then to the second row (Model, Body Style), and finally to the bright submit button at the bottom right of the card. Any validation messages appear just under the dropdown stack as a highlighted strip, and a summary band just below the form confirms the chosen vehicle. In parallel, peripheral attention moves to the right sidebar, where a glossy "Quick Links" card provides fast access to categories, analogous to the "Latest Products" panel in the mockup. The visual flow thus steps down the page in clear horizontal bands while gently splitting attention between primary form actions on the left and secondary exploration on the right.

layout.overall_description: Within a full-width metallic-dark gradient background, a fixed-width central container (around 980px) is centered on the page. The glossy header and nav span the top; below them, a dynamic hero banner features car audio imagery and a motion-filled driving scene, reinforced with bold uppercase copy. Just beneath the hero, the main content band appears as a slightly lighter gradient strip that contains a two-column layout. On the left, a large beveled card labeled "Select Your Vehicle" dominates, with four dropdowns arranged in two compact rows above a bright, high-contrast submit button and a thin highlighted area for validation messages. Directly under this card, still in the left column, a secondary info panel explains fitment accuracy with iconography and short lines of text. On the right, a narrower sidebar card echoes the mockup’s "Latest Products" block: a glossy, high-contrast panel of category quick links styled as pill buttons or mini tiles, stacked vertically with minimal gaps. The background gradients extend to the edges of the viewport, so color fills the side margins; the cards themselves stand out with subtle inner glows and borders. The overall impression is energetic and busy yet structured: clearly defined horizontal steps down the page and a strong left-column focus on completing the selector, supported by a right-column panel for quick navigation to product categories.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component is non-visual and sits conceptually at the top of the main content band, just below the hero banner and aligned with the center of the fixed-width container. It initializes the state for all vehicle selection components in both columns but does not render any visible area, spacing, or decoration. It occupies no layout space; other visible components (the vehicle-selection-form card on the left and the sidebar card on the right) are arranged as if this controller were not present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, non-spacing element that spans the logical scope of the main two-column content container without affecting visual layout or flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the vehicle-selection-form card in the left column, the Year dropdown sits in the upper left quadrant of the card, forming the first item in the top row of inputs. It occupies roughly 48% of the form card’s inner width, leaving about an 8–16px horizontal gap to its right neighbor (the Make dropdown). A bold, small-uppercase label (e.g., "YEAR") sits directly above it with 4–8px spacing; the dropdown itself is tall and glossy, with a strong gradient and subtle bevel, matching the 2010s hardware aesthetic. There is approximately 12–16px of padding between the top of the card and the Year label, and 8–12px below the dropdown before the next input row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form card; aligns to the left edge of the card’s content area and resizes proportionally with the card while preserving its roughly half-width relationship to the Make dropdown.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Make dropdown is placed in the same top row as the Year dropdown, occupying the upper right quadrant of the vehicle-selection-form card. It aligns horizontally with the Year dropdown, starting after an 8–16px gap and extending to about 48% of the card’s inner width, flush with the card’s right padding. Its label ("MAKE") is positioned directly above, aligned to the left edge of the control for consistency. Vertically, it shares the same top margin from the card edge as the Year input, creating a clean, two-column row of fields. The Make dropdown’s height and visual style mirror the Year dropdown, with a glossy gradient surface and slight inner glow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned in the top input row of the form card, maintaining equal width and vertical alignment with the Year dropdown as the card scales.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Model dropdown is positioned directly beneath the Year dropdown, forming the left control in the second row of the vehicle-selection-form card. It aligns vertically with the Year dropdown’s left edge and shares the same approximate width (about 48% of the card’s inner width). There is a consistent vertical gap of about 12–16px between the bottom of the Year dropdown and the top of the Model label, with the label ("MODEL") in small uppercase. This arrangement creates a clear, stacked grid of inputs: Year above Model on the left, Make above Body Style on the right. The control’s glossy styling, height, and typography match the other dropdowns, maintaining a tight but readable vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column of the form grid; maintains its alignment under the Year dropdown and scales in width with the form card while preserving row and column relationships.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Body Style dropdown sits in the second row of inputs, directly beneath the Make dropdown on the right side of the vehicle-selection-form card. It aligns with the right padding of the card and the right edge of the Make dropdown above, mirroring the Model dropdown’s position on the left. There is a uniform vertical gap of 12–16px between the Make dropdown and the Body Style label, and the control itself is the same height and width as the other fields (about 48% of the card’s inner width). This creates a compact 2×2 grid of dropdowns that fills most of the upper portion of the card, presenting a dense but orderly selection area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the right column of the form grid; it holds its place under the Make dropdown and adapts in width with the card while maintaining the symmetrical two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation panel appears directly below the grid of four dropdowns inside the vehicle-selection-form card, spanning almost the full inner width of the card (around 92–96%, respecting side padding). It is separated from the bottom row of dropdowns by a tight 8–12px gap. When active, it manifests as a low-height, high-contrast band—either a bright warning yellow or red gradient—framed with a thin, sharp border and a small warning icon on the left, followed by concise text. The band hugs the inputs closely, keeping the overall form compact and dense while clearly signaling issues before the user reaches the submit button further below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, expanding vertically as needed within the form card when messages are present and collapsing to zero height (or hidden) when not in use, without affecting the placement of elements above it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The selection summary is presented as a slim, glossy info band positioned immediately beneath the main vehicle-selection-form card in the left column, separated by about 12–16px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the left column (roughly 70% of the page container), aligning exactly with the form card’s left and right edges to feel like a direct extension of the form. The band is relatively low in height—just enough for a single line or two of text summarizing the chosen Year/Make/Model/Body Style—with a subtle gradient and perhaps a small checkmark or car icon on the left. This creates a clear step in the visual flow: select values in the card above, submit, and then read the confirmation summary in this narrow band just below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the left column; it grows slightly in height if more text is required but remains anchored directly beneath the form card, pushing the fitment info panel further down as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The vehicle-selection-form is the dominant card in the main content area, positioned at the top of the left column just below the hero banner. It occupies about 70% of the central container’s width and roughly half of the visible content height below the hero, making it the primary functional focal point. The card is centered vertically within the left column’s segment, with 24–32px of top margin from the bottom of the hero and 16–24px of bottom margin to the summary band. Inside, 16px of padding on all sides frame the content. The form’s internal layout follows a tight grid: heading at the top, two rows of side-by-side dropdowns beneath, the validation strip below those, and the submit button anchored near the bottom right. The card has a glossy, dark-to-light gradient background, an outer glow or subtle drop shadow, and a beveled border, echoing the product-focused hero above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored to the top of the left column within the main two-column band. It does not scroll independently; instead, it moves with the page, preserving its central placement and proportionate width as the layout scales within the fixed-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit button sits inside the vehicle-selection-form card, positioned near the bottom right corner of the card’s content area. It is separated from the validation panel or the lowest dropdown row by about 16–24px of vertical space, creating a distinct call-to-action zone. Horizontally, it aligns flush with the right edge of the dropdown fields, leaving a comfortable 16px right padding inside the card. The button is visually bold: a saturated green or yellow gradient with strong beveling and a soft outer glow, larger in height than the dropdowns to signal clickability. Its width is moderate, covering roughly 25–35% of the card’s width, so it feels substantial without spanning the entire card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form card, right-aligned and anchored near the lower edge of the card’s content area. On longer pages it scrolls naturally with the card; it does not stick or float separately.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category quick links are placed in the right sidebar at the same vertical level as the top of the vehicle-selection-form card, filling the top portion of the narrower right column. This component is contained in a glossy, beveled sidebar card that echoes the "Latest Products" block from the mockup: approximately 30% of the page container’s width and similar in height to the form card, so their bottom edges align visually across the two columns. Within this card, quick links are arranged vertically as bold pill-shaped buttons or short rows, each spanning most of the sidebar card width with 8–12px vertical gaps. Small category icons or arrows on the left and right of each link add to the busy, marketing-heavy feel. The card has 16px internal padding and sits 24–32px below the hero, aligned to the right edge of the central container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, aligned to the top of the main content band and scrolling with the page. It does not stick to the viewport but maintains its relative vertical alignment with the form card on the left.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fitment info panel is a secondary information card positioned directly below the vehicle-selection-summary band in the left column. It spans the full width of the left column, matching the form card’s and summary band’s horizontal alignment, and is separated from the summary by about 16–24px of vertical space. The panel uses a slightly lighter gradient than the form card, with a subtle border and perhaps a small fitment or shield icon on the left, followed by short, punchy text about compatibility guarantees or installation notes. Internally, there is 16px of padding and a compact layout: a bold title at the top, one or two concise lines of body copy, and possibly a small inline link for more details. This panel creates a clear, third step in the vertical flow of the left column: choose vehicle, see summary, then read fitment details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the left column, pushing any subsequent content downward as needed. It scrolls with the page and does not overlay or float over other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-synchronizer component is non-visual and conceptually sits behind the entire vehicle selection experience, updating the URL as the user interacts with the form. It does not occupy visible screen space or have borders, gradients, or padding. From a layout perspective, it is associated with the same central content container as the form and summary but has no direct impact on the spatial relationships of visible elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-spacing; logically scoped to the full page but with no fixed, floating, or sticky behavior in the visual layout.

name: Vehicle Fitment Results
title: Vehicle Fitment Results
description: Shows product categories and recommended products that fit the currently selected vehicle, and lets users navigate to vehicle-specific category and product pages.

core_features: Load and validate the currently selected vehicle using getSelectedVehicle; if none is selected, show a vehicle-required message and link to vehicle_selector.html.
Retrieve the fitment overview for the selected vehicle using getVehicleFitmentOverview, including the vehicle, compatible product categories, and recommended products.
Display a concise summary of the selected vehicle (year, make, model, body style) with a control to navigate to vehicle_selector.html to change the vehicle.
List compatible product categories from getVehicleFitmentOverview.categories, each showing the category name and compatible product count, and link to category.html scoped to the selected vehicle.
Display recommended fitment products from getVehicleFitmentOverview.recommendedProducts in a configurable list/grid, each linking to product.html with a ref=vehicle_fitment parameter.
Provide optional client-side sorting and view-mode toggles for recommended products, controlled by URL parameters (e.g., sort and view) so that the state is shareable via URL.
Handle empty or partial fitment responses gracefully (e.g., no categories or no recommended products) by showing appropriate functional messaging and navigation options.
user_workflows:
Accessing the page after selecting a vehicle:
1. User submits the vehicle selection form on vehicle_selector.html.
2. vehicle_selector.html redirects the user to vehicle_results.html (this page).
3. On load, the page-init controller calls getSelectedVehicle.
4. If getSelectedVehicle.hasSelectedVehicle is true, the controller calls getVehicleFitmentOverview.
5. The vehicle summary bar displays getVehicleFitmentOverview.vehicle.
6. The fitment category list displays getVehicleFitmentOverview.categories as navigable links.
7. The recommended products section displays getVehicleFitmentOverview.recommendedProducts.
8. User selects a fitment category to browse by clicking a category row.
9. Browser navigates to category.html with categoryId set to the clicked categoryCode and fitmentScope=selected_vehicle.

Visiting the page directly from a bookmark or URL:
1. User navigates directly to vehicle_results.html (with or without optional URL parameters such as sort or view).
2. The page-init controller reads URL parameters for state (e.g., recommended products sort order and view mode).
3. The controller calls getSelectedVehicle.
4. If hasSelectedVehicle is false, the page shows a message that a vehicle must be selected and a link to vehicle_selector.html; no fitment overview call is made.
5. If hasSelectedVehicle is true, the controller calls getVehicleFitmentOverview.
6. Data from getVehicleFitmentOverview is bound to the vehicle summary, category list, and recommended products components.
7. Changing sort/view controls updates the URL parameters and re-renders the recommended products section accordingly.

Changing the selected vehicle from the results page:
1. User clicks the "Change Vehicle" control in the vehicle summary bar.
2. Browser navigates to vehicle_selector.html.
3. On vehicle_selector.html, user chooses a different vehicle and submits the form (handled on that page).
4. vehicle_selector.html redirects back to vehicle_results.html.
5. On load, getSelectedVehicle returns the newly chosen vehicle; the page calls getVehicleFitmentOverview and refreshes all components for the new vehicle context.

Browsing a compatible category from the fitment overview:
1. User reviews the list of compatible categories in the fitment category list.
2. User clicks a category (e.g., a categoryName entry).
3. The click handler builds a URL to category.html including categoryId set to the selected categoryCode and fitmentScope=selected_vehicle.
4. Browser navigates to category.html, where results are filtered to the selected vehicle’s exact-fit products for that category.

Exploring a recommended product from the vehicle fitment page:
1. User scrolls to the recommended products section populated from getVehicleFitmentOverview.recommendedProducts.
2. User clicks on a recommended product card or name.
3. The click handler builds a URL to product.html with id set to the product.id of the clicked recommended product and ref=vehicle_fitment.
4. Browser navigates to product.html, where the product is displayed in detail and can be added to cart or compared (on that page).

Adjusting recommended products view and sort using URL parameters:
1. User opens vehicle_results.html; the controller reads existing URL parameters (e.g., sort and view).
2. Based on the sort parameter, the recommended products section orders getVehicleFitmentOverview.recommendedProducts client-side (e.g., by price or rating if available in the Product data).
3. Based on the view parameter, the section renders products in the configured view mode (e.g., list or grid layout logic handled by front-end).
4. User changes the sort or view using controls in the recommended products header.
5. The change handler updates the URL parameters using the History API or a full reload, ensuring the updated state is reflected in the address bar.
6. The controller re-reads the URL parameters and re-renders the recommended products accordingly, enabling sharable links with consistent view/sort state.
interactions:
Page load and data initialization:
- Event: onPageLoad
- Behavior: The page-init controller parses the current window.location.search for state parameters (e.g., sort, view) and then calls getSelectedVehicle.
- If getSelectedVehicle.hasSelectedVehicle is false, the controller renders the no-selected-vehicle state and does NOT call getVehicleFitmentOverview.
- If hasSelectedVehicle is true, the controller calls getVehicleFitmentOverview and distributes the returned data to downstream components.

"Change Vehicle" interaction:
- Component: vehicle-summary-bar
- Event: onClickChangeVehicle
- Behavior: Navigates the browser to vehicle_selector.html without additional parameters. The selection page is responsible for updating SelectedVehicle and redirecting back here.

Category link click interaction:
- Component: fitment-category-list
- Event: onClickCategoryItem(category)
- Behavior: Builds a URL to category.html with query parameters categoryId set to category.categoryCode (from getVehicleFitmentOverview.categories) and fitmentScope set to selected_vehicle. Triggers window.location navigation.

Recommended product click interaction:
- Component: recommended-products-grid
- Event: onClickProduct(product)
- Behavior: Builds a URL to product.html with query parameters id set to product.id and ref set to vehicle_fitment. Triggers window.location navigation.

Recommended products sort change:
- Component: recommended-products-toolbar
- Event: onChangeSort(newSortValue)
- Behavior: Updates the sort value in URLSearchParams (e.g., ?sort=newSortValue while preserving other existing parameters). The controller then re-computes the ordering of getVehicleFitmentOverview.recommendedProducts client-side based on Product fields (such as price or ratingAverage) and re-renders the list.

Recommended products view mode toggle:
- Component: recommended-products-toolbar
- Event: onToggleView(newViewMode)
- Behavior: Updates the view parameter in URLSearchParams (e.g., ?view=newViewMode). The recommended products section reads this state and renders a different layout mode (e.g., altering how much information per product is shown).

Handling empty fitment overview:
- Component: fitment-empty-state
- Event: onFitmentDataLoaded (with categories and recommendedProducts)
- Behavior: If categories is empty and recommendedProducts is empty, shows a message indicating no compatible products are currently available for the selected vehicle and provides a link to vehicle_selector.html to try a different vehicle.
page_functionality.state_logic:
This page does not require inbound URL parameters for its core functionality, but it supports optional URL parameters to control client-side state, ensuring that state is shareable and bookmarkable. On every load, the page-init controller reads window.location.search and interprets the following optional parameters:

- sort: Controls how recommended products from getVehicleFitmentOverview.recommendedProducts are ordered client-side. Supported values are interpreted by front-end logic and may reference Product fields (e.g., price or ratingAverage). If sort is missing or unrecognized, the natural order from the API is used.
- view: Controls the display mode for recommended products (e.g., list or grid). If view is missing or unrecognized, a default mode defined by front-end logic is used.

State evaluation flow:
1. On page load, parse sort and view from the URL (if present) and store them in an in-memory page state object.
2. Call getSelectedVehicle. If hasSelectedVehicle is false, the page renders only the no-selected-vehicle message and ignores sort/view because there is no fitment data to display.
3. If a vehicle is selected, call getVehicleFitmentOverview to retrieve vehicle, categories, and recommendedProducts.
4. Apply the current sort parameter to the recommendedProducts array client-side (no additional API calls) before passing it to the recommended-products-grid component.
5. Pass the current view parameter to the recommended-products-grid to determine its rendering mode.
6. When the user changes sort or view via UI controls, the event handlers update the corresponding URL parameters (using pushState/replaceState or a full page reload) and then re-run the rendering logic using the already-fetched fitment data. All state is thus derived from the combination of the API responses and the current URL; no localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and page-level state. Parses URL parameters, checks whether a vehicle is selected, retrieves the vehicle fitment overview if applicable, and routes data to child components.
Displays when no vehicle is currently selected. Informs the user that a vehicle must be chosen to see fitment results and provides navigation to vehicle_selector.html.
Shows a concise summary of the selected vehicle (derived from getVehicleFitmentOverview.vehicle) and provides a "Change Vehicle" interaction that navigates to the vehicle selection page.

Lists all compatible product categories returned by getVehicleFitmentOverview.categories. Each category row displays the categoryName and productCount and provides a link to the corresponding category.html view scoped to the selected vehicle.

Provides controls for sorting and view-mode selection for the recommended products section. Updates URL parameters when the user changes sort or view and notifies the recommended products grid to re-render.

Displays the list of recommended products from getVehicleFitmentOverview.recommendedProducts, ordered and formatted according to the current sort and view state. Each product entry links to product.html with id and ref parameters.

Handles the case where getVehicleFitmentOverview returns no categories and no recommended products. Informs the user that no compatible products were found for the current vehicle and provides a navigation option to select a different vehicle.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page plugs into the existing site frame: full-width glossy header band with logo and social on top, then the horizontal navigation band, then (site-wide) hero. The Vehicle Fitment Results content begins directly beneath that hero as another full-width band that contains a centered fixed-width content container (~960–1024px). Inside this container, components are arranged as stacked, visually distinct modules separated by subtle horizontal rules and gradient shifts. First comes a high-impact vehicle-summary bar, then a two-column content area where the left column carries the recommended-products-toolbar and recommended-products-grid, and the right column carries the fitment-category-list as a stacked sidebar. Message panels (no-selected-vehicle-state and fitment-empty-state) override or replace the two-column layout when shown, occupying the central content area as primary modules.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page plugs into the existing site frame: full-width glossy header band with logo and social on top, then the horizontal navigation band, then (site-wide) hero. The Vehicle Fitment Results content begins directly beneath that hero as another full-width band that contains a centered fixed-width content container (~960–1024px). Inside this container, components are arranged as stacked, visually distinct modules separated by subtle horizontal rules and gradient shifts. First comes a high-impact vehicle-summary bar, then a two-column content area where the left column carries the recommended-products-toolbar and recommended-products-grid, and the right column carries the fitment-category-list as a stacked sidebar. Message panels (no-selected-vehicle-state and fitment-empty-state) override or replace the two-column layout when shown, occupying the central content area as primary modules.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the global header/nav/hero, the page-specific content occupies the remaining 70–80% of the viewport height above the fold. The vehicle-summary-bar takes the first 15–20% of that page-specific space as a strong horizontal strip. The remaining area is a two-column layout: the left column occupies about 70% of the centered content width and is dedicated to the toolbar and product grid; the right column takes about 30% and is dedicated to the category list. Vertical spacing between these major bands is around 24–32px, and each card-like module uses 12–16px internal padding. Empty and no-vehicle states take over the central 60–70% of the content width and sit roughly in the middle of the visible content area, pushing other components offscreen or hiding them.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is moderate-to-high, echoing the original mockup. There is minimal blank background; nearly every horizontal band (vehicle summary, toolbar, product area, sidebar) uses a gradient or textured background and a bordered card feel. Text blocks are short and punchy—titles, labels, and key data points—while imagery and buttons dominate. The grid shows multiple products above the fold (e.g., 2–3 columns x 2–3 rows in the left column), with tight but readable spacing (8–16px gutters). The sidebar category list is a vertical stack of compact category tiles with small thumbnails or icons and bold labels, separated by 8–12px. Message panels follow the same glossy, carded treatment and fill much of the central area so the page still feels visually rich even when little data is present.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The site-wide flow starts top-left at the logo, sweeps right through the nav, then drops into the hero. From the hero, the eye moves straight down to the vehicle-summary-bar, which acts like a mini-hero for the selected vehicle, using strong contrast and possibly a background image strip. From there, the gaze follows a mild Z-pattern across the page-specific content: first across the summary band, then down into the left column with the toolbar (sorting/filtering the recommendations) and immediately into the product grid. The strong imagery and call-to-action buttons in the grid anchor attention. The right sidebar is discovered on the second pass as the user scans rightward; category tiles act like vertical tabs for navigating between fitment categories. When no vehicle or no fitment data is available, the visual flow is interrupted intentionally by a centered, high-contrast message panel that dominates the content area, with a clear primary action that leads the user back into a valid flow.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the glossy site header, navigation, and motion-heavy hero, the Vehicle Fitment Results page begins with a bold, full-width vehicle summary band that spans the entire centered content container. It sits on a saturated gradient background—greens and yellows echoing the brand—with a beveled, card-like foreground where the currently selected vehicle details are highlighted on the left and key actions (change vehicle, edit) sit on the right. Below this band, the layout breaks into a classic two-column body: a wide left column (around 70% width) and a narrower right column (around 30%), both sharing the same horizontal background strip so they feel like a single, dense section. In the left column, a glossy toolbar strip runs across the top, containing view controls and sort options, and directly beneath it the recommended-products-grid fills the remainder of the column, stacking product tiles in 2–3 columns with strong product imagery and bright call-to-action buttons. On the right, the fitment-category-list forms a tall, vertically stacked sidebar, each category appearing as a compact, high-contrast tile with subtle gradient panels, icons, and bold labels. Everything is tightly spaced with 8–16px gaps, and thin borders or glow effects separate modules rather than large white gaps. If no vehicle is selected, the two-column content disappears, replaced by a large, centered message panel inviting the user to select a vehicle. If a vehicle is selected but no products fit, the left column’s grid area is taken over by a similarly styled, prominent empty-state card. The overall effect is energetic, marketing-heavy, and visually guided: from header and hero into the vehicle summary, then down and left into actionable recommendations, with categories framing the experience from the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init-controller is not visually represented but governs which of the visual components appear in the main content area. It sits conceptually above the page-specific content region that begins just below the global hero. When no vehicle is selected, it ensures that only the no-selected-vehicle-state panel occupies the central content container, keeping the vehicle-summary-bar, fitment-category-list, recommended-products-toolbar, recommended-products-grid, and fitment-empty-state hidden. When a vehicle is selected, it reveals the vehicle-summary-bar at the top of the content container and the two-column layout below. If, for a selected vehicle and category, there are no recommended products, it replaces the recommended-products-grid within the left column with the fitment-empty-state message panel while preserving the toolbar and category sidebar. Because it is logical rather than visual, it does not consume any layout space; it simply orchestrates which bands and modules are rendered in the existing grid and bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; no spatial behavior. It conditions visibility of other components within the fixed-width, center-aligned content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-selected-vehicle-state appears in the main content region immediately beneath the global hero and below any site-wide page title, occupying the central 60–70% of the content width and centered horizontally within the 960–1024px container. Vertically, it is positioned with approximately 64px of space below the hero band, so it feels like the next strong step in the vertical flow. The panel itself is a large, glossy card with a subtle dark-to-light vertical gradient and a thin, glowing border. It has 24px padding on all sides, with a bold heading at the top, a concise explanatory line beneath, and a prominent button (e.g., 'Select Your Vehicle') centered or right-aligned near the bottom. There is modest vertical breathing room (24–32px) above and below the card, filled by the page background gradient rather than blank white. While this state is visible, the vehicle-summary-bar and the two-column area (fitment-category-list, toolbar, grid, and fitment-empty-state) are not rendered, so the message panel reads as the primary focal module in the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered card within the content container. It does not scroll independently; as the user scrolls, it moves with the page like other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The vehicle-summary-bar is a full-width horizontal band sitting at the very top of the page-specific content area, directly below the hero banner and any page title, and above the two-column body. It spans the entire 960–1024px centered content container, with 16–24px of vertical padding inside. The bar sits on a saturated gradient strip that extends edge-to-edge of the browser window, while the content is constrained to the centered grid, echoing the glossy header and nav treatments. Inside the band, the left two-thirds are devoted to vehicle identity: a bold, high-contrast label for the vehicle year/make/model in large text, with secondary details (trim, body style) beneath in smaller text. The right third contains interactive elements—buttons like 'Change Vehicle' or a small dropdown, and perhaps an icon representing the vehicle—right-aligned but vertically centered. A faint inner glow or bevel effect separates this band from the background, and a thin horizontal rule or shift in gradient marks its bottom edge. Vertically, there is about 24px of space between the bottom edge of this bar and the start of the two-column content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves like a sticky sub-header: optionally, on scroll, it can remain fixed at the top of the viewport beneath the global nav, maintaining a constant reminder of the selected vehicle while the user scrolls through categories and products.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fitment-category-list resides in the right-hand sidebar of the two-column body that starts beneath the vehicle-summary-bar. The two-column container is centered and matches the overall content width; within it, the right column occupies roughly 30% of that width. The category list begins at the same vertical baseline as the recommended-products-toolbar in the left column, creating a clean horizontal alignment across the two columns. Each category appears as a compact, card-like tile spanning the full width of the sidebar column, with 12–16px internal padding and 8–12px vertical spacing between tiles. Tiles use subtle horizontal gradients, beveled edges, and possibly a small icon or thumbnail on the left with the category name in bold, uppercase text on the right, maintaining the marketing-heavy aesthetic. The list scrolls with the page as a single column; if it is taller than the viewport, the user scrolls the entire page to see additional categories. There is 16–24px of padding between the outer edge of the sidebar and the container boundary, and a light vertical rule or shadow separates the sidebar from the left main column, reinforcing the two-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar within the right column of the two-column layout; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not float or stick independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommended-products-toolbar sits at the top of the left main column directly beneath the vehicle-summary-bar and aligned horizontally with the top of the fitment-category-list on the right. It spans the full width of the left column, which occupies about 70% of the centered content container. The toolbar is a low but visually strong strip (around 48–56px tall) with a glossy, high-contrast gradient background that distinguishes it from the lighter grid area below. On the left side of the toolbar, there is a bold label like 'Recommended Products' along with a brief count or sublabel; on the right side, controls such as sort dropdowns, view toggles, or filter pills are right-aligned, all within about 12–16px of horizontal padding. A subtle inner shadow at the bottom edge indicates that content (the grid) sits directly below it. There is minimal vertical space (8–12px) between the bottom of the toolbar and the top of the recommended-products-grid, ensuring a tight, integrated look consistent with the dense visual language.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts like a local sub-header for the product area; it may optionally become sticky within the left column when the user scrolls past the initial fold, staying pinned beneath the vehicle-summary-bar so that sort and view controls remain accessible while the grid scrolls below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommended-products-grid occupies the remainder of the left main column beneath the recommended-products-toolbar. It extends the full width of the left column (about 70% of the content container) with 16px of inner padding around the grid area. The grid itself is arranged in 2–3 product tiles per row depending on available width, with consistent horizontal gutters of approximately 16px and vertical spacing of 24px between rows. Each product tile is a glossy, carded module featuring a dominant product image at the top (taking about the upper half to two-thirds of the card), with the product name, a few fitment-related bullet points, and a strong call-to-action button ('View Details', 'See Fitment') below. Cards use gradients, bevels, and glow effects to match the early 2010s consumer electronics aesthetic, and the images and buttons are saturated and high-contrast to draw attention. The grid stretches vertically down the page; below the last row, there is 32–48px of space before any subsequent section or footer. When populated, this grid is the main focus of the two-column region, visually outweighing the sidebar due to its width, imagery, and density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, scrolling naturally with the page. Its top is anchored immediately beneath the toolbar; when the toolbar (and vehicle-summary-bar, if sticky) are pinned, only the grid content scrolls under them.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fitment-empty-state appears in place of the recommended-products-grid when a vehicle is selected but there are no compatible products for the current category or selection. It resides in the same left main column beneath the recommended-products-toolbar, spanning the same width (about 70% of the content container) and aligned to the same left margin. The card uses a high-contrast, glossy treatment—a dark-to-light gradient panel with a bright accent border—to stand out in the grid area. It has generous padding (24px) and occupies a moderate vertical height (roughly equivalent to 2–3 rows of product cards), giving it substantial visual weight. The content is centrally aligned within the card: a bold headline communicates that no products fit this vehicle/category, followed by a short explanatory line and one or two strong action buttons, such as 'View All Compatible Categories' or 'Change Vehicle'. There is 24–32px of space between the bottom of this card and the next section or the page footer, maintaining the sense of a distinct module. The right sidebar fitment-category-list remains visible beside it, so users can pivot into other categories even in this empty state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static panel occupying the left column’s product area. It replaces the product grid within that region while active and scrolls with the page like other content.

name: Wishlist
title: Wishlist
description: Page where users can view, manage, and move their saved car audio products between the wishlist and shopping cart.

core_features: Load and display all wishlist items for the current user using getWishlistItems, including associated product information.
Allow navigation from any wishlist product (image or name) to its product detail page via product.html with URL parameters.
Allow users to move a wishlist item directly into the cart and remove it from the wishlist using moveWishlistItemToCart.
Allow users to add a wishlist product to the cart while keeping it in the wishlist using addToCart.
Allow users to remove individual items from the wishlist using removeFromWishlist.
Support selection of quantities for each wishlist item before moving to cart or adding to cart.
Provide optional bulk actions to move or remove multiple selected wishlist items by calling the appropriate interfaces per item.
Offer a navigation control to open cart.html (View Cart) after cart-related actions.
user_workflows: View wishlist items: (1) User navigates to wishlist.html. (2) On page initialization, the wishlist-page-loader component calls getWishlistItems with no parameters. (3) The response wishlist and items are stored in page state. (4) The wishlist-item-list component renders each item using its WishlistItem and Product data. (5) The wishlist-summary component shows a count of items based on the loaded list. (6) If no items are returned, the wishlist-empty-state component displays a message and relevant navigation options.

Open product detail from wishlist: (1) User locates a product in the wishlist-item-list view. (2) User clicks the product image or product name inside wishlist-item-row. (3) The click handler reads the productId from the bound Product entity. (4) The browser navigates to product.html with URL parameters id set to the productId and ref set to wishlist (e.g., product.html?id={productId}&ref=wishlist).

Move a single wishlist item to cart: (1) User finds an item in the wishlist-item-row. (2) User optionally adjusts the quantity using the quantity-selector control for that row. (3) User clicks the Move to Cart button within that row. (4) The row-level handler calls moveWishlistItemToCart with parameters wishlistItemId (from WishlistItem.id) and quantity (from the quantity selector, defaulting to 1 if the user has not changed it). (5) On success, the response cart and cartItem are used to update any cart indicators on the site (outside this page, if present), and the wishlist-page-loader triggers a refresh by calling getWishlistItems again to get the updated wishlist items. (6) The wishlist-notification-bar shows a success message that a product has been moved to the cart, and the URL is updated to wishlist.html?lastAction=moveToCart&lastItemId={wishlistItemId}. (7) The wishlist-cart-navigation component displays or emphasizes the View Cart button so the user can go to cart.html if desired.

Add a wishlist item to cart while keeping it in the wishlist: (1) User finds an item in the wishlist-item-row. (2) User optionally adjusts quantity via the quantity-selector. (3) User clicks the Add to Cart button within that row. (4) The handler calls addToCart with productId (from Product.id), quantity (from the quantity-selector, defaulting to 1), and protectionPlanId set to null (or omitted) since no protection plan is selected on this page. (5) On success, the wishlist state remains unchanged because addToCart does not modify the wishlist. (6) The wishlist-notification-bar displays a success message using the returned cart and addedItem data. (7) The URL is updated to wishlist.html?lastAction=addToCart&lastProductId={productId} so the notification can be restored on page reload if needed.

Remove a single item from the wishlist: (1) User finds an item in the wishlist-item-row. (2) User clicks the Remove link or button on that row. (3) The handler calls removeFromWishlist with wishlistItemId (from WishlistItem.id). (4) On success, the response items array replaces the existing wishlist items in page state. (5) The wishlist-item-list re-renders based on the updated items. (6) The wishlist-notification-bar shows a success message, and the URL may be updated to wishlist.html?lastAction=remove&lastItemId={wishlistItemId}.

Bulk move multiple wishlist items to cart: (1) User checks one or more selection checkboxes in multiple wishlist-item-row components. (2) User clicks the bulk Move Selected to Cart action in the wishlist-bulk-actions toolbar. (3) The bulk handler reads all selected wishlistItemIds and the associated quantities from each row. (4) For each selected item, the handler calls moveWishlistItemToCart with the corresponding wishlistItemId and quantity. (5) After all calls resolve, the wishlist-page-loader either filters removed items from local state or calls getWishlistItems again to synchronize the list. (6) The wishlist-notification-bar shows a summary message indicating how many items were successfully moved and, where applicable, how many failed, using internal error-tracking state. (7) The URL is updated to wishlist.html?lastAction=bulkMove&movedCount={count}.

Bulk remove multiple wishlist items: (1) User checks one or more selection checkboxes in multiple wishlist-item-row components. (2) User clicks the bulk Remove Selected action in the wishlist-bulk-actions toolbar. (3) The handler confirms the action with the user (e.g., via a confirmation dialog). (4) For each selected wishlistItemId, the handler calls removeFromWishlist. (5) After all calls complete, the page either recomputes the in-memory item list based on successful removals or calls getWishlistItems once more to reload the full updated list. (6) The wishlist-notification-bar displays a summary message with the number of items removed. (7) The URL is updated to wishlist.html?lastAction=bulkRemove&removedCount={count}.

View cart after wishlist operations: (1) After any successful addToCart or moveWishlistItemToCart action, the wishlist-cart-navigation component may highlight or render a View Cart button. (2) User clicks View Cart. (3) The browser navigates directly to cart.html with no additional parameters as defined. (4) The cart page then uses its own logic to load and display the cart corresponding to the updated Cart entity.
interactions: Page load interaction: When the DOM is ready for wishlist.html, the wishlist-page-loader component fires its onInit event, which calls getWishlistItems with no parameters. The returned wishlist and items array populate the shared state used by all wishlist-related components.

Item image click: In each wishlist-item-row, the product image has an onClick handler that constructs a URL of the form product.html?id={Product.id}&ref=wishlist and navigates the browser to that URL.
Product name click: In each wishlist-item-row, the product name is a clickable element with the same behavior as the image, navigating to product.html with id and ref query parameters.

Quantity change interaction: Each wishlist-item-row contains a quantity-selector with an onChange handler. When the user changes the quantity, the new value is stored in row-level state and used as the quantity parameter for subsequent addToCart or moveWishlistItemToCart calls for that row.

Move to Cart button click: Each wishlist-item-row exposes a Move to Cart button with an onClick handler. The handler reads the bound WishlistItem.id and the current quantity, then calls moveWishlistItemToCart. On success, it triggers a state refresh (either by filtering out the moved item or by calling getWishlistItems), updates the notification bar, and modifies the URL query string to encode the last action.

Add to Cart button click: Each wishlist-item-row exposes an Add to Cart button with an onClick handler that reads Product.id and quantity from the row, then calls addToCart with those values and a null or omitted protectionPlanId. On successful response, it updates cart-related UI (if present) and triggers a success notification without altering the wishlist items.

Remove button click: Each wishlist-item-row has a Remove action with an onClick handler that calls removeFromWishlist with the bound WishlistItem.id. On success, it replaces the current items state with the response.items array and re-renders the wishlist list.

Item selection checkbox interaction: Each wishlist-item-row contains a checkbox for selection used by bulk actions. Its onChange handler updates a collection of selected wishlistItemIds in a shared state object managed by wishlist-bulk-actions.

Bulk Move Selected interaction: The wishlist-bulk-actions toolbar has a Move Selected to Cart button with an onClick handler. The handler iterates the selected wishlistItemIds and their stored quantities, invokes moveWishlistItemToCart for each, then refreshes the state, updates the notification bar with a summary, and adjusts the URL parameters to reflect a bulk move action.

Bulk Remove Selected interaction: The wishlist-bulk-actions toolbar has a Remove Selected button with an onClick handler that may first request user confirmation. Upon confirmation, it calls removeFromWishlist for each selected wishlistItemId, then updates the wishlist items state based on the results and updates the notification bar and URL to reflect a bulk removal.

View Cart button interaction: The wishlist-cart-navigation component contains a View Cart button that is always available but may be visually emphasized after cart actions. Its onClick handler simply navigates to cart.html without calling any API interfaces.

Notification interaction: The wishlist-notification-bar reads URL parameters such as lastAction, lastItemId, lastProductId, movedCount, or removedCount during initialization. It uses these values and internal state to display relevant success or error messages. Closing or dismissing a notification may trigger an update to the URL to remove or reset these query parameters.

Sorting or view-mode change interaction (optional): If present, toolbar controls for sorting or changing the view mode have onChange handlers that update the URL parameters (e.g., sort, view) and re-render the list based on the current in-memory items from getWishlistItems, without additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Wishlist page relies on URL query parameters and in-memory state derived from the getWishlistItems, addToCart, removeFromWishlist, and moveWishlistItemToCart interfaces. The main data state is the current wishlist (Wishlist entity), its items (WishlistItem entities), and the associated products (Product entities) loaded from getWishlistItems on page initialization and subsequent refreshes. URL parameters are used to represent view state and contextual feedback rather than to drive server queries. The following URL parameters are recognized and interpreted purely on the client:
- lastAction: Encodes the most recent operation affecting the wishlist or cart, such as addToCart, moveToCart, remove, bulkMove, or bulkRemove. On page load, wishlist-notification-bar reads this parameter and shows an appropriate message; dismissing the notification may trigger a URL update that removes or clears lastAction.
- lastItemId: Optionally stores the identifier of the affected WishlistItem for actions like moveToCart or remove. This can be used to highlight or scroll to the relevant row when the page is reloaded, as long as that item still exists in the current items list.
- lastProductId: Optionally stores the Product.id related to an addToCart action to support contextual notifications or highlighting.
- movedCount and removedCount: Used for bulk actions to show how many items were moved or removed during the last bulk operation. These are read on initialization by wishlist-notification-bar and do not affect server calls.
- sort: Represents the client-side sorting preference for displaying wishlist items (e.g., by price, rating, or name). On page load or when this parameter changes, wishlist-item-list sorts the in-memory array of items returned by getWishlistItems. The parameter is not sent to any interface and does not alter backend behavior.
- view: Represents the client-side view mode (e.g., list or compact). On page load or change, the wishlist-item-list and related components adjust their rendering logic accordingly using this value.
Navigation to other pages also uses URL parameters: navigating to product.html uses id set to the Product.id and ref set to wishlist; navigating to cart.html uses no additional parameters as specified. The page does not use localStorage or other persistent client storage; all state beyond the URL is maintained in memory for the life of the page session and refreshed as needed from the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Initializes and refreshes the wishlist page data by calling getWishlistItems and disseminating the returned wishlist, items, and associated product data to other components. It also listens for an external refresh signal (for example, after bulk operations) to reload data.

Displays a concise summary of the wishlist, such as the total number of items and indication of whether any items are selected for bulk actions. It updates automatically when the underlying items list changes.

Renders the collection of wishlist items as a list or grid of wishlist-item-row components. It applies client-side sorting and view-mode preferences based on the sort and view URL parameters without additional API calls.

Represents a single wishlist entry, showing product details and controls. Allows navigation to product details, quantity selection, moving the item to cart (removing from wishlist), adding the product to cart while keeping it in the wishlist, selecting the item for bulk actions, and removing it from the wishlist.

Manages bulk operations over multiple selected wishlist items. Aggregates selected wishlistItemIds and quantities, calls moveWishlistItemToCart for bulk move operations and removeFromWishlist for bulk removal, then triggers page data refresh and updates URL parameters for feedback.

Provides a numeric input for setting the desired quantity for a wishlist item. The selected quantity is used as the quantity parameter for subsequent addToCart or moveWishlistItemToCart calls originating from the same row.

Displays success or error messages for actions such as moving items to cart, adding items to cart, or removing items from the wishlist. It reads URL parameters like lastAction, lastItemId, lastProductId, movedCount, and removedCount on initialization to restore notification context and can update the URL if notifications are dismissed.

Provides a View Cart button and optionally contextual text indicating that items have recently been added or moved to the cart. Clicking the button navigates the user to cart.html without invoking any API.

Displays when getWishlistItems returns no items. Informs the user that the wishlist is empty and provides navigation options, such as links to product listing or search pages, using normal anchor navigation without API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: The wishlist page plugs into the existing fixed-width, center-aligned layout with full-width header, navigation, and hero banner above it. The wishlist-specific content starts as a distinct band below the hero. Inside the central 960–1024px-wide container, the page uses the same 2-column structure seen in the mockup: a dominant left column (~70% width) for the wishlist summary and item list, and a narrower right column (~30% width) for supporting controls (wishlist-to-cart navigation). The notification bar appears as a slim, glossy horizontal band just above the wishlist content, spanning the full browser width but aligning its text to the central container. Within the left column, the layout is linear: wishlist summary on top, followed by the product list, with bulk actions pinned to the bottom of that column. Item rows are stacked vertically inside a visually unified card-like panel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The wishlist page plugs into the existing fixed-width, center-aligned layout with full-width header, navigation, and hero banner above it. The wishlist-specific content starts as a distinct band below the hero. Inside the central 960–1024px-wide container, the page uses the same 2-column structure seen in the mockup: a dominant left column (~70% width) for the wishlist summary and item list, and a narrower right column (~30% width) for supporting controls (wishlist-to-cart navigation). The notification bar appears as a slim, glossy horizontal band just above the wishlist content, spanning the full browser width but aligning its text to the central container. Within the left column, the layout is linear: wishlist summary on top, followed by the product list, with bulk actions pinned to the bottom of that column. Item rows are stacked vertically inside a visually unified card-like panel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Header + navigation + hero collectively occupy roughly the top 60% of the initial viewport, consistent with the mockup’s emphasis on branding and product imagery. The remaining ~40% of the above-the-fold area is allocated to the wishlist: the notification bar uses a slim band (about 8–10% of this wishlist area), the wishlist summary consumes a shallow but prominent strip (about 15–20%), and the rest is dominated by the main content split into a 70/30 two-column layout. Within the left column, the item list takes almost all the vertical space, with each item row roughly 96–120px tall and separated by tight 8–12px gaps. The right column is a tall, vertically stacked card for wishlist-cart-navigation that aligns to the top of the item list and may remain visible as the list scrolls. Horizontal spacing follows the mockup’s tight-but-readable pattern: standard section gaps are around 24–32px, and internal card padding is around 12–16px on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains the mockup’s moderate-to-high density: nearly every area is visually treated with gradients, borders, or glossy surfaces. The wishlist summary, notification bar, item list container, and sidebar navigation card all appear as distinct modules with strong backgrounds and thin borders or glow edges. Text blocks are brief—headlines, counts, short status messages—while product images, buttons, and labels dominate. Padding inside modules sticks to the 12–16px range to keep content legible but compact. Vertical stacking is tight: the notification bar sits close under the hero with only a small 16–24px gap, and the wishlist summary sits closely under the notification bar, followed immediately by the two-column content with minimal whitespace between sections.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Within the global page flow (logo → nav → hero), the wishlist section establishes its own mini Z-pattern. The eye drops from the hero into the bright, full-width notification bar, reading left-to-right across the message. It then continues downward into the wishlist summary panel, starting at the top-left section title and item count, then scanning rightward to any totals or key stats. Below that, the layout splits: the main eye path flows down the larger left column through the vertical stack of product cards, scanning each row from left to right (image → product details → price and quantity → actions). In parallel, the sidebar card on the right stays in peripheral vision, pulling occasional attention to the cart navigation and primary CTA button(s). At the bottom of the left column, the bulk action toolbar forms a strong horizontal band that catches the eye as a final control area, encouraging global actions before users leave the page.

layout.overall_description: Below the glossy hero banner, a slim, bright notification band stretches across the full width of the viewport, its green-yellow gradient and small icon immediately signaling status about the wishlist. Inside the centered content container, the wishlist section begins with a wide, beveled summary panel that spans almost the entire content width, giving a quick snapshot of how many products are saved and any key messaging.
