Directly beneath this summary, the content splits into two columns: on the left, a darker, high-contrast panel fills about 70% of the width, housing a stacked list of wishlist items with product photos on the left, specs and titles in the middle, and price, quantity controls, and action buttons on the right. On the right, a tall, glossy sidebar card takes up the remaining 30% width, offering cart navigation and checkout-related CTAs, visually tethered to the main list but standing out via stronger glow and gradient. As users scroll, the list items slide up within their container while the bottom-aligned bulk action band stays visually anchored in the left column, forming a strong horizontal control strip to move or remove multiple items at once. When there are no items, the entire two-column structure collapses into a single, centered empty-state panel with a large icon, friendly message, and bold call-to-action, still embedded in the same glossy, gradient-rich background that ties back to the rest of the site.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-page-loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay confined to the main content region beneath the hero, not covering the global header or navigation. It spans the full width of the centered content container (roughly 960–1024px) and extends vertically from just under the hero/notification bar down to just above the footer area. The overlay uses a dark, slightly transparent gradient (top slightly lighter than bottom) that lets the underlying wishlist modules faintly show through, in keeping with the glossy, layered style. At its absolute center, a circular, chrome-like loading indicator (about 64–80px in diameter) is displayed, surrounded by a soft outer glow, with a short uppercase status label just below it. The loader dims all wishlist components (summary, list, sidebar, bulk actions) equally and sits above them in stacking order so no controls can be interacted with while it is visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolutely positioned overlay within the main content container, centered both horizontally and vertically, covering the wishlist area while allowing header/navigation to remain visible; it is non-scrolling and remains fixed in place relative to the viewport while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-summary is the first visible wishlist-specific panel under the hero and notification bar. It sits in the center-aligned content container, spanning nearly the full width of that container (approximately 90–95% of the 960–1024px content width) with equal left and right margins. Vertically, it occupies a shallow strip about 96–128px tall, with 16–20px of padding on all sides. The panel has a bright, metallic gradient background (e.g., dark-to-light green or green-to-yellow) with a subtle bevel and a thin highlight border to echo the mockup’s card style. Inside, the content is arranged horizontally: on the left two-thirds, a bold uppercase title like “YOUR WISHLIST” and the total item count are stacked with a short descriptive line below; on the right third, condensed summary stats (e.g., estimated total, number of items available) are right-aligned, possibly separated by a faint vertical divider line. The bottom edge of this panel aligns horizontally with the top edges of the two-column area below, creating a clean visual step into the main content bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level panel anchored at the top of the wishlist content stack; it scrolls away with the page and does not remain fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-item-list occupies the left main column beneath the wishlist-summary. It starts about 16–24px below the summary’s bottom edge and runs downward, taking roughly 70% of the available content width. Its left edge aligns with the left edge of the summary panel’s content, while its right edge roughly aligns with a vertical column separation line that leaves the remaining 30% width for the sidebar. The list is contained within a single, dark, glossy panel that stretches the full height of the column, with a subtle inner shadow and 12–16px padding on the left and right to frame the item rows. Each wishlist-item-row is stacked vertically within this panel, separated by 8–12px gaps, and the panel’s top and bottom edges are clearly defined with a beveled border to distinguish it from the background gradient of the page. The bottom of the panel is visually connected to the wishlist-bulk-actions toolbar, which either appears as the panel’s last band or as an attached strip immediately below it, maintaining the sense of one cohesive module in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, filling available vertical space and scrolling with the page; no sticky behavior, but its bottom edge may align with a sticky bulk action bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each wishlist-item-row is a horizontally oriented product card inside the wishlist-item-list panel. It spans nearly the full internal width of the list, leaving 12–16px of inset padding on the left and right from the list’s container edge. Vertically, each row is about 96–120px tall, with 12–16px padding on all sides, giving enough room for a medium-sized product photo and a couple of lines of text. The row background is a slightly lighter dark gradient than the list container, with a subtle top highlight and bottom shadow to create a beveled, pressed-in look. On the left ~30% of the row width sits the product image, framed in a glossy, metallic border that aligns vertically centered in the row. The central ~40–45% of the row is dedicated to text: product name in bold at the top, truncated if necessary, with 1–2 short lines of key specs or features below. On the right ~25–30%, the price is positioned at the top-right, large and high-contrast; directly beneath it sits the quantity-selector, and underneath or to the right of that, a prominent action button (e.g., “MOVE TO CART”) aligned to the row’s right edge. Secondary actions (remove, save for later) are smaller links placed near the bottom-right corner. All content in the row is left-to-right aligned, supporting quick scanning along the horizontal axis.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block elements stacked vertically inside the list; they do not have independent scrolling or sticky behavior and inherit scrolling from the parent list container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-bulk-actions toolbar forms a strong, horizontal band at the bottom of the wishlist-item-list area. It spans the full width of the left column (matching the list’s width) and is about 56–72px tall, with 12–16px vertical padding. Visually, it has a brighter, more saturated gradient (e.g., yellow-green or lime) than the list background, with a pronounced top highlight that makes it feel like a raised control bar. Its top border aligns exactly with the bottom edge of the last wishlist-item-row, and the two are separated by a narrow 4–8px gap or a faint dividing line. Within the bar, the main bulk action controls (e.g., select-all checkbox, “Move selected to cart”, “Remove selected”) are left-aligned in a row, while a compact helper message or secondary link (e.g., “Clear selection”) is right-aligned. This bar visually caps the left column, acting as the final control band users see after scanning downward through their items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially positioned directly under the list, it can become sticky relative to the viewport on scroll: once the user scrolls past its original position, it remains pinned to the bottom edge of the viewport within the left column area, so that bulk actions stay accessible even when the list is tall.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity-selector is a compact control embedded within the right-hand side of each wishlist-item-row, directly beneath or beside the price. It occupies roughly one-third of the right segment of the row, about 96–128px in width, and 32–40px in height. The control uses the same glossy, beveled styling as other inputs: a dark metallic capsule or small rectangular field with subtle inner shadow, containing either a numeric value with up/down arrows stacked on the right, or plus/minus buttons flanking the number. It is aligned vertically with the primary action button (e.g., “MOVE TO CART”) so that both controls form a tight vertical cluster, making it visually obvious that quantity and action are related. The selector is right-aligned to the row’s inner padding edge, leaving a small 8–12px gap between it and the main button or stacking it directly above that button.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline control within each item row; it does not move independently and participates in the row’s standard layout and scrolling behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-notification-bar appears as a slim, full-width band directly below the global hero or immediately beneath the main navigation if the hero is minimized on this page. It stretches edge-to-edge across the browser window, but its text and icons are constrained within the same centered 960–1024px content area as the rest of the page. Vertically, it is about 40–56px tall, with 8–12px of padding and a strong, high-contrast gradient (e.g., bright green to yellow) that echoes promotional banners from the mockup. An icon or status badge is left-aligned within the container, followed by a concise message about recent wishlist actions (e.g., “Item moved to your cart”) and possibly a small inline link (like “View cart”). The right side of the bar may feature a dismiss or close icon, vertically centered. The bar sits tightly above the wishlist-summary, with only a small 8–16px gap or a thin dividing rule separating them, creating the sensation of stacked, energetic bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears as needed; it is positioned statically in the document flow so content below it shifts when it is shown or hidden. It may slide down into view when triggered but does not remain sticky during scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-cart-navigation occupies the right-hand sidebar column starting at the same vertical level as the top of the wishlist-item-list. It takes up approximately 30% of the content container’s width, aligned flush to the right edge of the centered grid, with a small 16–24px horizontal gap separating it from the left column. The card is vertically oriented and about as wide as a medium panel; it may extend down beyond the fold depending on its content. It features a bright, glossy gradient (darker at the top, lighter at the bottom) with a solid bevel and a thin glow at the edges, making it more visually punchy than the darker list container. Inside, a clear heading (“READY TO CHECK OUT?” or “YOUR CART”) is placed near the top, followed by a compact summary of cart status and a large, high-contrast CTA button (e.g., “GO TO CART”) centered or right-aligned. Additional secondary links (e.g., “Continue shopping”, “View all cart items”) are stacked below in smaller type. The card’s top aligns horizontally with the first item row in the left column, creating a balanced visual row across the two columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky sidebar element within the right column: as the user scrolls down the wishlist, the card stays pinned within the viewport (with a small top offset below the header/notification), ensuring that cart navigation options remain persistently visible alongside the scrolling item list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the wishlist is empty, the wishlist-empty-state replaces the usual left-column item list and visually simplifies the two-column layout. The empty-state panel appears centered within the content container beneath the wishlist-summary (or directly beneath the notification bar if the summary is omitted in this mode). It spans about 60–70% of the content width, leaving generous but still gradient-filled margins on either side, and is slightly taller than a typical summary card, around 200–260px. The panel uses a mid-tone gradient background—lighter and more inviting than the list’s dark panel—with a soft glow and a subtle icon or illustration (such as an empty car audio slot or stylized wishlist icon) centered in the upper half. Below the icon, a bold, friendly headline (e.g., “YOUR WISHLIST IS EMPTY”) is centrally aligned, followed by a brief line of guidance text and a primary button (“BROWSE PRODUCTS” or “START SHOPPING”) centered horizontally. The right sidebar (wishlist-cart-navigation) can either be hidden or displayed in a minimized state, but visually, the main focus is this central panel, which sits as the dominant block in the area that was previously a 2-column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that replaces the left-column list in the layout; it scrolls with the page like other panels. When active, it suppresses or visually de-emphasizes the bulk actions and item list to keep the layout clean and focused on the call-to-action.

name: About
title: About
description: Informational page that loads and displays CMS-managed content about the car audio store, and offers navigation back to shopping (notably to the Car Audio category).

core_features: Load About page content from the CMS using the getPageContent interface and render the returned title and bodyHtml.
Display informational content about the store, its mission, expertise, and car audio focus entirely from dynamic CMS content (no hardcoded text).
Expose a primary call-to-action that sends users to the car audio shopping category (category.html) with the appropriate categoryId URL parameter.
Optionally support deep-linking to specific sections within the About content via URL parameters or fragment identifiers, and adjust the scroll position accordingly.
Show metadata such as the content last updated date using lastUpdated from the getPageContent response.
Handle loading and error states for the getPageContent call, with a retry interaction if content fails to load.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial visit to About
1. User navigates to about.html via footer navigation or by entering the URL.
2. On page load, the About page determines the appropriate pageCode value based on the current route or configuration.
3. The page calls getPageContent with that pageCode.
4. While waiting for the response, the page shows a loading state.
5. When getPageContent returns successfully, the page stores title, bodyHtml, and lastUpdated in its internal state.
6. The page updates the browser tab/document title to match the returned title (if configured to do so) and renders bodyHtml in the content area.
7. If a section parameter or fragment is present in the URL, the page scrolls to the corresponding section within the rendered content.

Workflow: Handling load error and retry
1. User navigates to about.html.
2. The page calls getPageContent.
3. If getPageContent returns an error or cannot be reached, the page displays a non-blocking error message and hides the main content area.
4. The page presents a retry control (e.g., a button).
5. When the user activates the retry control, the page calls getPageContent again with the same pageCode.
6. On success, the error state is cleared and the content is rendered as in the initial workflow.

Workflow: Navigate to Car Audio shopping from About
1. User reads the About content loaded via getPageContent.
2. Within the visible page (either inside the CMS content or in a dedicated call-to-action area), the user sees a functional link or button labeled to indicate shopping for car audio products.
3. When the user activates this control, the page triggers navigation to category.html with a query string parameter categoryId set to "car_audio".
4. The browser loads category.html?categoryId=car_audio, where the category page uses its own logic to display the Car Audio category.

Workflow: Deep-link to a specific About section (optional)
1. Another page or an external source links to about.html with an additional URL parameter (for example, about.html?section=<sectionKey>) or fragment (for example, about.html#<sectionAnchor>). The exact key/anchor names are defined in or aligned with the CMS content.
2. The user opens this URL.
3. The page loads About content using getPageContent as usual.
4. After bodyHtml has been rendered, the page reads the section parameter and/or fragment from the URL.
5. If a matching section element or anchor is found within the rendered bodyHtml, the page programmatically scrolls to that element.
6. If no matching section is found, the page remains at the top, with no additional error message (to avoid coupling logic to specific static section names).
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: Browser loads about.html.
- Behavior: The page initializes internal state (loading=true, error=null, content=null). It determines a pageCode (for example, from the filename, route configuration, or a mapping) and calls getPageContent(pageCode). When the response arrives, it sets loading=false, stores the returned title, bodyHtml, and lastUpdated, and renders them.

Interaction: Retry loading content
- Trigger: User activates a 'Retry' control shown when the previous getPageContent call failed.
- Behavior: Clears the previous error state, sets loading=true, and calls getPageContent again with the same pageCode. On success, it replaces the error UI with the rendered content.

Interaction: Click 'Shop Car Audio' call-to-action
- Trigger: User clicks the dedicated Shop Car Audio link or button within the About page.
- Behavior: The page performs a client-side navigation (or standard link navigation) to category.html with the URL query parameter categoryId set to "car_audio". No additional API calls are required on the About page; the target category page handles product loading.

Interaction: In-page section navigation via URL
- Trigger: User lands on about.html with an additional query parameter or fragment referencing a section (e.g., a section key or anchor), or manually changes the URL fragment.
- Behavior: After content is loaded and rendered, the page inspects the URL for a section identifier. If a matching element or anchor exists within bodyHtml, the page scrolls to that element. If not, no action is taken beyond default rendering.

Interaction: Optional internal anchor clicks within content
- Trigger: User clicks an internal anchor link within bodyHtml (e.g., a link that points to another section of the same page).
- Behavior: Browser updates the fragment identifier in the URL and scrolls to the corresponding element. The page may, after scroll, re-read the fragment if needed, but no new API call is made.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About page state is driven primarily by the getPageContent result and the URL.

URL Handling:
- Base URL: about.html (no required query parameters).
- Optional query parameters:
  - section: An optional parameter that, if present, is interpreted as an internal key for a specific section of the About content. Its value is not hardcoded in the page logic; instead, the page attempts to match it to anchors or identifiable elements within the rendered bodyHtml (for example, by ID). If a match is found, the page scrolls to that element after content load.
- Optional fragment identifier:
  - A fragment (e.g., #someAnchor) may be used. If present, the browser default behavior scrolls to an element with the matching id or name. The page may additionally read this fragment after rendering to perform any necessary adjustments (e.g., offset calculations) without tying to specific static anchor names.

getPageContent State:
- pageCode determination: The page derives a pageCode suitable for getPageContent based on runtime context (such as the route name, filename, or a configuration mapping). This logic must not rely on hardcoded content strings beyond what is required to identify this page within the CMS configuration.
- On initial load:
  - loading = true
  - error = null
  - content = null
- After successful getPageContent(pageCode) response:
  - loading = false
  - error = null
  - content = { title, bodyHtml, lastUpdated } (exact structure mirrors API response)
  - The document title may be updated to content.title, if configured.
- On error from getPageContent:
  - loading = false
  - error = { type, message } (structure internal to the page; not hardcoded to specific text content)
  - content remains null or unchanged.

Navigation State:
- Outgoing navigation to category.html is parameterized and does not depend on additional state from this page. When the user activates the Shop Car Audio CTA, the page constructs a URL of the form category.html?categoryId=car_audio and triggers navigation.
- The About page does not maintain or modify cart, wishlist, vehicle selection, or any product-related state; it solely manages its content state and navigation based on URL and getPageContent.

components.functionality: On page initialization, determine the correct pageCode for this About page and call getPageContent with it. Manage loading, success, and error states and expose the resulting title, bodyHtml, and lastUpdated to child components.

Render the CMS-managed About content using the bodyHtml returned by getPageContent. Ensure that all links and internal anchors in bodyHtml function normally, and that the content updates whenever a new getPageContent response is received.
Synchronize the browser document title or visible page heading with the title value returned from getPageContent, ensuring that it reflects current CMS data without hardcoded text.

Display the lastUpdated metadata from getPageContent in an appropriate location (for example, as a small note). It formats and shows this value dynamically without hardcoding any date or label text tied to a specific value.

Observe the state of the getPageContent call and show or hide an error message and retry control when needed. It does not hardcode any content about the store; it only manages generic error UI and triggers reloads.

After bodyHtml is rendered, read the current URL for a section query parameter and/or fragment identifier. If it finds a matching element or anchor within the rendered content, it scrolls to that element. Reacts to URL changes (e.g., fragment changes) without requiring additional API calls.

Provide a functional call-to-action that, when activated, navigates the user from the About page to the car audio shopping category. It constructs a URL to category.html, setting the categoryId query parameter to "car_audio" as defined by the navigation architecture.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global header, nav, and hero banner, the About page content sits in the familiar fixed-width, center-aligned container that splits into a 70% left main column and a 30% right sidebar. The left column is reserved for the About title, last-updated metadata, and the rich text content block, all grouped inside a glossy, card-like panel that reads as the primary informational module. The right column visually mimics the 'Latest Products' sidebar from the mockup but repurposes it for page-level utilities: a vertical in-page navigation panel at the top and a prominent 'Shop Car Audio' call-to-action below it. Loading and error states occupy or overlay the left column content area, maintaining the same card proportions, while the title manager operates at document level, not as a visible element.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global header, nav, and hero banner, the About page content sits in the familiar fixed-width, center-aligned container that splits into a 70% left main column and a 30% right sidebar. The left column is reserved for the About title, last-updated metadata, and the rich text content block, all grouped inside a glossy, card-like panel that reads as the primary informational module. The right column visually mimics the 'Latest Products' sidebar from the mockup but repurposes it for page-level utilities: a vertical in-page navigation panel at the top and a prominent 'Shop Car Audio' call-to-action below it. Loading and error states occupy or overlay the left column content area, maintaining the same card proportions, while the title manager operates at document level, not as a visible element.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Within the central content container (roughly 960–1024px wide), the left main column occupies about 70% of the width, starting at the left edge of the container, while the right sidebar occupies about 30%, aligned flush to the right. Vertically, the About content block (title + metadata + rich text) consumes most of the area beneath the hero, extending at least one full viewport height on typical screens, with the sidebar column stretching to match its height visually. Padding inside all cards and panels follows the 12–16px range, with 16–24px used for the top of the About card to give the header and metadata more breathing room. Vertical separation between the hero band and the About content band, and between the About content band and any lower-page sections, is about 24–32px and visually marked by a change in gradient or a subtle horizontal rule instead of blank whitespace.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderate-to-high content density, echoing the mockup’s busy marketing feel. The About text is broken into short paragraphs with strong subheadings, and all primary surfaces (About card, sidebar panels, CTA button) sit on glossy or gradient backgrounds with no large empty gutters. Spacing follows the 8px scale but rarely exceeds 32px of empty space at once; most areas are filled with text, icons, buttons, or gradient treatments. The in-page navigation appears as a stack of compact but legible glossy items, and the CTA is large and visually loud, ensuring the utility elements feel as active and designed as the hero area.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows the established Z-pattern: after the global header and nav, focus drops into the hero banner and then down into the About module. Within this module, the eye lands first on the bold 'About' heading at the top-left of the main content card, then immediately catches the smaller last-updated metadata band aligned toward the right under the heading. From there, the eye travels down through the rich text body in the left column. After scanning some of the About copy, attention naturally shifts rightward to the sidebar: first to the vertical in-page navigation at the top (styled like a mini promo card), then down to the bright, high-contrast 'Shop Car Audio' CTA button. When loading or on error, the left card area is taken over by a centered state (spinner or error panel) that becomes the new focal point, while the sidebar remains visible but secondary.

layout.overall_description: Below the glossy, full-width header and navigation bands and just under the large hero banner, a centered content strip appears on a dark, subtly textured or gradient background. This strip is a fixed width, around 960–1024px, and is divided into a wider left column and a narrower right sidebar, continuing the two-column structure from the mockup. The left column is dominated by a high-contrast About card: a beveled, slightly glowing panel with a soft dark-to-light gradient and a bright highlight line near the top. At its top-left sits a large, bold 'About' title, with a narrow metadata strip just beneath it, aligned to the right, indicating when the content was last updated. Below this header area, the rich About text flows in neatly spaced sections, punctuated by subheadings and occasional inline imagery, maintaining the dense but structured feel of a marketing site. On the right, a tall sidebar card mirrors the style of the mockup’s 'Latest Products' panel: the top portion contains a stacked set of glossy, pill-like links that allow the user to jump to sections within the About content, and immediately below them is an oversized, saturated green-yellow 'Shop Car Audio' button that spans almost the full width of the sidebar. The background on either side of the content container continues the page’s gradients, so there are no pale, empty gutters—just rich color framing the central module. When content is loading, a semi-opaque overlay or centered loader occupies the left card, and if an error occurs, a bold, high-contrast error panel replaces the About copy, but the overall two-column structure remains visually consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content loader occupies the entire left main column area where the About card lives. It is positioned directly below the hero banner, aligned with the left edge of the central content container and spanning roughly 70% of the container width. Vertically, it extends from just under the space reserved for the About title down to the approximate height of the full About content card. Visually, it appears as a glossy, dark card with 16px padding on all sides and a subtle inner glow, matching the surrounding panels. In the center of this card, both horizontally and vertically, a bright, animated loading indicator (e.g., a circular spinner or pulsing bar) sits, sized at about 64px, with a brief 'Loading About Us…' label directly beneath it. The right sidebar (in-page nav and CTA slots) remains visible in its normal position to the right of the loader, reinforcing the two-column layout even during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loader is a stateful overlay within the left column: it either replaces the content renderer inside the main card area or sits above it with a semi-opaque dark gradient background. It scrolls with the page like regular content, but while active, it blocks interaction with the About text area beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content renderer is the main body of the About page and occupies the entire left column of the central content container, roughly 70% of the total width. It appears as a tall, glossy content card positioned immediately below the hero band, with about 24px of vertical spacing separating it from the hero. The card has 16px padding on all sides, rounded or beveled corners consistent with the marketing style, and a subtle vertical gradient (darker at the top, slightly lighter toward the center). At its top, space is reserved for the About heading and last-updated strip; below that, the renderer flows the rich text content. Headings in the content align to the left and are spaced with 16–24px of vertical margin above them, while paragraphs sit in 8–16px vertical rhythm, ensuring the text feels dense but readable. Any inline images or callouts are placed within this card, typically aligning to the left margin and not extending beyond the card’s edges. The card’s bottom edge ends around the same vertical level as the bottom of the sidebar, maintaining a visual balance between columns when possible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The renderer is static within the left column, scrolling normally with the page. It resizes vertically as content length changes but maintains the same horizontal position and padding. In loading or error states, it is either replaced or covered by the corresponding state component.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The title manager primarily affects the document title in the browser tab and does not render a visible UI element. Visually, the perception of the page title is handled by the topmost heading inside the About content card, which sits at the top-left of the left column. This visible heading is large, bold, and high-contrast—approximately 1.5–2 times the body text size—and is given extra breathing room: about 16px of top padding within the card and 8–12px of space below before the metadata strip. The title manager keeps this visual title consistent with the browser title but does not occupy its own dedicated area on the canvas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; operates at the document level. It has no spatial footprint and does not affect layout flow directly, other than ensuring the visible H1 and browser title remain synchronized.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The last-updated display is a slim metadata strip positioned within the top section of the About content card, just beneath the main 'About' heading and aligned toward the right side of the card. It sits on the same horizontal band as the lower edge of the heading area, separated from the heading by about 4–8px of vertical space, following the tight but readable spacing of the mockup. The text is set in a smaller font size than the body copy and uses a lighter or slightly desaturated color (e.g., light gray or pale yellow-green) to differentiate it from the main content, possibly on a semi-transparent dark pill or faint gradient bar about 24px tall. Horizontally, it occupies the right-most 40–50% of the card’s width, leaving some empty space between it and the heading on the left, which reinforces the Z-shaped visual flow from the heading to the metadata and then down into the content. The entire strip is contained within the card’s 16px internal padding, ensuring it aligns with the text column below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline element at the top of the About content card. It scrolls naturally with the content and does not stick to the viewport. Its height remains fixed, but it can expand slightly if the metadata wraps on smaller widths.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error handler takes over the same spatial footprint as the About content renderer within the left column. When an error occurs, the usual glossy content card is replaced by a bold, high-contrast error panel, aligned with the left edge of the content container and spanning roughly 70% of the width. The panel retains the 16px internal padding and gradient framing but shifts to a warning color scheme—deep red or dark charcoal with bright red accents—and a subtle glow or stroke to make it stand out. At the top-left of the panel, a strong, uppercase error headline (e.g., 'CONTENT UNAVAILABLE') appears, roughly the same size as the normal About heading. Below it, within 8–16px of vertical space, a short explanation and any retry instructions appear as concise lines of text. A compact action button or link (e.g., 'Try Again' or 'Go to Home') may be aligned to the lower right of this card area. During this state, the right sidebar (in-page navigation and CTA) remains visible in the usual 30% column to the right, subtly encouraging navigation to shopping or other pages even when the About content fails to load.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Stateful replacement in the left column. It fully replaces the normal content renderer while active and scrolls with the page. It does not overlay the sidebar and does not use fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section scroll manager occupies the upper portion of the right sidebar column, directly opposite the top third of the About content card. It appears as a vertically stacked set of glossy, pill-shaped or tab-like buttons inside a sidebar card that spans the full 30% column width. This sidebar card starts at the same vertical level as the top of the About card and has 12–16px padding around the items, with each navigation item separated by about 8px vertically. The background of the card mirrors the mockup’s 'Latest Products' panel: a darker gradient base with lighter top highlights and a subtle inner shadow, framed by a thin, bright stroke. Each navigation item is left-aligned, with an icon or small arrow on the left and the section title text to the right, creating a dense, menu-like stack. The card’s overall height is roughly one-third to one-half of the full sidebar height, leaving clear space beneath it for the CTA. The sidebar aligns flush to the right edge of the main content container, with no large gap between it and the container edge, emphasizing density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The in-page navigation behaves as a sticky sidebar panel on taller viewports: once the user scrolls past its initial position, it remains pinned near the top of the viewport within the constraints of the sidebar column, allowing quick jumping between sections of the About content. On shorter viewports or when sticky behavior is unavailable, it scrolls normally but always maintains its position above the CTA within the sidebar column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Shop Car Audio' CTA sits directly below the section scroll manager within the right sidebar column, occupying nearly the full width of the sidebar. It is styled as a large, glossy, high-contrast button—approximately 48px–56px tall—with strong green and yellow gradients, beveled edges, and a subtle outer glow that makes it pop against the darker sidebar background. There is about 16px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the in-page navigation card and the top of this button, keeping the sidebar visually dense but clearly segmented. The button text is centered both horizontally and vertically, set in bold uppercase typography, and spans about 70–80% of the button’s width, leaving comfortable left and right padding. The button aligns with the inner padding of the sidebar card above, so its left edge visually lines up with the navigation items. This placement ensures that once users scan the About content and glance over to the right, the bright CTA is one of the most eye-catching elements below the navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the sidebar column; it scrolls normally with the page. On taller viewports, while the section scroll manager may behave sticky near the top, the CTA remains positioned beneath it in the natural flow and will scroll out of view as the user moves down the page.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page where users can view store contact details, submit a contact form for support, and navigate to Help / FAQ content.

core_features: Load and display dynamic contact details (support email, phone, hours, mailing address, Help / FAQ summary) using getContactInfo.
Load optional CMS-managed informational content for the Contact page using getPageContent with a pageCode derived from the page context or URL.
Provide a contact form with fields for name, email, subject, and message, and submit it using submitContactForm.
Validate contact form input before submission (e.g., required fields, basic email format) and show success or error messages based on submitContactForm response.
Prepopulate contact form fields (such as subject or message) from URL parameters when provided, to streamline user inquiries from other pages.
Offer a Help / FAQ navigation link, with context-aware URL parameters (e.g., source of navigation, subject/topic) to help the Help page tailor its content.
user_workflows:
View contact information:
1. User opens contact.html (via footer navigation or direct URL).
2. On page load, the page initializes by calling getContactInfo.
3. The page also derives a pageCode for CMS content (for example from a URL parameter or route identifier) and calls getPageContent(pageCode).
4. The contact info panel displays the support email, phone number, support hours, and mailing address from getContactInfo.
5. The Help / FAQ summary text from getContactInfo is shown near a Help / FAQ navigation link.
6. The user chooses a contact method (call, email client, mailing address) based on the information provided.

Submit a general contact request:
1. User opens contact.html.
2. The page parses URL parameters such as name, email, subject, and message if present and pre-fills the corresponding form fields.
3. User reviews or enters their name, email, subject, and message.
4. On clicking the form submit action, client-side validation runs (e.g., checks that required fields are present and email has a valid format).
5. If validation passes, the page calls submitContactForm with the current form field values.
6. When submitContactForm returns, the page checks the success flag.
7. If success is true, a success confirmation state is shown (e.g., a confirmation message, and optionally the form is cleared or disabled).
8. If success is false, an error state is shown with the returned message, and the form stays populated so the user can adjust and resubmit.

Use Contact page for specific topics (e.g., product, order, installation):
1. User clicks a "Contact" link from another page with query parameters, for example contact.html?subject=<encoded-subject>&context=<encoded-context>.
2. On load, the Contact page parses the subject and context URL parameters.
3. The subject field is prefilled with the provided subject value.
4. Optionally, the message field is prefilled with a template that incorporates the context parameter (for example, by including the context value in the initial message text), without hardcoded wording in this specification.
5. User completes or adjusts the message and submits the form, which is sent via submitContactForm.
6. The rest of the flow follows the general contact request workflow (validation, submission, feedback).

Navigate to Help / FAQ for self-service:
1. User opens contact.html and views the Help / FAQ summary text from getContactInfo.
2. User clicks the Help / FAQ link component.
3. The Contact page constructs a URL to help.html, optionally appending query parameters such as from=contact and subject=<current-subject-field-value> or context=<parsed-context>.
4. The browser navigates to help.html with those parameters, enabling the Help page to interpret them and display relevant content.
interactions:
Page load initialization:
- Trigger: Browser loads contact.html.
- Behavior: The page parses URL parameters (e.g., name, email, subject, message, context, contentPageCode). It then calls getContactInfo with no parameters and getPageContent with a pageCode derived from either the contentPageCode URL parameter or the page's routing configuration. The response data is stored in page state and bound to the contact info and CMS content components.

Contact info actions:
- Trigger: User clicks on the displayed support email.
- Behavior: Client opens the user's email client using a mail action with the supportEmail from getContactInfo.
- Trigger: User clicks on the displayed phone number.
- Behavior: On supported devices, client initiates a call using the supportPhone from getContactInfo.
- Trigger: User interacts with the mailing address (e.g., clicks it).
- Behavior: Client may open a mapping or directions experience using the mailingAddress from getContactInfo, without hardcoded address values in this specification.

Form field editing:
- Trigger: User types into the name, email, subject, or message fields.
- Behavior: Local page state for the form is updated on each change. Any field-level validation (e.g., marking required fields) updates in real time as the user edits.

Form submission:
- Trigger: User activates the submit action on the contact form.
- Behavior: Page performs client-side validation. If validation fails, the user sees inline error indicators and the submitContactForm interface is not called. If validation passes, the page sets a "submitting" state (e.g., disables the submit button) and calls submitContactForm with the current name, email, subject, and message values.
- Response handling: When submitContactForm returns, the page clears the "submitting" state. If success is true, the page sets a "submitted successfully" state and displays the response message; it may optionally clear or lock the form fields. If success is false, the page sets an "error" state and shows the message returned from submitContactForm, leaving fields intact for correction.

Help / FAQ navigation:
- Trigger: User clicks the Help / FAQ link or button.
- Behavior: The page reads current state (such as any subject or context information from URL or form). It then constructs a URL to help.html, optionally appending query parameters like from=contact, subject=<current-subject>, and context=<current-context>. Browser navigates to that URL.

CMS content refresh:
- Trigger: Optional user interaction such as a language change or manual refresh control, if implemented at a higher level.
- Behavior: The page may re-derive the pageCode and call getPageContent again with the (possibly updated) pageCode to refresh the informational bodyHtml and title.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Contact page state is fully driven by URL parameters and interface responses, not by localStorage. Example URL parameters and their effects:
- name: When present (contact.html?name=<encoded-name>), the initial value of the name field in the contact form is set to this value.
- email: When present, initializes the email field of the contact form.
- subject: When present, initializes the subject field of the contact form and may also be propagated to the Help / FAQ navigation URL.
- message: When present, initializes the message field of the contact form so other pages can pass in a suggested message body.
- context: Generic context parameter (e.g., indicating order, product, installation, or other topics as encoded by the caller). The Contact page does not interpret specific static values in this specification but may display or pass this value along to help.html to allow contextual handling.
- contentPageCode: When present, this parameter determines the pageCode value passed to getPageContent. If absent, the Contact page derives pageCode from its own routing or configuration (for example, from the filename or route identifier) without specifying concrete static values in this specification. The getPageContent response (title and bodyHtml) is used to render additional informational content on the Contact page.
Additionally, transient UI state is derived entirely from interface responses and user actions:
- contactInfoState: Populated from getContactInfo and used to display supportEmail, supportPhone, supportHours, mailingAddress, and helpFaqSummary.
- cmsContentState: Populated from getPageContent(pageCode) and used to display title and bodyHtml.
- formState: Contains local values for name, email, subject, message, plus validation and submission status flags (e.g., isSubmitting, submitSuccess, submitError, submitMessage). This state is initialized from URL parameters when available and updated with user input.
- navigationState: Derived when constructing URLs for help.html, combining static path "help.html" with dynamic query parameters from the current URL and form state, avoiding any static business data values.

components.functionality: Initializes the Contact page by parsing URL parameters, loading contact information and optional CMS content, and seeding the contact form state.
Displays dynamic contact details including support email, phone, support hours, mailing address, and a short Help / FAQ summary, and exposes basic actions like starting a call or email.
Renders CMS-managed informational content for the Contact page, such as explanatory text or instructions, using the title and bodyHtml returned by getPageContent.
Provides input fields for name, email, subject, and message, prefilled from URL parameters when available, validates the input, and submits the data via submitContactForm, then displays the result.
Presents a Help / FAQ link with descriptive text from getContactInfo.helpFaqSummary and builds a contextual navigation URL to help.html using current form and URL state.
Shows submission status for the contact form, including loading state during submitContactForm call, and success or error messages from the interface response.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global header, nav, and hero banner, the Contact page content sits inside a fixed-width, center-aligned container (about 960–1024px). The Contact area starts with a horizontal status / feedback band (if present), then immediately splits into the familiar two-column layout: a wider left column (~70%) for the main contact content (intro text and form) and a narrower right column (~30%) for support-related side content (contact info and help navigation). Each block is treated as a distinct glossy card or band with clear borders and gradient backgrounds, echoing the hero/promo modular structure from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global header, nav, and hero banner, the Contact page content sits inside a fixed-width, center-aligned container (about 960–1024px). The Contact area starts with a horizontal status / feedback band (if present), then immediately splits into the familiar two-column layout: a wider left column (~70%) for the main contact content (intro text and form) and a narrower right column (~30%) for support-related side content (contact info and help navigation). Each block is treated as a distinct glossy card or band with clear borders and gradient backgrounds, echoing the hero/promo modular structure from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header + navigation remain in the top 15–20% of the viewport, with the hero banner occupying the next ~40%, just like on other pages. The Contact page's primary content band begins just below the hero. Within this band, the left main column gets roughly 70% of the container width, stacking the CMS intro above the contact form. The right sidebar takes roughly 30% of the width, stacking the contact info panel above the help navigation block. If shown, the submission feedback banner spans nearly the full inner content width as a shallow horizontal strip above both columns. Vertical spacing between these modules uses 24–32px gaps implied by gradient shifts and card edges rather than large empty whitespace.
layout_strategies.content_density: The Contact page maintains a moderate-to-high content density. The left column is visually full: a bold heading and short intro text above a multi-field form, with minimal unused space inside each card. The right column is vertically packed with contact details and help links, each in its own compact card with 12–16px padding and tight but readable line spacing. Background gradients and framed cards fill most areas; there are no large blank regions. Text blocks are short and broken by headings, icons, and labels, keeping the page image-forward and visually busy in the same spirit as the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows the established flow: logo and nav at the top, then hero. From the bottom of the hero, the eye is caught by the horizontal submission feedback banner (when present), then drawn into the left column where a strong 'Contact Us' style heading and intro text live. From there, attention naturally moves downward into the contact form fields and primary submit button. Peripheral attention then shifts right toward the sidebar, first hitting the contact info panel at the top (store address, phone, map/marker icon), and then moving down to the help navigation links below. The vertical stack of modules and the strong left-column emphasis mirror the mockup’s hero-to-feature-to-sidebar flow.

layout.overall_description: Under the glossy header, navigation band, and large hero banner, the Contact page content begins as a new horizontal band with a subtle gradient that distinguishes it from the hero above. At the very top of this band, a full-width, low-profile status strip appears when relevant to show submission feedback. Directly beneath it, the content is organized into a center-aligned, fixed-width container that splits into two columns: a wide left column for the main interaction (intro text and a contact form) and a narrower right column for reference information (contact details and help navigation). Each piece—intro copy, form, contact info, help links—is enclosed in its own card-like panel with beveled edges, inner glow, and saturated accent colors, separated vertically by 24–32px of space and subtle horizontal rules or gradient transitions. The overall effect is a busy, marketing-style composition where the left column clearly carries the primary task while the right column feels like a stacked, glossy sidebar module, echoing the product/“latest” pattern from the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that temporarily sits above the entire site, including the header, navigation, hero, and all Contact page content. It covers 100% of the width and height of the browser window, aligned flush to all edges. In the center of the viewport, a compact loading panel (roughly 280–320px wide) is centered both horizontally and vertically, following the same glossy, high-contrast aesthetic: dark background, bright green/yellow highlight border, and a small product-style spinner or pulse animation. The rest of the overlay uses a semi-opaque dark gradient, slightly revealing the underlying layout to hint at the page beneath. Once the content is ready, this overlay fades out, revealing the header, hero, and the two-column Contact layout underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed position, full-screen overlay. It sits at the highest z-index while active, blocking interaction with underlying components. It does not scroll; the overlay remains pinned to the viewport until dismissed, then is removed from the flow entirely.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission feedback banner is positioned at the very top of the Contact content band, immediately below the hero banner and spanning the full width of the inner content container (roughly 960–1024px) while remaining centered in the viewport. It is relatively shallow in height—around 56–72px (7–9 × base unit)—and reads like a promotional strip: a vivid gradient background (e.g., green for success, yellow for warning) with a subtle inner glow, a left-aligned status icon and bold message title, and smaller supporting text to the right or just below. There is about 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom edge of this banner and the start of the two-column layout, so the main contact blocks appear as a distinct step below it. When not active, this space collapses and the left/right columns rise to sit closer to the hero, keeping the page compact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in normal document flow at the top of the Contact section, pushing the rest of the page content downward when visible. It scrolls away with the page and does not stick to the top of the viewport. It is hidden entirely when there is no status to show, causing the main content to shift upward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact CMS content occupies the top of the left column within the main content band. The two-column container is centered on the page, with the left column taking about 70% of the 960–1024px width. Within this left column, the CMS content sits as the first card, directly below the submission feedback banner (or directly below the hero if no banner is visible), separated by roughly 24px of vertical space. This card spans the full width of the left column and has 16–20px padding on all sides. At a minimum, it contains a prominent 'Contact Us' style heading near the top left, a short paragraph or two of supporting text, and possibly a small icon or underline accent echoing the brand colors. The card height is moderate—roughly 120–200px depending on content—and it serves as an introduction and context for the form below. A subtle bottom border or gradient edge visually separates this intro card from the contact form card that follows, with a 16–24px gap between them to maintain the stacked module feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column. It scrolls naturally with the page and does not resize dynamically beyond normal responsive behavior (e.g., stacking above the form on narrow screens if needed).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form sits directly beneath the contact CMS content in the left column, maintaining the same 70% column width alignment. It appears as a substantial, primary card that occupies most of the left column area above the fold: approximately 320–480px tall depending on field count, with generous 16–24px padding. This card begins about 16–24px below the bottom of the CMS content card, separated by a clear visual edge (either a drop shadow, a gradient band, or both). Within the card, fields are arranged in a stacked, vertical sequence: name, email, subject, message, etc., with 8–16px vertical spacing between form controls. Labels are aligned left and close to their respective fields, keeping density high but readable. The submit button is placed toward the bottom right of this card, large and high-contrast, echoing the CTA style seen in the mockup's promo areas. The bottom of the form card has about 24px of margin before the next major section (if any), ensuring it reads as the main focal block in the lower half of the above-the-fold area. On typical desktop viewports, a user can see the form’s heading, most key fields, and at least the start of the sidebar content without scrolling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static main-content element occupying the lower portion of the left column. It scrolls with the page, with no fixed or sticky behavior. Width remains tied to the left column; on smaller screens, it can stack so that the form becomes full-width above or below the sidebar blocks while preserving the same card structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact info panel anchors the top of the right sidebar column. The overall content container is centered, with the right column taking about 30% of the 960–1024px width. This panel is aligned flush with the top edge of the CMS content card on the left, so both columns start at roughly the same vertical position, just below the submission feedback banner. The contact info panel card spans the full width of the right column and uses 12–16px padding. It is relatively tall for a sidebar module (approximately 200–260px), accommodating the store name, address, phone, email, and possibly a small map thumbnail or icon cluster arranged vertically. The title (e.g., 'Store Contact') sits at the top left of the card, with details stacked underneath. The panel’s visual weight is slightly less than the form but more than the help navigation below, using bold labels and maybe a small glossy header bar within the card to attract attention. A 16–24px vertical gap separates this panel from the help-navigation-block card below, mirroring the vertical spacing rhythm of the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar column. It scrolls with the page and remains aligned to the column width, without sticky behavior. On very narrow screens, it may stack above or below the form but should still appear before the help navigation block to preserve hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help navigation block is positioned directly below the contact info panel in the right sidebar column, maintaining the same 30% column width. It appears as a shorter, vertically stacked card—roughly 160–220px tall—whose top edge sits about 16–24px beneath the bottom of the contact info panel. Inside, a concise heading (e.g., 'Help & FAQ') sits at the top left, with a list of navigation links (Help Center, FAQ, Warranty Info, etc.) arranged in a single vertical column. Each link row has 8–12px vertical spacing and may include a small right-pointing arrow icon, giving it a menu-like, 'Latest links' feel similar to the 'Latest Products' card in the mockup. The block fills most of the remaining visible sidebar area on an average viewport and forms a visually coherent stack with the contact info panel above it. It is present on all Contact page loads, providing an always-available pathway to support content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked within the right sidebar column, scrolling with the page. It does not float or stick, and its vertical position is defined purely by its placement beneath the contact info panel.

name: Shipping & Returns
title: Shipping & Returns
description: Informational page that loads dynamic shipping options and CMS-managed shipping and returns policies, with URL-driven navigation between sections and a link to view live shipping costs in checkout.

core_features: Load and display CMS-managed shipping and returns policy content using the getPageContent interface.
Dynamically fetch and list all available shipping methods using the getShippingMethods interface, including their names, descriptions, estimated delivery windows, and free-shipping flags.
Indicate which shipping method is the default option and which methods represent free shipping, based on getShippingMethods data.
Allow users to switch between Shipping-focused and Returns-focused views via a tab-style interaction that is synchronized with a URL query parameter.
Allow users to view details for individual shipping methods, driven by a URL query parameter that reflects the selected method.
Provide structured access to key policy subsections (such as processing times, delivery estimates, restrictions, and return time frames) using URL-controlled anchors or section identifiers.

Provide a navigation link to checkout.html that guides users to see live shipping costs during checkout, optionally including URL parameters that represent the current context (such as selected tab or method).
Expose the policy last-updated date from getPageContent so users understand how current the information is.
user_workflows: Workflow: Viewing shipping methods and estimates -> User lands on shipping_returns.html -> Page reads URL parameter 'tab'; if missing or invalid, defaults logically to the shipping view -> Page calls getPageContent with the page-specific pageCode and renders the shipping policy section -> Page calls getShippingMethods and renders a list of shipping methods with their names and estimated delivery ranges -> User optionally clicks an individual shipping method to see more details, which updates the 'method' query parameter and highlights that method.

Workflow: Understanding free shipping eligibility at policy level -> User lands on shipping_returns.html with 'tab' set to the shipping view -> Page content from getPageContent explains eligibility rules for free shipping (based on CMS-managed text) -> getShippingMethods response is used to visually flag which methods represent free shipping via their isFreeShipping attribute -> If support or marketing shares a deep link including 'method' for a free-shipping method, the page selects and scrolls to that method on load.

Workflow: Learning about returns and exchanges -> User lands on shipping_returns.html with URL parameter 'tab=returns' or switches to the Returns tab -> Page shows the returns section from the CMS content (time frames, conditions, return initiation steps, etc.) -> User clicks internal anchors (mapped to a 'section' query parameter) to jump to subsections such as return window, condition requirements, or exchange procedures -> User reads the necessary policy information and may follow further links (e.g., to a separate returns request flow if implemented elsewhere).

Workflow: Going from policy page to see live shipping costs -> User is on shipping_returns.html, reviewing shipping methods and policy text -> User clicks the "See live shipping costs during checkout" link -> Page navigates to checkout.html, optionally appending context query parameters (e.g., 'from=shipping_returns' and the currently selected 'method' code) -> Checkout page then uses its own logic and data (not part of this page) to calculate real-time shipping charges based on the cart and shipping address.

Workflow: Support-shared deep link to a specific topic -> Customer support sends a URL like shipping_returns.html?tab=shipping&section=processing_times (parameter names and values determined by implementation) -> User visits this URL -> On load, page reads 'tab' and 'section' parameters, selects the correct tab, then scrolls to or expands the associated subsection in the shipping or returns content -> User sees the specific policy information referenced by support without needing to scroll manually.
interactions: User clicks on the 'Shipping' or 'Returns' tab -> Tab click handler updates the 'tab' query parameter via the History API, triggers conditional rendering of the respective content section, and optionally scrolls to the top of that section.

User clicks on a listed shipping method row or title -> Click handler updates the 'method' query parameter to the clicked method's code, highlights that method as selected, and expands or scrolls to its detailed view.

User clicks an in-page anchor or subsection link (for example, processing times, delivery estimates, restrictions, return window, or exchange procedure) -> Click handler updates the 'section' query parameter and scrolls to the corresponding anchor inside the CMS content.

On initial page load or when the URL changes (e.g., via back/forward browser navigation) -> A centralized URL-state handler parses 'tab', 'method', and 'section' parameters and updates the tab selection, selected shipping method, and focused subsection accordingly without reloading data unnecessarily.

User clicks the link or button that leads to checkout.html to see live shipping costs -> Click handler builds a navigation URL to checkout.html, optionally including contextual parameters derived from the current page state (such as the active 'tab' and selected 'method'), and triggers a standard navigation.

On data-loading events -> When getPageContent resolves, the page content component updates the main policy content and last updated indicator; when getShippingMethods resolves, the shipping list component renders or updates the shipping options; on error, the page can display a generic error state or a fallback message managed by CMS (no hardcoded text in the front-end).
page_functionality.state_logic: This page uses URL query parameters to control its functional state, with no reliance on localStorage. The base URL is shipping_returns.html, and the following query parameters are read and written: (1) 'tab' controls whether the Shipping or Returns view is active. Valid values correspond to internal constants for each tab (for example, a value representing the shipping tab and a value representing the returns tab). On load, the page checks 'tab'; if it is missing or not recognized, the logic defaults to the shipping view. Whenever the user switches tabs, the tab-switching component updates the 'tab' parameter using the History API, without reloading the page. (2) 'method' represents the currently selected shipping method. Its value must match one of the ShippingMethod.code values returned by getShippingMethods. On load, after getShippingMethods resolves, the page compares the 'method' parameter to the available codes. If a match is found, that method is marked as selected and may be scrolled into view or expanded. If there is no match or 'method' is absent, the logic falls back to an appropriate default, such as the method with isDefault set to true or, if none are flagged as default, the first method in the list. When the user selects a different shipping method, the list component updates the 'method' parameter accordingly and replaces the previous value in the URL. (3) 'section' represents a focused subsection within the shipping or returns policy (for example, a particular anchor or fragment ID that the CMS content defines). On load, after getPageContent resolves, the content component maps the 'section' value to the corresponding anchor within bodyHtml and scrolls to it if available. When the user clicks an internal anchor navigation link, the handler updates 'section' and performs the scroll. If 'section' is missing or invalid, no special scrolling occurs and the view is left at the top of the active tab. All query parameter changes use pushState/replaceState to avoid full reloads, but the page also listens for popstate events so that browser back/forward navigation restores the appropriate tab, selected method, and subsection.

components.functionality: Fetches the CMS-managed content for the Shipping & Returns page via getPageContent and exposes the title, main bodyHtml, and lastUpdated metadata to downstream components. It may parse or segment bodyHtml into logical subsections (such as shipping content and returns content, plus internal anchors) according to conventions defined in the CMS.

Controls which high-level policy content is visible (Shipping vs Returns) based on an internal tab state that is synchronized with the 'tab' query parameter. It receives the structured or segmented content from the page-content loader, and renders the appropriate subsection for the active tab.

Calls getShippingMethods on page initialization to retrieve all available shipping methods and normalizes them into a consumable collection. It exposes the list, loading state, and error state to the shipping-methods-list component.

Displays the collection of shipping methods returned by shipping-methods-loader. For each ShippingMethod, it renders the name, description, base cost (if appropriate for this page), estimatedDaysMin and estimatedDaysMax, and visual indicators for default (isDefault) and free-shipping (isFreeShipping) statuses. It also handles selection of a method, which controls a detail view and synchronizes with the 'method' query parameter.

Shows detailed information for the currently selected shipping method. It listens to selection changes from shipping-methods-list and uses the corresponding ShippingMethod object to display its properties (such as detailed description and the delivery window). It reacts to the 'method' query parameter and updates when that parameter changes, ensuring back/forward navigation re-selects the correct method.

Provides an in-page navigation control (for example, a list of topics) that maps to named anchors or identifiers embedded in the CMS bodyHtml. When the user clicks a topic, it updates the 'section' query parameter and scrolls to the matching subsection. It also responds to changes in the 'section' parameter (including browser back/forward) by scrolling to the appropriate location in the active tab's content.

Renders the returns and exchanges portion of the CMS-managed policy text. It is controlled by the policy-tab-switcher and only visible when the Returns tab is active. Within this section, anchors or markers defined in bodyHtml can be targeted by the 'section' query parameter for deep-linking to specific parts of the returns policy, such as return windows, conditions, and how to initiate a return.

Renders the shipping-related portion of the CMS-managed policy text. It is visible when the Shipping tab is active and may contain anchors corresponding to processing times, delivery estimates, geographic restrictions, or free-shipping explanations. It cooperates with policy-section-anchor-navigation to scroll to specific anchors based on the 'section' query parameter.

Displays the lastUpdated value returned by getPageContent so users can see when the Shipping & Returns policy was last modified. The component formats and renders this metadata without hardcoding any dates.

Provides an actionable link that navigates the user from shipping_returns.html to checkout.html to view live shipping costs based on their cart and shipping address. When clicked, it builds a navigation URL that may include the current 'tab' and selected 'method' as query parameters so that checkout can, if desired, pre-select or highlight the referenced shipping method.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page sits inside a fixed-width, center-aligned container (≈980–1024px) over a full-width dark gradient background. Above, the site-wide glossy header, nav, and hero remain consistent with the marketing-heavy home page. Within the main content, the layout switches to a classic 2-column structure: a dominant left column (≈70% width) for the shipping/returns policies and shipping methods, and a right sidebar (≈30% width) for quick navigation (anchors) and the live-shipping-costs CTA. Content is grouped into card-like modules with strong borders, gradients, and image/graphic accents. The page-level content loader wraps the entire content band, while specific loaders sit inside their associated modules (shipping methods).

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page sits inside a fixed-width, center-aligned container (≈980–1024px) over a full-width dark gradient background. Above, the site-wide glossy header, nav, and hero remain consistent with the marketing-heavy home page. Within the main content, the layout switches to a classic 2-column structure: a dominant left column (≈70% width) for the shipping/returns policies and shipping methods, and a right sidebar (≈30% width) for quick navigation (anchors) and the live-shipping-costs CTA. Content is grouped into card-like modules with strong borders, gradients, and image/graphic accents. The page-level content loader wraps the entire content band, while specific loaders sit inside their associated modules (shipping methods).
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the hero, the Shipping & Returns content band occupies roughly the lower 40–45% of the above-the-fold area on a typical laptop viewport, extending vertically as needed. Within this band, the left main column takes about 70% of the container width and carries the page title (outside scope), last-updated indicator, tab switcher, and the main policy and shipping methods content. The right column takes about 30% and contains a tall sidebar card with anchor navigation at the top and the live shipping cost link treatment below it. Vertical spacing between logical modules (tab switcher, policy sections, lists, CTAs) follows the 24–32px rule, while internal padding in each card follows the 12–16px guideline. Background gradients span full width behind the centered container, avoiding white gutters and instead using color to separate bands.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is moderately dense: nearly every visible surface has some treatment (gradient fills, glossy cards, outlined sections). Text content is broken into short paragraphs and headings; emphasis is on strong section titles, highlighted labels, and structured lists rather than long-form reading. The policies are chunked into subsections that can be deep-linked via the anchor navigation. The shipping methods list is compact but legible, with each row having 8–12px padding and tight vertical stacking. Cards and bands ensure there are no large blank voids; instead, density is controlled via padding and a clear grid, maintaining readability amid rich visuals.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the existing site header and hero down into a bold 'Shipping & Returns' band. Within this band, the eye hits the left-aligned page title area and the right-aligned last-updated indicator, then drops immediately to the horizontal policy tab switcher. Once a tab is selected, the eye flows down through the policy content (shipping or returns), then into the shipping methods list and its detail panel when applicable. On the right, the glossy sidebar draws attention secondarily: first to the anchor navigation (which mirrors key headings in the left column), then to the prominent 'See live shipping costs in checkout' CTA block below. The repeated use of banded cards and gradients creates clear vertical steps, guiding the user downward while the sidebar anchor navigation provides a vertical, sticky reference point as they scroll.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s glossy header, navigation, and hero, a distinct horizontal content band spans the full width of the browser window, filled with a dark-to-light gradient that frames a centered, fixed-width content container. Inside this container, the Shipping & Returns area splits into a larger left column and a narrower right sidebar, both sitting on slightly lighter, beveled card surfaces to separate them from the background gradient. At the top of the left column, the page title and last updated text form a tight header strip, immediately followed by a bold, glossy tab switcher that lets users toggle between shipping and returns views. Beneath this, the active policy text is laid out in a structured, sectioned card that flows down into a visually distinct shipping methods module with a list and detail area. On the right, a tall sidebar card runs parallel, containing a vertical anchor menu with sharp, pill-like links to the main sections and, just below, a bright button-like link to see live shipping costs in checkout. A subtle full-width loading veil can overlay the entire content area when CMS data is loading, and a smaller loader can temporarily replace the shipping methods list. The result mirrors the original mockup’s energetic, marketing-heavy style while reshaping it into a structured, information-first page that still feels glossy, high-contrast, and visually guided from top-left to bottom-right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This loader appears as a semi-transparent, glossy overlay that covers the entire main Shipping & Returns content band inside the centered container. It stretches from just below the page-level band title (top of the left column’s header strip) across both the left main column and the right sidebar, spanning 100% of the container width and the visible height of the policy and methods content. Vertically, it starts about 24px below the bottom edge of the hero band and extends down to just above the footer band, following the content height. The overlay uses a subtle diagonal gradient and animated shimmer bars aligned horizontally, matching the page’s high-contrast styling. While active, it slightly mutes the underlying cards (tab switcher, policy sections, sidebar) but preserves their outlines so users can still perceive the layout beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-width, fixed-position overlay within the content container, positioned above all other components in stacking order but constrained to the main content band (not covering header or footer). It scrolls with the page, always covering the corresponding content region until loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy tab switcher sits at the very top of the left main column, directly below the page title and last-updated indicator. It spans nearly the full width of the left column (≈90–95% of that column’s width), centered with 8–12px inset padding from the column edges. Its height is roughly 48–56px, presenting 2–3 large, pill-shaped tabs (e.g., 'Shipping Policy' and 'Returns Policy') laid out horizontally left-to-right.
