Each tab occupies about one third of the available width, with the active tab rendered as a bright, glossy pill (saturated green or yellow) that slightly protrudes forward with a subtle drop shadow and top highlight, echoing the hero’s glossy controls. A thin beveled bar or underline spans the full width beneath the tabs, anchoring them visually to the column. There is 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the tab switcher and the top of the active policy content section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, anchored at the top of the left column. Optionally can become sticky within the left column when the user scrolls past its original position, remaining pinned just below the viewport’s top until the user scrolls past the policy band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping methods loader is a localized loading state that sits exactly in the space reserved for the shipping methods list and detail view within the left column. It is a card-sized rectangle centered horizontally within the left column, matching the width of the underlying methods module (≈90% of the column width), and having a height of roughly 180–240px depending on viewport. It appears directly below the main shipping policy text block, with 24px vertical spacing separating it from the text. The loader surface uses a slightly darker gradient than the surrounding card, with 2–3 horizontal shimmering bars mimicking where list rows and the detail panel would appear. It sits inside the same beveled border as the final module, ensuring the layout’s vertical rhythm is preserved even while loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the same block space as the shipping-methods-list and shipping-method-detail-view. It replaces these components while shipping data is loading and disappears once data is available.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping methods list occupies a dedicated module in the lower part of the left column, shown when the Shipping Policy tab is active. This module is a vertically oriented card stretching about 90% of the left column’s width, centered horizontally, with 16px internal padding on all sides. It sits 24–32px below the last paragraph or subheading of the shipping policy section. Within this card, the list is aligned to the left and uses 100% of the card’s inner width. Each shipping method is displayed as a compact row, about 56–64px tall, with a semi-gloss background, 8–12px vertical padding, and a subtle top-to-bottom gradient. Rows are stacked tightly with 4–8px vertical gaps, separated by thin, highlighted dividers. The method name and key attributes (e.g., 'Standard 3–5 days') are left-aligned, while a small arrow or plus icon is right-aligned, indicating selection. When a method is selected, its row gains a stronger glow or brighter gradient to signal focus, and the detail view card appears directly beneath the list within the same module.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll, expanding vertically as rows are added. It shares its vertical space with the detail view: when a row is selected, the detail panel appears directly below the list while the list maintains its height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping method detail view appears as an expanded, inset card immediately below the shipping methods list within the same left-column module. It inherits the same width as the list (≈90% of left column width) and is visually nested with a slightly lighter gradient and an inner bevel to differentiate it. There is a 12–16px gap between the bottom of the list and the top edge of this detail card. Inside, the content is structured in 2–3 short blocks: a bold method name at the top, followed by concise bullet points or labeled rows (e.g., delivery time, coverage, restrictions), all left-aligned. The detail panel’s height is moderate (≈160–220px), ensuring it feels substantial but not overwhelming compared to the policy text above. It aligns vertically with the right sidebar elements, creating a balanced lower page rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and collapsible within the main flow: it only appears when a list item is active. When no method is selected, its space collapses, and the methods list sits closer to the content below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The anchor navigation is the primary element in the right sidebar column. It is contained in a tall, glossy sidebar card that begins at the same vertical height as the tab switcher on the left. The card spans nearly the full width of the sidebar (≈95% of sidebar width), with 16px internal padding. Its top sits about 8–12px below the bottom edge of the page title band so that the left and right columns visually align. Inside the card, a bold 'Jump to' or 'Policy Sections' heading appears at the top, followed by a vertical list of anchor links (e.g., 'Shipping Policy', 'Shipping Methods', 'Returns Policy'). Each anchor item is rendered as a small pill-like button, stretching nearly full width within the card, with 8–10px vertical padding and 4–8px spacing between pills. The active section’s anchor uses a brighter gradient and a subtle right-side glow, echoing the active tab style and reinforcing the vertical flow. The total height of this anchor navigation block is roughly the upper half of the sidebar, leaving room below for the live shipping costs link card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the sidebar: once the user scrolls past its initial position, the anchor navigation card remains pinned near the top of the viewport within the right column, ensuring persistent access to section jumps while the left column scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The returns policy section shares the same physical footprint as the shipping policy section in the left column, and is shown when the 'Returns' tab is active. It occupies about 90% of the left column width, centered horizontally, and starts 16–24px below the policy tab switcher. The content sits on a light, glossy gradient card with 16px padding, framed by a beveled border to separate it from the background band. Headings within the returns policy are left-aligned, bold, and slightly larger than body text, with 16–24px of vertical spacing above each heading. Paragraphs are broken into short blocks with 8–12px spacing and occasional sub-bullets. The section’s vertical size depends on content length, but it typically fills much of the viewport height, aligning visually with the mid and lower portions of the right sidebar. Anchor targets are placed just above key internal headings so that clicking the anchor navigation snaps these headings to just below the viewport’s top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the main content flow, toggled by the policy-tab-switcher. When returns is active, it replaces or hides the shipping-policy-section and shipping-methods modules, but maintains the same vertical spacing relative to the tab switcher.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping policy section is the main textual block under the shipping tab in the left column. It mirrors the returns policy’s dimensions: about 90% of left column width, centered, with 16px padded content inside a glossy card. It begins 16–24px below the tab switcher. Within this card, the content is structured into clearly labeled subsections (e.g., 'Processing Time', 'Domestic Shipping', 'International Shipping') with strong, uppercase or semi-uppercase headings and short paragraphs. Thin, glowing horizontal rules or subtle gradient shifts separate major subsections, reinforcing the vertical rhythm. The bottom of this section ends 16–24px above the shipping methods module, creating a distinct step down to the more data-driven list. The anchor navigation on the right is aligned so that clicking 'Shipping Policy' scrolls this section’s top heading to the upper viewing area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow, occupying the left column beneath the tab switcher whenever the Shipping tab is active. It scrolls normally with the page, with its top and key headings serving as anchor destinations.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The last-updated indicator is a small but clearly visible text element within the topmost strip of the left column, sharing space with the page title (which sits to its left). It is right-aligned within the left column’s width, placed on the same horizontal line as the page title or just 4px below it to create a subtle two-line hierarchy. The indicator text (e.g., 'Last updated: Jan 15, 2026') is rendered in a smaller font size with a slightly lighter color but sits on a semi-transparent, pill-shaped background that echoes the site’s glossy style. This pill has about 6–8px horizontal padding and 4px vertical padding, with a soft glow around its edges. There is 8–12px vertical spacing between this title/metadata strip and the policy tab switcher below, tying the entire top of the left column into a cohesive header band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static metadata element, anchored to the top of the left column and scrolling with the main content. It does not stick or float separately.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'See live shipping costs in checkout' link is presented in the right sidebar as a prominent, button-like card placed directly beneath the anchor navigation card. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of the anchor navigation card and the top of this CTA card. The card spans about 95% of the sidebar width, centered, with 16px padding and a bright, saturated gradient (e.g., neon green to yellow) that contrasts strongly with the darker background. The link text is centered both vertically and horizontally, set in bold, uppercase or semi-uppercase, with a small right-pointing arrow icon to suggest forward navigation. The card’s height is roughly 56–72px—large enough to feel like a key action but not as tall as the anchor navigation block above. It visually aligns horizontally with the lower edge of the shipping methods module in the left column, reinforcing the connection between reading about shipping and taking action to see exact costs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar column, scrolling normally with the page. It remains visible below the sticky anchor navigation as the user scrolls through the main content, but does not itself stick to the viewport.

name: Privacy & Terms
title: Privacy & Terms
description: Displays the combined privacy policy and terms of use content loaded from the CMS and supports navigating, linking, and printing specific sections.

core_features: Load and display privacy & terms content dynamically using the getPageContent interface
Show the official page title and last updated date from the CMS data
Render rich text HTML for privacy and terms sections (including headings, lists, and links) from bodyHtml
Provide an in-page table of contents for quick navigation to key sections (e.g., privacy, cookies, terms, warranties, shipping/checkout references)
Support deep-linking to specific sections via URL parameters and anchors (e.g., ?section=privacy or hash-based anchors)
Allow users to print or save the current policy view (optionally triggered automatically via a URL parameter)
Handle loading and error states when fetching the content from getPageContent
user_workflows:
Workflow: Accessing Privacy & Terms from footer
1. User clicks the "Privacy & Terms" link in the footer.
2. Browser navigates to privacy_terms.html (optionally with URL parameters such as ?section=privacy).
3. The page controller component derives the appropriate pageCode for this page from the routing configuration.
4. The controller calls getPageContent(pageCode).
5. While waiting, a loading state is shown in the main content area.
6. On success, the title, lastUpdated, and bodyHtml are passed to the content and TOC components.
7. The content component renders the HTML; the TOC component parses headings and renders navigable links.
8. If a section parameter or hash is present, the page scrolls to the corresponding section.
9. User reads the policy and terms, optionally navigating via the table of contents.

Workflow: Navigating between Privacy and Terms sections
1. User arrives on privacy_terms.html and sees the table of contents listing major sections detected in bodyHtml.
2. User clicks a TOC item (e.g., labeled by a heading within the content, such as a privacy-related or terms-related heading).
3. The TOC component prevents default navigation, computes the section anchor, updates the URL (e.g., ?section=<section-id> and/or #<section-id>) using the History API, and requests the page to scroll to that element.
4. The section sync component scrolls smoothly to the target section and visually marks the selected TOC item as active.
5. If the user copies the URL from the browser, the ?section or hash is preserved, enabling deep-linking to that part of the policy.

Workflow: Direct linking to a specific section
1. User receives or bookmarks a link to privacy_terms.html with a section indicator (e.g., privacy_terms.html?section=<section-id> or privacy_terms.html#<section-id>).
2. The browser loads the page with the given URL parameters or hash.
3. The controller calls getPageContent(pageCode) and passes the loaded content to child components.
4. Once bodyHtml is rendered, the section sync component reads the current URL parameters/hash.
5. The component finds the matching section element (based on its id or data attribute derived from headings) and scrolls to it.
6. The TOC highlights the corresponding entry as active.
7. The user can then read or further navigate to other sections via the TOC.

Workflow: Printing or saving the policy
1. User wants a copy of the privacy and terms for records.
2. User clicks the "Print" button on the page.
3. The print button component triggers the browser's print dialog for the current page content.
4. User prints or saves to PDF using browser capabilities.
5. Alternatively, a stakeholder can share a link like privacy_terms.html?print=1 so that when a user opens it, after content is fully loaded and rendered, the page auto-triggers the print dialog once (handled by the print button component via an initialization event).

Workflow: Handling content load errors
1. The page controller calls getPageContent(pageCode) when the page initializes.
2. If the request fails or returns an error, the controller sets an error state.
3. The main content component displays a generic, non-hardcoded error message region (e.g., sourced from a generic error handling mechanism or UI config, not from hardcoded literals in this spec) and hides the TOC.
4. Optionally, a retry action (button) can be exposed; when clicked, it triggers another call to getPageContent(pageCode).
interactions:
Click interaction: Footer "Privacy & Terms" link
- Action: Navigate to privacy_terms.html (optionally with URL parameters such as ?section= or ?print=1).
- Result: Triggers page initialization and getPageContent(pageCode) call.

Page initialization interaction
- Trigger: DOMContentLoaded or equivalent page load event on privacy_terms.html.
- Action: The controller component reads URL parameters (e.g., section, print), determines the correct pageCode for this page from routing configuration, and calls getPageContent(pageCode).
- Result: Updates the content and TOC components when data is returned; manages loading and error states.

In-page navigation via table of contents
- Trigger: User clicks an item in the TOC (which corresponds to a heading in bodyHtml).
- Action: Prevent default anchor behavior, update URL parameters (e.g., set ?section=<section-id> and optional hash), then scroll to the target section element.
- Result: Visible jump/scroll to the desired section, TOC item is marked active.

Deep-link navigation via URL changes
- Trigger: User changes the URL hash manually, or navigates back/forward causing URL parameter section/hash to change.
- Action: The section sync component listens to popstate/hashchange and re-reads the current section identifier.
- Result: The page scrolls to the corresponding section and updates the TOC active state.

Print interaction
- Trigger A: User clicks a print button in the page header or above the main content.
- Trigger B: Page detects a URL parameter (e.g., print=1) after content load and automatically starts the print flow once.
- Action: The print button component calls the browser's print function.
- Result: Browser shows print dialog for the current page, allowing user to print or save as PDF.

Retry loading content interaction
- Trigger: User clicks a retry button if loading the page content failed.
- Action: The controller re-invokes getPageContent(pageCode).
- Result: On success, content and TOC render; on failure, error remains visible.
page_functionality.state_logic:
This page does not use localStorage or other client-side storage; all state is managed through URL parameters, the current route, and in-memory component state.

Route-based state:
- The base URL privacy_terms.html identifies this specific informational page. Routing configuration associates this URL with a specific pageCode used when calling getPageContent.
- The exact value of pageCode is determined by the application routing or configuration and is not hardcoded by this specification. The controller simply uses the configured pageCode for this route when calling getPageContent(pageCode).

URL parameters and hash:
- section: Optional query parameter (e.g., ?section=<section-id>) indicating which logical section of the policy (derived from headings in bodyHtml) should be scrolled into view on load.
  - On initial load, after bodyHtml is rendered, the section sync component checks for section.
  - If a matching element id or data attribute exists, the page scrolls to that element and the TOC marks it as active.
  - When a user clicks a TOC item, the component updates section in the URL via History API without reloading the page.
- Hash (#<anchor>): Optional hash that may also reference a specific anchor within bodyHtml.
  - On load and on hashchange, if an element with that id exists, the section sync component scrolls to it.
  - The component also attempts to map the hash to a TOC item to highlight the appropriate entry.
- print: Optional query parameter (e.g., ?print=1) indicating that the page should automatically open the print dialog once the content has successfully loaded.
  - The controller or print component checks for print after the getPageContent call completes and content is rendered.
  - If present and valid, it triggers the print dialog and then clears or ignores subsequent automatic print triggers (so printing happens only once per navigation event).

Component state:
- Loading state: While getPageContent(pageCode) is in progress, the main content region displays a loading indicator and the TOC is disabled or hidden.
- Error state: If getPageContent fails or returns invalid data, the page stores an error flag and optional error details in memory, which results in showing an error message region instead of the main content.
- Content state: Once getPageContent returns successfully, title, bodyHtml, and lastUpdated are stored in component state and bound to content and metadata components.
- TOC state: After bodyHtml renders, the TOC component reads the DOM or parsed HTML, collects headings, and stores them as a list of section items (id, label, and level). It also maintains the currently active section, synchronized with URL parameters and scroll position.

All navigation and state changes that must persist across reloads are encoded into URL parameters or hash; there is no reliance on localStorage, cookies, or other client storage mechanisms for this page.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, calls getPageContent with the correct pageCode for this route, manages loading and error states, and distributes title, bodyHtml, and lastUpdated to child components. It also interprets URL parameters (section, print) and passes derived state to the section sync and print components.

Renders the page title, last updated metadata, and the full HTML content for privacy & terms returned by getPageContent. Provides identifiable section anchors for headings to support TOC navigation and deep-linking.

Builds a table of contents by scanning the rendered bodyHtml for headings, generates a list of sections with anchors, and allows users to click to navigate to those sections. Keeps its active item synchronized with the current section (determined from URL parameters and scroll position).
data_binding: Derived heading structure from rendered bodyHtml

components.functionality: Synchronizes the currently visible/selected section with URL parameters and the hash. On initialization and on URL changes, it reads the section query parameter or hash and scrolls to the corresponding section anchor in the content. It also updates the URL when TOC items are clicked, without reloading the page.
data_binding: Section anchors in the rendered content

components.functionality: Provides a user-triggered print action and optionally auto-triggers printing when the print URL parameter is present and the content has finished loading.
data_binding: Content load status from page controller

components.functionality: Displays the loading state while getPageContent is in progress and shows an error message and retry control if loading fails.

Displays non-editable metadata such as the lastUpdated value from getPageContent and any additional CMS-provided metadata that may be included in bodyHtml (e.g., version information). This component does not hardcode any metadata values; it simply reflects what is provided.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: After the global glossy header and navigation bands, the page introduces a legal-specific hero/heading zone followed by a full-width metadata bar, then drops into the familiar two-column structure from the mockup. The left column is dedicated to the main policy content viewer, while the right column stacks the policy table of contents, print action, and any related status indicators as a single sidebar module. Non-visual logic (URL sync, page controller) anchors these visible components but does not interrupt the visual bands.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: After the global glossy header and navigation bands, the page introduces a legal-specific hero/heading zone followed by a full-width metadata bar, then drops into the familiar two-column structure from the mockup. The left column is dedicated to the main policy content viewer, while the right column stacks the policy table of contents, print action, and any related status indicators as a single sidebar module. Non-visual logic (URL sync, page controller) anchors these visible components but does not interrupt the visual bands.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header + nav retain roughly the top 15–20% of the initial viewport. Immediately below, a compact legal hero/metadata band occupies ~15% of the viewport height as a full-width strip within the centered 960–1024px container. Beneath that, the main body is split into a 70/30 two-column layout: the policy-main-content occupies about 70% of the container width on the left, while the sidebar (policy-toc, print button, and any sidebar status) takes the remaining 30% on the right. Vertical spacing between bands is tight but clear (about 24–32px), with each band or card framed by gradients or borders rather than large blank gaps.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays moderate-to-high to match the original marketing-heavy mockup: every major area (metadata bar, main content, sidebar) appears as a visually distinct module with gradient fills, borders, and strong headings. The policy text itself is broken into short sections with clear headings, and the right-hand TOC offers quick jumps instead of long uninterrupted paragraphs. Padding within cards uses 16–24px on all sides to preserve readability while keeping the layout visually busy with minimal empty space.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the top-left logo through the nav, then into the legal page’s hero/metadata bar, where a bold 'Privacy & Terms' title and high-contrast bar act as the focal anchor before the long-form content begins. From there, attention drops into the left column’s first heading, while the bright, beveled TOC card on the right is visible in peripheral vision. As users scroll, a sticky sidebar TOC/print cluster remains in view to the right, while the left column’s section headings drive vertical scanning. URL-based section syncing reinforces this flow by updating the address as each section heading crosses a subtle viewport threshold.

layout.overall_description: Within the site’s glossy, full-width header and navigation, the Privacy & Terms page sits in a center-aligned, fixed-width container that mirrors the original product layout. A high-contrast legal header band directly beneath the hero area announces 'Privacy & Terms' on a saturated gradient strip, with metadata like last updated date and version inline on the same band. Below this, the content area splits into a two-column layout: the left side is a tall, softly beveled card that holds the full policy text, with bold section headings and short paragraphs cascading downward; the right side is a narrower sidebar card composed of a stacked TOC, a prominent gradient print button, and any supplemental status messaging. The background outside the container is filled with brand-colored gradients, so the page feels visually rich and continuous. As users scroll, the left column provides the main reading path while the right sidebar remains largely in view as a sticky navigation/action strip, echoing the original mockup’s structured yet energetic flow, just adapted for dense textual content instead of a hero product shot.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller acts as the structural canvas for the Privacy & Terms content inside the existing site shell. Visually, it manifests as a centered fixed-width content container (approximately 960–1024px wide) that begins just below the global hero/marketing banner and navigation band. Within this container, it first reserves a shallow horizontal band at the top for the policy-metadata-bar that spans the full container width. Directly beneath that, it establishes a two-column grid: a left column occupying roughly 70% of the content width and a right column occupying roughly 30%, both aligned to share the same top baseline under the metadata bar. The left column houses the policy-main-content card, while the right column stacks the policy-toc at the top, then the policy-print-button and any sidebar-related status elements. Vertical spacing between these internal blocks follows the 8px-based system (e.g., 16–24px gaps), and the entire structure scrolls as a unified page, sitting on top of a full-width gradient background that extends to the edges of the browser window.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a static, scrolling two-column grid within the fixed-width central container; does not itself appear as a distinct visual element but governs the positions and widths of all child components under the hero/metadata band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-main-content occupies the dominant left column beneath the metadata bar. It appears as a tall, rounded-rectangle card with a subtle vertical gradient (e.g., dark charcoal at the top blending to a slightly lighter tone) and a faint inner glow or soft border, filling about 70% of the central container’s width (around 680–720px on a 1024px grid). The top edge of this card aligns horizontally with the top of the right-hand sidebar’s TOC card, about 16–24px below the metadata bar. Inside, the top-left begins with a strong, uppercase heading such as 'Privacy Policy & Terms of Use,' followed immediately to its right or just below it by the policy-load-state-indicator. The content viewer scrolls vertically and is segmented into clearly labeled sections; each section starts with a bold subheading (16–20px) followed by short paragraphs and list items, all padded 16–24px from the card edges. Section anchors are placed with a bit of offset so that when a user navigates via the TOC, the section heading lands comfortably below the sticky navigation/header area rather than flush to the top of the viewport. The card extends downward as far as needed, creating a tall scrollable reading area that serves as the main axis of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling content within the left column; it grows vertically with the amount of legal text and scrolls underneath any sticky header or sidebar behavior, while maintaining its fixed 70% column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-toc is positioned in the right column at the top of the sidebar, aligned so that its top edge lines up with the top of the policy-main-content card, just beneath the metadata bar. It occupies nearly the full width of the right column (minus 12–16px padding to the container edges), appearing as a glossy sidebar card with a slightly darker, high-contrast gradient background and a prominent, beveled header strip labeled 'Contents' or 'Jump to Section.' Inside, section titles are stacked vertically in a dense but readable list, each line separated by about 8–12px with small bullet or chevron icons. The card maintains around 16px of internal padding on all sides, and its height adjusts to the number of sections, but its width remains locked to roughly 30% of the main container. There is a clear vertical gap of about 16–24px below this TOC card before the print button area starts, so the sidebar feels like a series of distinct modules.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky sidebar module within the right column: once the user scrolls past its initial position, the TOC card (and the attached print button/status below if grouped) remains pinned near the top-right of the viewport with approximately 16–24px offset from the global navigation, until the page’s footer or the bottom of the main content is reached.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-url-sync component has no visible UI; it conceptually overlays the main content column, tracking the vertical position of each policy section relative to the viewport. It is associated with the section anchors inside the policy-main-content and with the link items in the policy-toc but does not consume any physical space or alter the visual arrangement. Its 'position' can be thought of as an invisible layer bound to the scrollable region of the left column, activating as each section heading aligns within a predefined viewport band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial in the layout: it does not occupy width or height, and it scrolls implicitly with the main content, updating URL fragments and TOC highlight states based on scroll position without changing the page’s geometry.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-print-button sits in the right sidebar directly beneath the policy-toc card, separated from it by a vertical gap of about 16–24px. It appears as a wide, high-gloss, bevelled button spanning approximately 80–100% of the sidebar’s inner width (e.g., 8–12px inset from the right column edges), centered horizontally. The button height is substantial (about 40–48px), with a vibrant green or yellow gradient fill, a subtle inner glow, and crisp, uppercase label text like 'PRINT THIS POLICY' centered within. There is about 12–16px padding around the label inside the button. Under the button, a small explanatory line (if included) shares the same card or sits in a lightly separated area, with an 8px gap. Below the button zone, a vertical margin of 16–24px sets it apart from any additional sidebar indicators or content, keeping the sidebar modules visually stacked.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Grouped with the sidebar module: when the TOC becomes sticky, the print button remains visually attached just beneath it as part of the same sticky block, maintaining its horizontal alignment in the right column while staying visible as the user scrolls through long content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-load-state-indicator is a compact element positioned near the top of the policy-main-content card so users immediately see the loading or current state of the legal document. It is aligned to the top-right corner of the main content area, either on the same baseline as the main 'Privacy & Terms' heading within the card or just beneath that heading with a small 4–8px vertical offset. Visually, it can appear as a small pill-shaped badge or a subtle inline strip (e.g., 'Loading…', 'Updated just now', or an animated spinner with text) no wider than 120–160px and about 24–28px tall. It floats inside the card’s padding, with at least 8–12px clearance from the card’s outer border, so it feels integrated but slightly detached from the text body. Once loading is complete, it transitions to a low-key state but remains in the same top-right position, offering status without dominating the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the top area of the main content card; it scrolls naturally with the policy-main-content and does not exhibit sticky or fixed behavior, remaining attached to the section where the main policy begins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-metadata-bar forms a full-width horizontal band at the top of the Privacy & Terms content area, positioned directly below the global hero/navigation region and above the two-column layout. It spans the entire width of the central container (960–1024px) while the surrounding page background extends outward with a darker or contrasting gradient. The bar is relatively shallow—about 56–72px high—with a glossy gradient fill (e.g., rich green or metallic charcoal) and a faint bevel, visually echoing the hero and promo strips from the original mockup. On the left side of this bar, taking up roughly the first 60–70% of its width, a bold page title like 'Privacy & Terms' is left-aligned, in large, uppercase typography. To the right, within the remaining 30–40% of the bar, is neatly stacked metadata such as 'Last updated: [date]' and 'Document version: [x.x],' arranged either in a single line or in two compact lines. Padding of about 16–24px is applied top and bottom, with the content vertically centered in the band. The bottom edge of the bar provides a clear visual break—often with a subtle drop shadow or dividing line—where the two-column content region begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static, scrolling band between the global header/hero and the main two-column content; it does not stick to the top on scroll but moves out of view as the user reads down the page, serving as the initial anchor for page context and metadata.

name: Help / FAQ
title: Help / FAQ
description: Provides structured help content and frequently asked questions about products, compatibility, installation, and how to use site features, with direct navigation to related flows and contact options.

core_features: Load page-level help content from a CMS or static content source via getPageContent and render the title, descriptive text, and any general help information.
Fetch FAQ entries via getFaqEntries and display them grouped by their category field, with questions that expand to show rich-text answers.
Use the optional faqCategory URL parameter to determine which FAQ category should be active (expanded) when the page loads.
Allow users to switch between FAQ categories using on-page controls, updating the faqCategory URL parameter so the state is shareable and supports browser navigation.
Render answerHtml for each FAQ entry, allowing embedded links that navigate to vehicle_selector.html, checkout.html, or other pages when clicked.
Provide a clear navigation option to contact.html for users who still need assistance after reading the FAQs.
user_workflows: User views general help and FAQs: (1) The browser loads help.html, optionally with a faqCategory query parameter. (2) The page shell parses faqCategory and stores a pending active category value. (3) The help-static-content component calls getPageContent with a pageCode derived from the help.html route and renders the returned title, bodyHtml, and lastUpdated metadata. (4) The faq-loader component calls getFaqEntries and passes the resulting entries to other components. (5) The faq-category-filter component derives the list of distinct categories from the FAQ entries and selects the category that matches faqCategory when present; if no match exists or faqCategory is missing, it selects a default category based on the loaded data. (6) The faq-accordion-list component renders the questions for the active category and allows the user to expand or collapse each answer.

User changes FAQ category using the page controls: (1) The user clicks a category control in the faq-category-filter component. (2) The component updates the activeCategory state in the page and triggers the faq-deep-link-handler to write the selected category value into the faqCategory URL parameter without reloading the page. (3) The faq-accordion-list component re-renders with the filtered list of questions for the new activeCategory, with all answers initially collapsed. (4) If the user uses the browser back or forward buttons, faq-deep-link-handler listens for URL changes, updates activeCategory accordingly, and the FAQ list updates to match the URL.

User expands questions and follows help links: (1) In the faq-accordion-list component, the user clicks a question header; the component toggles that question's expanded state while leaving other questions unchanged or collapsing them, depending on the chosen behavior. (2) The component renders the associated answerHtml when expanded. (3) If the answerHtml contains links to other pages such as vehicle_selector.html or checkout.html, clicking those links navigates the browser to those pages, optionally including any URL parameters defined in the link. (4) The user can return to help.html, and because the faqCategory parameter is preserved in the URL, the same category remains active when the page reloads.

User cannot resolve their issue and contacts support: (1) After browsing FAQs, the user activates a dedicated control in the support-navigation component (for example, a button or link indicating that more help is needed). (2) The component performs a client-side navigation to contact.html, optionally preserving the current faqCategory in the URL if the application routing requires it. (3) The user then completes their interaction on the contact page to get additional assistance.
interactions: On initial page load, the page shell reads the faqCategory query parameter from the URL, initializes the internal activeCategory state with that value when it matches a category from getFaqEntries, and triggers getPageContent and getFaqEntries to populate the page.

When a user clicks a category control in the FAQ category list, the faq-category-filter component updates the activeCategory state and uses the History API (or a full URL navigation if needed) to set the faqCategory parameter in the address bar while keeping the FAQ data already loaded in memory.

When a user clicks a question in the FAQ list, the faq-accordion-list component toggles that question's expanded or collapsed state and conditionally renders the corresponding answerHtml, including support for HTML links and basic interaction such as clicking those links to navigate to other pages.

When a user activates the contact support control, the support-navigation component navigates to contact.html, ensuring the interaction does not require any additional API calls and relies solely on standard navigation behavior.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the faqCategory URL parameter and the data returned from getPageContent and getFaqEntries. On load, the page parses faqCategory from the query string. After getFaqEntries resolves, the page computes the set of available categories from the category field of each FAQ entry. If faqCategory matches one of these category values, activeCategory is set to that value; otherwise, activeCategory is set to a default derived from the available categories (for example, the first one in the list as returned by the API). This logic allows direct deep linking to a specific FAQ category. When the user changes categories from within the UI, activeCategory is updated first, and then faqCategory in the URL is updated to the same value using URL manipulation (such as pushState or a full redirect), so the URL always reflects the current category selection. Browser back and forward actions are handled by re-reading the faqCategory parameter and updating activeCategory; no persistent client storage such as localStorage or cookies is used. All FAQ question expansion states are kept in in-memory page state, derived from user interactions and the loaded FAQ entries; they are reset on a full page reload. The bodyHtml and answerHtml values are rendered directly based on the responses from getPageContent and getFaqEntries, without hardcoded questions, answers, or categories in the page code.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page setup, parses the faqCategory URL parameter, triggers data loading via subordinate components, and maintains shared state such as activeCategory and currently expanded FAQ questions.

Requests generic help content for the Help / FAQ page via getPageContent using a pageCode value derived from the current route, and renders the returned title, bodyHtml, and lastUpdated fields above or alongside the FAQ section.
Fetches the full list of FAQ entries for the site by calling getFaqEntries and exposes the resulting collection to other components for grouping, filtering, and rendering.

Derives the set of distinct categories from the loaded FAQ entries, renders a selectable list of categories, determines the initially selected category using the faqCategory URL parameter and available data, and updates activeCategory and the URL when the user selects a different category.

Displays FAQ questions and answers for the currently activeCategory. It filters the FaqEntryCollection by the category field, shows a list of questions, and toggles visibility of answerHtml blocks when questions are clicked. It also passes through any link interactions contained within the answerHtml so that standard navigation to other pages occurs when those links are clicked.

Keeps the activeCategory state and the faqCategory URL parameter synchronized. On load and on URL changes (including browser back/forward), it updates activeCategory based on faqCategory if the value matches an available category. When activeCategory changes from within the UI, it updates the faqCategory parameter in the URL.

Provides a dedicated control that users can activate when FAQs do not resolve their issue, performing a navigation to contact.html. This component may also render any contact-related links provided within the HelpPageContent bodyHtml.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width center-aligned grid, likely based on a 960–1024px content width with 2 main columns (wide content area on left, narrower sidebar on right). Column widths appear roughly 70% main content / 30% sidebar, aligned within a central container.
layout.layout_pattern: Reuse the mockup’s banded structure: a full-width glossy header and navigation at the top, followed by a bold, marketing-style hero band specifically themed for Help / FAQ (still using product/road imagery). Below the hero, the page drops into a centered fixed-width content container that splits into two columns: a dominant left column for help intro text, FAQ category filter, and accordion list, and a narrower right sidebar for support navigation and quick actions. Functional, invisible components (loader, deep-link handler) are layered within this structure, primarily over or inside the left column FAQ area.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Reuse the mockup’s banded structure: a full-width glossy header and navigation at the top, followed by a bold, marketing-style hero band specifically themed for Help / FAQ (still using product/road imagery). Below the hero, the page drops into a centered fixed-width content container that splits into two columns: a dominant left column for help intro text, FAQ category filter, and accordion list, and a narrower right sidebar for support navigation and quick actions. Functional, invisible components (loader, deep-link handler) are layered within this structure, primarily over or inside the left column FAQ area.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain the same overall proportions: header + nav ≈ top 15–20% of the viewport, a visually dominant help-themed hero occupying roughly 35–40% of the above-the-fold area, and the remaining 40–50% split into a 70% width main column and 30% width sidebar within a 960–1024px centered container. Horizontal bands (header, nav, hero, content background, footer) span the full viewport width with gradients, while the actual content sits centered with minimal side gutters. The FAQ main area (filter + accordion) consumes most of the left column height, while the sidebar support card stack runs alongside it and can extend slightly longer on taller pages.
layout_strategies.content_density: Preserve the moderate-to-high density: little true white space, but enough padding and clear card boundaries to keep content readable. Each distinct logical group—hero, help intro, category filter, FAQ list, support navigation—is treated as a visually separated module with glossy backgrounds, gradients, and borders. Text blocks remain relatively short and are paired with strong headings, icons, and buttons, so the FAQ area feels active and structured rather than minimal. Vertical spacing between modules is around 24–32px, with 12–16px padding inside cards.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in a similar Z-pattern: logo and global nav first, then across to any support link in the navigation, down into the Help / FAQ hero headline on the left, and then across the hero imagery on the right. From there, the eye steps downward to a narrow help-intro strip and then into the left-column category filter. The user’s attention is then pulled down through the stacked FAQ accordion items. In parallel, a strong, colorful support-navigation card in the right sidebar is kept visible (sticky or near-top anchored) so the eye occasionally jumps right for secondary actions (contact, guides, downloads) before returning left to continue reading FAQs. Loader and deep-link behaviors appear in the center of the FAQ content area, reinforcing that this is the primary interaction zone.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar glossy header and horizontal navigation band, a bold Help / FAQ hero stretches full width, with a dark-to-bright green gradient and a car-audio product on the right driving into a stylized highway background. On the left of this hero, large uppercase copy like “HELP CENTER” and a short tagline sit in glossy, semi-transparent bands, echoing the marketing feel of the home page. Beneath the hero, the content snaps into a centered, fixed-width container around 980px wide, sitting on a slightly darker gradient strip that frames the main area. This container is divided into a 70/30 two-column layout: the left column starts with a compact help-intro card and then immediately presents a wide, tab-like FAQ category filter, followed by a dense stack of accordion-style FAQ questions, each in its own beveled row with expanding answer panels. The right column is a tall, glossy “Support & Contact” sidebar card with strong headings, icon-labeled buttons, and quick links, visually echoing the ‘Latest Products’ card from the mockup but repurposed for support tasks. When FAQs are loading, a semi-transparent overlay with a spinner appears centered over the left column, dimming the accordion list beneath. Deep links scroll and open the appropriate FAQ within the accordion, causing the corresponding item to glow slightly or stand out. Gradients, subtle glows, and narrow vertical spacing keep the page busy and energetic, while the fixed-width grid and clear module boundaries maintain order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-page-shell spans the full browser width and height, forming the structural frame for all other components. At the very top, it reserves a 80–96px tall band for the existing glossy site header with logo and social icons, and immediately below it, a 48–64px horizontal navigation band that stretches edge-to-edge with a dark green-to-black gradient. Below the navigation, the shell places a large Help / FAQ hero section occupying roughly 35–40% of the above-the-fold height (about 260–340px on a typical laptop screen). This hero is center-aligned within a 960–1024px content container, but its gradient background and imagery bleed full-width. Inside the hero, the left 55–60% of the container is dedicated to text (big ‘HELP CENTER’ heading, a short subtitle, and possibly a CTA button), while the right 40–45% displays product/road imagery consistent with the site’s aesthetic. Directly under the hero, the shell introduces the main content band: a full-width, slightly lighter gradient strip that contains a fixed-width centered container (about 980px). Within this container, the shell splits the space into a left main column (≈70% width, ~680–700px) and a right sidebar column (≈30% width, ~280–300px), with a 24px gutter between them. All page-level content components (help-static-content, faq-category-filter, faq-accordion-list) are stacked vertically in the left column, while the support-navigation component occupies the right sidebar. The shell also provides the layering context for faq-loader and faq-deep-link-handler, allowing them to overlay or control the main content area without shifting global layout. At the bottom, the shell reserves space for the global footer band, matching the site’s full-width gradient style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, center-aligned fixed-width content within full-width bands; responsible for column layout and stacking order. It anchors header, hero, main two-column content, and footer in a vertically scrolling page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-static-content appears as the first card inside the left main column, directly beneath the hero and aligned to the left edge of the 70% column. It spans the full width of that column (excluding a consistent 16px inner padding on all sides) and is about 120–180px tall depending on text length. Visually, it resembles a glossy information strip: a dark, semi-transparent panel with a subtle inner glow, beveled edges, and a top-to-bottom gradient slightly lighter than the background, making it read as a distinct module. At the top-left of this card, a strong heading like “How can we help?” is positioned, taking up the first row; beneath it, a short 2–3 line explanatory paragraph runs nearly full width. Optionally, a row of 2–3 pill-like quick links (e.g., ‘Installation’, ‘Compatibility’, ‘Account Help’) is placed in a horizontal line at the bottom, aligned left, each with about 8–12px horizontal spacing between them. Vertical margins of 24–32px separate this card from the hero above and the faq-category-filter below, maintaining the stepwise vertical flow seen in the mockup. The card’s left edge aligns flush with the column’s left edge, and its right edge aligns flush with the column’s right edge, forming a clean block that visually anchors the start of the FAQ section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that scrolls with the page, acting as a top anchor for the left column FAQ content. No sticky behavior; it simply maintains consistent margins above and below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-loader occupies the same horizontal footprint as the FAQ interaction area—essentially overlaying the combined space of the faq-category-filter and faq-accordion-list inside the left main column—whenever FAQ data is loading or refreshing. Visually, it manifests as a semi-transparent dark overlay panel that sits above the FAQ card region, starting about 16px below the bottom edge of the help-static-content card and extending down to cover the entire visible height of the accordion list area. The overlay respects the 70% column width, aligning its left and right edges with the column boundaries and leaving the right sidebar (support-navigation) visible and interactive. Centered within this overlay (both horizontally within the left column and vertically within the current FAQ area viewport), a glossy circular spinner or progress indicator is displayed, possibly accompanied by a short label like “Loading FAQs…” in uppercase, using the same bright green accent to match the site’s call-to-action style. The overlay darkens the underlying FAQ text and controls while still letting their shapes be faintly visible, reinforcing continuity with the content beneath. Margins above and below the overlay match the FAQ area so that when the loader hides, the FAQ interaction area feels stationary, without visual jumps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as an absolute overlay within the stacking context of the left main column; it sits on top of faq-category-filter and faq-accordion-list when active but does not affect their layout. It scrolls with the main column content, always covering the same region of the FAQs currently in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-category-filter sits immediately below the help-static-content within the left main column, separated by a 24px vertical gap. It spans nearly the full width of the left column (minus 16px internal padding), occupying a horizontal strip about 56–72px tall. Visually, it mimics the site’s navigation tabs: a glossy, dark gradient background with bright green or yellow segmented buttons for each FAQ category (e.g., ‘All’, ‘Products’, ‘Installation’, ‘Ordering’). These category buttons are arranged in a single horizontal row, aligned left, each about 80–120px wide depending on label length, with 8–12px gaps between them. The active category has a stronger gradient, a subtle inner glow, and possibly a small beveled top edge, recalling the selected state of the site’s main navigation. The entire filter strip is treated as a card with slightly rounded corners and a thin, lighter outer stroke to stand off from the background. The lower edge of this card sits only 16–24px above the top of the faq-accordion-list, visually tying the filter directly to the questions below and maintaining the high-density look. The filter’s left and right edges align exactly with the FAQ accordion card beneath, forming a unified visual block over which the loader can appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that scrolls with the left column content. It anchors the top of the interactive FAQ block; when deep links or filters change, the page may scroll just enough to keep this strip near the top of the viewport so users always see the active category context.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-accordion-list is the largest functional block in the left main column, positioned directly beneath the faq-category-filter with a narrow 16–24px vertical gap. It occupies the full width of the left column and extends downward as tall as needed to accommodate all questions. The entire list is contained within a tall, card-like panel with a dark, multi-stop gradient background and a subtle inner shadow, visually connected to the category filter above. Inside this panel, individual FAQ items are stacked vertically with minimal spacing (8–12px) between them to maintain a dense, information-rich feel. Each FAQ item row is about 48–64px tall when collapsed, spanning the full card width; it features a beveled, slightly raised surface and a left-aligned question title in bold, high-contrast text. A small plus/minus icon or chevron sits on the right edge of each row, indicating expand/collapse, with a soft green glow when hovered or focused. When an item is expanded, its row grows downward to reveal the answer text in a slightly lighter panel directly beneath the question, with 12–16px padding around the answer content. The expanded answer area does not break the card; instead, it pushes subsequent questions down, preserving the single vertical stack. The top of the accordion card is anchored approximately one full screen height below the top of the hero on initial load, ensuring that at least the first several FAQs are visible above the fold on most screens. Side padding of 16px keeps text away from edges, and the list’s background gradient transitions subtly near the bottom into the page’s main background, allowing the footer band to feel like the next clear step down.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding content that scrolls within the left column. Items expand inline, pushing others down; the container height grows with content. When navigated via deep links, the list scrolls so the targeted question aligns near the top of the visible area just beneath the category filter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-deep-link-handler has no direct visible presence but influences the layout behavior of the faq-accordion-list. It conceptually occupies the same horizontal region as the left main column FAQ area, sitting logically just above the accordion list in the stacking order. When the page loads with a deep link (e.g., a URL fragment pointing to a specific FAQ), or when a user interacts with an FAQ that updates the URL, this handler programmatically expands the corresponding accordion item and scrolls the left column so that the targeted question appears approximately 48–72px below the top of the viewport—just under the fixed header and navigation height, and visually anchored right beneath the faq-category-filter. As a result, the deep-linked FAQ appears as the first fully visible item in the list, with its answer already open. The handler ensures the scroll offset respects the height of the hero if still visible, so the question is not hidden under any fixed bands. This creates a smooth layout experience where deep-linked answers feel naturally integrated with the page’s vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that manipulates scroll position and accordion open states. It affects how the left column content is positioned within the viewport but occupies no visual space of its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-navigation component occupies the entire right sidebar column, starting near the top of the main content area, aligned vertically with the top edge of the help-static-content in the left column. It is rendered as a tall, vertically stacked glossy card, about 280–300px wide and variable in height (typically 320–480px), depending on the number of actions. The card background uses a rich dark gradient with a faint diagonal texture, and its edges are clearly defined with a beveled border and soft outer glow to echo the ‘Latest Products’ card from the mockup. At the top of the card, a bold heading such as “Support & Contact” is left-aligned, followed by 2–3 lines of brief descriptive text. Below this, a vertical stack of primary support actions is laid out: large, full-width buttons (e.g., ‘Contact Us’, ‘Find a Dealer’, ‘Download Manuals’) with bright green or yellow gradients, 12–16px vertical spacing between them, and small icons on the left of each label. Under the primary buttons, secondary links (e.g., ‘Warranty Info’, ‘Return Policy’, ‘Track Order’) appear as smaller, text-only entries grouped in pairs or a simple list, separated by thin horizontal rules. Internal padding of 16–20px keeps content comfortably away from the card edges. The card’s top edge is roughly aligned with the faq-category-filter and the top of the accordion area, ensuring visual balance between the two columns. On taller pages, the bottom of the support-navigation card may not reach the footer, leaving a small gradient-backed gap that preserves the banded vertical structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-column element that may use sticky behavior: on larger screens, the card can become sticky within its column, pinning its top edge about 16–24px below the navigation band as the user scrolls, so support actions remain visible alongside the scrolling FAQs. On smaller screens or if sticky is not supported, it scrolls normally with the page while maintaining its fixed width and alignment to the right column.
